WHERE  condition
GROUP BY column1, column2
HAVING aggregate_condition
ORDER BY column1 [ASC | DESC] [NULLS FIRST | NULLS LAST]
FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY;

Example

-- Basic SELECT
SELECT employee_id, first_name, last_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  department_id = 10
ORDER  BY salary DESC;

-- With expressions and aliases
SELECT employee_id,
       first_name || ' ' || last_name  AS full_name,
       salary * 12                     AS annual_salary,
       ROUND(salary * 1.1, 2)          AS new_salary
FROM   employees;

-- DISTINCT
SELECT DISTINCT department_id, job_id
FROM   employees
ORDER  BY department_id;

-- DUAL table (Oracle-specific, virtual 1-row table)
SELECT SYSDATE, USER, SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','SESSION_USER')
FROM   DUAL;

1.3 GROUP BY and HAVING

-- GROUP BY with aggregate
SELECT   department_id,
         COUNT(*)              AS headcount,
         AVG(salary)           AS avg_salary,
         MAX(salary)           AS max_salary,
         SUM(salary)           AS total_salary
FROM     employees
WHERE    hire_date >= DATE '2010-01-01'    -- Filter rows BEFORE grouping
GROUP BY department_id
HAVING   COUNT(*) > 5                      -- Filter groups AFTER grouping
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

-- GROUP BY ROLLUP — subtotals
SELECT   department_id, job_id, SUM(salary)
FROM     employees
GROUP BY ROLLUP(department_id, job_id);
-- Produces: per dept+job, per dept, grand total

-- GROUP BY CUBE — all combinations
SELECT   department_id, job_id, SUM(salary)
FROM     employees
GROUP BY CUBE(department_id, job_id);

-- GROUPING SETS — specify exact groupings
SELECT   department_id, job_id, SUM(salary)
FROM     employees
GROUP BY GROUPING SETS (
    (department_id, job_id),
    (department_id),
    ()
);

-- GROUPING() function — detect subtotal rows
SELECT   department_id,
         job_id,
         SUM(salary),
         GROUPING(department_id) AS is_dept_rollup,
         GROUPING(job_id)        AS is_job_rollup
FROM     employees
GROUP BY ROLLUP(department_id, job_id);

1.4 Pagination

-- ANSI SQL (Oracle 12c+): FETCH FIRST
SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER  BY salary DESC
OFFSET 10 ROWS FETCH NEXT 10 ROWS ONLY;    -- Page 2, 10 per page

-- FETCH FIRST without offset
SELECT * FROM employees
FETCH FIRST 5 ROWS ONLY;

-- WITH TIES — include rows tied at boundary
SELECT * FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS WITH TIES;

-- Oracle legacy (ROWNUM) — be careful with ORDER BY!
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT e.*, ROWNUM rn
    FROM   (SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC) e
    WHERE  ROWNUM <= 20                          -- Must limit in inner query
)
WHERE rn > 10;                                   -- Then filter lower bound

-- ROW_NUMBER() approach (most flexible)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT e.*,
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) rn
    FROM   employees
)
WHERE rn BETWEEN 11 AND 20;

Performance Notes

  • FETCH FIRST with ORDER BY on an indexed column can use index scan efficiently
  • ROWNUM in wrong position (WHERE before ORDER BY inner query) is a classic mistake
  • For large offsets, keyset pagination outperforms OFFSET

Interview Questions

  • Why does WHERE ROWNUM > 1 return no rows? (ROWNUM is assigned before ORDER BY; row 1 is always first filtered)
  • What is the difference between FETCH FIRST n ROWS ONLY and FETCH FIRST n ROWS WITH TIES?

2. Operators & Conditional Logic

2.1 Comparison & Logical Operators

-- Comparison
= <> != < > <= >=

-- Logical
AND  OR  NOT

-- Special operators
salary BETWEEN 3000 AND 6000           -- Inclusive both ends
last_name LIKE 'S%'                    -- % = any chars, _ = one char
last_name LIKE 'S\_mith' ESCAPE '\'   -- Escape special char
department_id IN (10, 20, 30)
department_id NOT IN (10, 20)
commission_pct IS NULL
commission_pct IS NOT NULL

-- ANY / ALL
salary > ANY (SELECT salary FROM employees WHERE dept=20)  -- > at least one
salary > ALL (SELECT salary FROM employees WHERE dept=20)  -- > every value

2.2 CASE Expression

-- Simple CASE (equality check)
SELECT last_name,
       CASE department_id
           WHEN 10 THEN 'Accounting'
           WHEN 20 THEN 'Marketing'
           WHEN 90 THEN 'Executive'
           ELSE 'Other'
       END AS dept_name
FROM   employees;

-- Searched CASE (arbitrary conditions)
SELECT last_name,
       salary,
       CASE
           WHEN salary < 3000                    THEN 'Low'
           WHEN salary BETWEEN 3000 AND 8000     THEN 'Medium'
           WHEN salary > 8000                    THEN 'High'
           ELSE 'Unknown'
       END AS salary_grade
FROM   employees;

-- CASE in WHERE clause
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE  CASE WHEN status = 'ACTIVE' THEN amount ELSE 0 END > 1000;

-- CASE in ORDER BY
SELECT last_name, department_id, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY
    CASE WHEN department_id = 90 THEN 0 ELSE 1 END,
    salary DESC;

2.3 NULL Handling Functions

-- NVL(expr, replacement_if_null)
SELECT last_name, NVL(commission_pct, 0) AS commission
FROM   employees;

-- NVL2(expr, value_if_not_null, value_if_null)
SELECT last_name,
       NVL2(commission_pct,
            salary + salary * commission_pct,
            salary) AS total_comp
FROM   employees;

-- COALESCE(e1, e2, e3, ...) — ANSI standard, returns first non-NULL
SELECT COALESCE(phone_number, email, 'No contact') AS contact
FROM   employees;

-- NULLIF(e1, e2) — returns NULL if e1 = e2, else returns e1
SELECT NULLIF(commission_pct, 0)    -- NULL if 0, else commission_pct
FROM   employees;

-- DECODE (Oracle-specific, legacy — prefer CASE)
SELECT DECODE(department_id, 10, 'Acct', 20, 'Mkt', 'Other') AS dept
FROM   employees;

-- NULL arithmetic rules
-- NULL + anything = NULL
-- NULL = NULL → UNKNOWN (use IS NULL, not = NULL)
-- COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(col) excludes NULLs
-- SUM/AVG ignore NULLs

Common Mistakes

  • WHERE col = NULL never matches (use IS NULL)
  • NOT IN with NULLs in subquery returns no rows — use NOT EXISTS instead
  • NULLs sort LAST in ASC, FIRST in DESC by default in Oracle; use NULLS FIRST/LAST

3. Joins

3.1 Join Types Reference

-- INNER JOIN — only matching rows
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       INNER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- LEFT OUTER JOIN — all left rows, NULLs for unmatched right
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- RIGHT OUTER JOIN — all right rows, NULLs for unmatched left
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       RIGHT JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN — all rows from both, NULLs where no match
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       FULL OUTER JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- CROSS JOIN — Cartesian product
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e CROSS JOIN departments d;
-- Result: every employee paired with every department

-- SELF JOIN — join a table to itself
SELECT e.last_name AS employee, m.last_name AS manager
FROM   employees e
       JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.employee_id;

-- NATURAL JOIN (ANSI) — joins on all common column names (use with caution)
SELECT * FROM employees NATURAL JOIN departments;

-- USING clause — join on named column (cleaner than ON for same-named cols)
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       JOIN departments d USING (department_id);

3.2 Old Oracle Join Syntax (Legacy — avoid in new code)

-- Old syntax — still common in legacy systems
SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e, departments d
WHERE  e.department_id = d.department_id;   -- INNER JOIN

SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e, departments d
WHERE  e.department_id = d.department_id(+); -- LEFT JOIN (d side is optional)

3.3 Anti Joins & Semi Joins

-- Anti Join — rows in A with NO match in B
-- Method 1: NOT EXISTS (most efficient with proper index)
SELECT e.employee_id, e.last_name
FROM   employees e
WHERE  NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM job_history jh
    WHERE  jh.employee_id = e.employee_id
);

-- Method 2: LEFT JOIN / IS NULL
SELECT e.employee_id, e.last_name
FROM   employees e
       LEFT JOIN job_history jh ON e.employee_id = jh.employee_id
WHERE  jh.employee_id IS NULL;

-- Method 3: NOT IN (avoid if subquery can return NULLs!)
SELECT employee_id FROM employees
WHERE  employee_id NOT IN (SELECT employee_id FROM job_history);

-- Semi Join — rows in A where match EXISTS in B (no B columns returned)
SELECT e.employee_id, e.last_name
FROM   employees e
WHERE  EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM job_history jh
    WHERE  jh.employee_id = e.employee_id
);

Performance Notes

  • NOT EXISTS generally outperforms NOT IN and MINUS on large tables
  • EXISTS stops scanning as soon as first match found (short-circuit)
  • Oracle optimizer often transforms IN (subquery) to a semi-join internally

Interview Questions

  • Why is NOT IN dangerous with NULLs? (If subquery returns any NULL, NOT IN returns no rows because x != NULL is UNKNOWN)
  • What is the difference between a semi-join and an inner join? (Semi-join returns rows from left table only; no duplicates even if multiple matches)

4. Subqueries

4.1 Types of Subqueries

-- Single-row subquery (returns one row, one column)
SELECT last_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

-- Multi-row subquery (use IN, ANY, ALL)
SELECT last_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  department_id IN (
    SELECT department_id FROM departments
    WHERE  location_id = 1700
);

-- Correlated subquery — references outer query
SELECT e.last_name, e.salary, e.department_id
FROM   employees e
WHERE  e.salary > (
    SELECT AVG(e2.salary)
    FROM   employees e2
    WHERE  e2.department_id = e.department_id   -- Correlated reference
);

-- Scalar subquery — returns single value, used in SELECT list
SELECT e.last_name,
       e.salary,
       (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees e2
        WHERE e2.department_id = e.department_id) AS dept_avg
FROM   employees e;

-- Inline view (derived table)
SELECT dept_stats.department_id, dept_stats.avg_sal, e.last_name
FROM   employees e
       JOIN (
           SELECT department_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
           FROM   employees
           GROUP BY department_id
       ) dept_stats ON e.department_id = dept_stats.department_id
WHERE  e.salary > dept_stats.avg_sal;

4.2 WITH Clause (CTE — Common Table Expressions)

-- Basic CTE
WITH dept_avg AS (
    SELECT department_id,
           AVG(salary) AS avg_sal,
           MAX(salary) AS max_sal,
           COUNT(*)    AS headcount
    FROM   employees
    GROUP BY department_id
)
SELECT e.last_name,
       e.salary,
       da.avg_sal,
       ROUND((e.salary - da.avg_sal) / da.avg_sal * 100, 1) AS pct_above_avg
FROM   employees e
       JOIN dept_avg da ON e.department_id = da.department_id
WHERE  e.salary > da.avg_sal
ORDER BY pct_above_avg DESC;

-- Multiple CTEs
WITH
    high_earners AS (
        SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary, department_id
        FROM   employees
        WHERE  salary > 10000
    ),
    dept_info AS (
        SELECT department_id, department_name, manager_id
        FROM   departments
        WHERE  location_id = 1700
    )
SELECT h.last_name, h.salary, d.department_name
FROM   high_earners h
       JOIN dept_info d ON h.department_id = d.department_id
ORDER BY h.salary DESC;

-- Recursive CTE (Oracle 11gR2+) — hierarchical data
WITH emp_hierarchy (employee_id, last_name, manager_id, lvl, path) AS (
    -- Anchor: top of hierarchy
    SELECT employee_id, last_name, manager_id, 1,
           CAST(last_name AS VARCHAR2(1000))
    FROM   employees
    WHERE  manager_id IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    -- Recursive: children
    SELECT e.employee_id, e.last_name, e.manager_id, eh.lvl + 1,
           eh.path || ' > ' || e.last_name
    FROM   employees e
           JOIN emp_hierarchy eh ON e.manager_id = eh.employee_id
)
SEARCH DEPTH FIRST BY last_name SET order_seq   -- Oracle 19c+
CYCLE employee_id SET is_cycle TO 'Y' DEFAULT 'N'
SELECT LPAD(' ', (lvl-1)*4) || last_name AS org_chart, lvl, path
FROM   emp_hierarchy
ORDER BY order_seq;

Performance Notes

  • CTEs are not always materialized in Oracle — optimizer may inline them
  • Use /*+ MATERIALIZE */ hint to force CTE materialization (acts as temp table)
  • Use /*+ INLINE */ to force CTE to be inlined (evaluated each reference)
  • Recursive CTEs replace CONNECT BY and work in ANSI SQL

5. Set Operations

-- UNION — combine results, remove duplicates (sorts internally)
SELECT employee_id, last_name, 'Current' AS status FROM employees
UNION
SELECT employee_id, last_name, 'Historical' FROM ex_employees;

-- UNION ALL — combine results, keep duplicates (faster, no sort)
SELECT department_id FROM employees
UNION ALL
SELECT department_id FROM departments;

-- INTERSECT — only rows appearing in both
SELECT employee_id FROM employees
INTERSECT
SELECT employee_id FROM job_history;    -- Employees who changed jobs

-- MINUS (Oracle) / EXCEPT (ANSI) — rows in first but not second
SELECT employee_id FROM employees
MINUS
SELECT employee_id FROM job_history;    -- Employees who never changed jobs

-- Rules:
-- 1. Same number of columns in each SELECT
-- 2. Matching data types (or compatible/convertible)
-- 3. Column names from FIRST query used in result
-- 4. ORDER BY applies to final result only (use column number or first-query alias)

SELECT employee_id, last_name FROM employees
UNION ALL
SELECT employee_id, last_name FROM ex_employees
ORDER BY last_name;    -- OK — last_name from first query

-- UNION vs UNION ALL performance
-- UNION: extra sort+dedup step (avoid if duplicates impossible or acceptable)
-- UNION ALL: always prefer when duplicates don't matter

6. DDL Statements

6.1 CREATE TABLE

-- Basic table creation
CREATE TABLE employees (
    employee_id    NUMBER(6)     CONSTRAINT emp_emp_id_pk PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name     VARCHAR2(20),
    last_name      VARCHAR2(25)  CONSTRAINT emp_last_name_nn NOT NULL,
    email          VARCHAR2(25)  CONSTRAINT emp_email_nn  NOT NULL
                                 CONSTRAINT emp_email_uk  UNIQUE,
    phone_number   VARCHAR2(20),
    hire_date      DATE          DEFAULT SYSDATE NOT NULL,
    job_id         VARCHAR2(10)  CONSTRAINT emp_job_nn NOT NULL,
    salary         NUMBER(8,2)   CONSTRAINT emp_salary_min CHECK (salary > 0),
    commission_pct NUMBER(2,2),
    manager_id     NUMBER(6),
    department_id  NUMBER(4),
    CONSTRAINT emp_dept_fk FOREIGN KEY (department_id)
        REFERENCES departments (department_id)
        ON DELETE SET NULL,
    CONSTRAINT emp_manager_fk FOREIGN KEY (manager_id)
        REFERENCES employees (employee_id)
);

-- CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS)
CREATE TABLE emp_backup AS
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE 1=1;   -- Copies data and structure

CREATE TABLE emp_structure AS
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE 1=2;   -- Copies structure only

-- Temporary table
CREATE GLOBAL TEMPORARY TABLE tmp_session_data (
    id     NUMBER,
    data   VARCHAR2(100)
)
ON COMMIT DELETE ROWS;               -- DELETE ROWS or PRESERVE ROWS

6.2 ALTER TABLE

-- Add column
ALTER TABLE employees ADD (nickname VARCHAR2(30));

-- Modify column
ALTER TABLE employees MODIFY (last_name VARCHAR2(50) NOT NULL);

-- Drop column
ALTER TABLE employees DROP COLUMN nickname;
ALTER TABLE employees SET UNUSED COLUMN nickname;  -- Mark unused (faster, no undo)
ALTER TABLE employees DROP UNUSED COLUMNS;          -- Remove unused columns

-- Rename column (Oracle 9i+)
ALTER TABLE employees RENAME COLUMN nickname TO preferred_name;

-- Constraints
ALTER TABLE employees ADD CONSTRAINT emp_sal_check CHECK (salary BETWEEN 0 AND 999999);
ALTER TABLE employees DISABLE CONSTRAINT emp_sal_check;
ALTER TABLE employees ENABLE CONSTRAINT emp_sal_check;
ALTER TABLE employees DROP CONSTRAINT emp_sal_check;

-- Rename table
ALTER TABLE employees RENAME TO emp;
-- Or: RENAME employees TO emp;

6.3 Constraints Reference

Constraint Purpose Null Allowed Notes
PRIMARY KEY Unique row identifier No One per table; creates unique index
UNIQUE No duplicate non-null values Yes Multiple per table; nulls don't conflict
NOT NULL Column must have value — Column-level only
CHECK Column must satisfy expression Yes Cannot reference other tables
FOREIGN KEY Referential integrity Yes References parent PK or UNIQUE
DEFAULT Provides default value — Not a constraint technically 
-- Deferred constraints (validated at COMMIT, not at DML)
ALTER TABLE orders ADD CONSTRAINT ord_fk_cust
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES customers (customer_id)
    DEFERRABLE INITIALLY DEFERRED;

SET CONSTRAINTS ord_fk_cust DEFERRED;
SET CONSTRAINTS ALL DEFERRED;

7. DML Statements

7.1 INSERT

-- Single row
INSERT INTO employees (employee_id, last_name, email, hire_date, job_id, salary)
VALUES (207, 'Smith', 'SMITH', DATE '2024-01-15', 'IT_PROG', 5000);

-- All columns (order-dependent — fragile, avoid in production)
INSERT INTO departments VALUES (280, 'R&D', 100, 1700);

-- From subquery
INSERT INTO emp_archive
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE termination_date < SYSDATE;

-- Multi-table insert (Oracle-specific)
INSERT ALL
    INTO order_items_2024 VALUES (order_id, item_id, quantity)
    INTO order_summary   VALUES (order_id, total)
SELECT order_id, item_id, quantity, total FROM orders_staging;

-- Conditional multi-table insert
INSERT ALL
    WHEN salary < 5000 THEN INTO low_earners VALUES (employee_id, salary)
    WHEN salary BETWEEN 5000 AND 10000 THEN INTO mid_earners VALUES (employee_id, salary)
    WHEN salary > 10000 THEN INTO high_earners VALUES (employee_id, salary)
SELECT employee_id, salary FROM employees;

-- RETURNING clause (get generated values back)
DECLARE
    v_id employees.employee_id%TYPE;
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO employees (employee_id, last_name, email, hire_date, job_id)
    VALUES (emp_seq.NEXTVAL, 'Jones', 'JONES', SYSDATE, 'SA_REP')
    RETURNING employee_id INTO v_id;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('New ID: ' || v_id);
END;
/

7.2 UPDATE

-- Basic update
UPDATE employees
SET    salary = salary * 1.1,
       last_modified = SYSDATE
WHERE  department_id = 20;

-- Update with subquery (correlated)
UPDATE employees e
SET    salary = (
           SELECT AVG(salary) * 1.05
           FROM   employees
           WHERE  department_id = e.department_id
       )
WHERE  salary < (
           SELECT AVG(salary)
           FROM   employees
           WHERE  department_id = e.department_id
       );

-- UPDATE with join (update inline view)
UPDATE (
    SELECT e.salary, e.department_id, d.department_name
    FROM   employees e
           JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
    WHERE  d.location_id = 1700
)
SET salary = salary * 1.08;
-- Note: join column must be key-preserved (unique key on join side)

7.3 DELETE vs TRUNCATE

-- DELETE — logged, rollback-able, fires row-level triggers
DELETE FROM employees WHERE department_id = 99;
DELETE FROM employees;   -- Delete all rows (slow, logged)

-- TRUNCATE — DDL, fast, not rollback-able, resets HWM
TRUNCATE TABLE employees;
TRUNCATE TABLE employees REUSE STORAGE;  -- Keep allocated space

-- TRUNCATE vs DELETE comparison:
-- TRUNCATE: DDL (auto-commit), no WHERE, resets HWM, no triggers, faster
-- DELETE: DML, supports WHERE, rollback-able, fires triggers, slower

7.4 MERGE (Upsert)

-- MERGE — insert OR update OR delete in one statement
MERGE INTO target_employees tgt
USING (
    SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary, department_id
    FROM   staging_employees
) src
ON (tgt.employee_id = src.employee_id)
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET
        tgt.last_name     = src.last_name,
        tgt.salary        = src.salary,
        tgt.last_updated  = SYSDATE
    WHERE tgt.salary <> src.salary           -- Only if salary changed
    DELETE WHERE src.salary IS NULL          -- Delete if salary is NULL in source
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (employee_id, last_name, salary, department_id, hire_date)
    VALUES (src.employee_id, src.last_name, src.salary, src.department_id, SYSDATE);

-- MERGE for upsert using DUAL
MERGE INTO settings s
USING (SELECT 'THEME' AS key, 'DARK' AS val FROM DUAL) src
ON (s.setting_key = src.key)
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET s.setting_value = src.val
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (setting_key, setting_value) VALUES (src.key, src.val);

8. TCL — Transaction Control

-- Transaction begins automatically with first DML statement
-- (Oracle does NOT auto-commit — unlike MySQL default)

INSERT INTO orders (order_id, amount) VALUES (1001, 500);
UPDATE inventory SET qty = qty - 1 WHERE product_id = 42;

COMMIT;             -- Make permanent, release locks

-- ROLLBACK — undo entire transaction
ROLLBACK;

-- SAVEPOINT — partial rollback
SAVEPOINT before_risky_update;

UPDATE employees SET salary = salary * 2;   -- Oops, too much

ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT before_risky_update;  -- Undo back to savepoint
-- (Transaction still active — must COMMIT or ROLLBACK fully)

COMMIT;

-- Implicit commits in Oracle (DDL auto-commits):
-- CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE, RENAME cause implicit COMMIT

-- Transaction properties
SET TRANSACTION READ ONLY;               -- No DML allowed in this tx
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
SET TRANSACTION NAME 'payroll_run';

Interview Questions

  • What triggers an implicit commit in Oracle? (DDL statements, normal program exit, disconnection)
  • What is the default isolation level in Oracle? (Read Committed)
  • Difference between ROLLBACK and ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT? (Full undo vs partial undo; savepoint leaves tx open)

9. DCL — Data Control

-- GRANT privileges
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON employees TO hr_user;
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO PUBLIC;             -- All users
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON employees TO hr_admin;
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO app_role WITH GRANT OPTION;  -- Can re-grant

-- GRANT system privileges
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO hr_user;
GRANT CREATE TABLE TO hr_user;
GRANT DBA TO admin_user;                         -- Full DBA role

-- REVOKE
REVOKE INSERT ON employees FROM hr_user;
REVOKE DBA FROM admin_user;

-- Roles
CREATE ROLE read_only_role;
GRANT SELECT ON employees TO read_only_role;
GRANT SELECT ON departments TO read_only_role;
GRANT read_only_role TO reporting_user;

-- Row Level Security (VPD — Virtual Private Database)
BEGIN
    DBMS_RLS.ADD_POLICY(
        object_schema  => 'HR',
        object_name    => 'EMPLOYEES',
        policy_name    => 'EMP_DEPT_POLICY',
        function_schema => 'HR',
        policy_function => 'DEPT_SECURITY_FUNCTION',
        statement_types => 'SELECT,INSERT,UPDATE,DELETE'
    );
END;
/

10. Oracle Data Types

10.1 Data Type Reference

Type Description Size / Notes
NUMBER(p,s) Numeric, p=precision, s=scale Up to 38 digits
NUMBER Floating-point numeric
VARCHAR2(n) Variable-length string Max 4000 (32767 in PL/SQL)
CHAR(n) Fixed-length, space-padded Max 2000
NVARCHAR2(n) Unicode variable-length
DATE Date + time (to second) No timezone
TIMESTAMP(p) Date + time (fractional secs) 0–9 digits
TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE Timestamp + TZ offset
TIMESTAMP WITH LOCAL TIME ZONE Stored in DB TZ, displayed in session TZ
INTERVAL YEAR TO MONTH Duration in years/months
INTERVAL DAY TO SECOND Duration in days/seconds
CLOB Character large object Up to 4GB (128TB with SECUREFILE)
BLOB Binary large object Up to 4GB
RAW(n) Raw binary data Max 2000 bytes
ROWID Physical row address
BOOLEAN True/False/Unknown PL/SQL only (SQL in Oracle 23c)
JSON Native JSON type Oracle 21c+
VECTOR Vector embeddings Oracle 23c (AI applications) 
-- DATE arithmetic
SYSDATE + 1             -- Tomorrow
SYSDATE - 7             -- One week ago
DATE '2024-12-25'       -- Date literal
TIMESTAMP '2024-12-25 08:00:00'

-- INTERVAL
SYSDATE + INTERVAL '3' MONTH
SYSDATE + INTERVAL '2' YEAR
SYSDATE + INTERVAL '1 12:30:00' DAY TO SECOND

-- TIMESTAMP with TZ
SELECT SYSTIMESTAMP AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York' FROM DUAL;
SELECT FROM_TZ(CAST(hire_date AS TIMESTAMP), 'America/Chicago') FROM employees;

11. Built-in Functions

11.1 String Functions

-- SUBSTR(string, start, [length]) — start is 1-based
SUBSTR('Hello World', 1, 5)          -- 'Hello'
SUBSTR('Hello World', 7)             -- 'World'
SUBSTR('Hello World', -5)            -- 'World' (negative = from end)

-- INSTR(string, substring, [start], [occurrence])
INSTR('Hello World', 'l')            -- 3 (first 'l')
INSTR('Hello World', 'l', 4)         -- 4 (starting from pos 4)
INSTR('Hello World', 'l', 1, 3)      -- 10 (third occurrence)

-- String manipulation
LENGTH('Hello')                      -- 5
UPPER('hello'); LOWER('HELLO')
INITCAP('hello world')               -- 'Hello World'
TRIM('  hello  ')                    -- 'hello'
LTRIM('  hello  '); RTRIM('  hello  ')
LPAD('42', 5, '0')                   -- '00042'
RPAD('hello', 10, '.')               -- 'hello.....'
REPLACE('Hello World', 'World', 'Oracle')  -- 'Hello Oracle'
TRANSLATE('Hello', 'aeiou', '12345') -- Replace chars 1-to-1
CONCAT('Hello', ' World')            -- 'Hello World' (use || for more than 2)
REVERSE('Hello')                     -- 'olleH'
REGEXP_REPLACE('Phone: 123-456-7890', '\d', 'X')  -- 'Phone: XXX-XXX-XXXX'
REGEXP_SUBSTR('2024-01-15', '\d{4}', 1, 1)        -- '2024'
REGEXP_INSTR('Hello World', 'W')     -- 7
REGEXP_LIKE(phone_number, '^\d{3}-\d{3}-\d{4}$')  -- Boolean (use in WHERE)

11.2 Numeric Functions

ROUND(3.456, 2)          -- 3.46
ROUND(3456, -2)          -- 3500 (negative scale rounds to tens/hundreds)
TRUNC(3.789, 2)          -- 3.78 (truncates, no rounding)
TRUNC(3.789)             -- 3    (default 0)
MOD(17, 5)               -- 2    (remainder)
ABS(-42)                 -- 42
CEIL(3.2)                -- 4    (smallest integer >=)
FLOOR(3.9)               -- 3    (largest integer <=)
POWER(2, 10)             -- 1024
SQRT(144)                -- 12
SIGN(-5); SIGN(0); SIGN(5)   -- -1, 0, 1
LN(2.718); LOG(10, 100)  -- Natural log, log base 10

11.3 Date Functions

SYSDATE                              -- Current date+time (no TZ)
SYSTIMESTAMP                         -- Current timestamp with TZ
CURRENT_DATE                         -- Session date
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP                    -- Session timestamp with TZ

ADD_MONTHS(SYSDATE, 3)              -- Add 3 months
ADD_MONTHS(SYSDATE, -6)             -- Subtract 6 months
MONTHS_BETWEEN(DATE '2024-12-31', DATE '2024-01-01')  -- 11 (months diff)
NEXT_DAY(SYSDATE, 'FRIDAY')         -- Next Friday
LAST_DAY(SYSDATE)                   -- Last day of current month
LAST_DAY(ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE,'MONTH'),-1))  -- Last day of previous month
TRUNC(SYSDATE)                      -- Midnight of today
TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'MM')                -- First day of current month
TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR')              -- First day of current year
TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'IW')                -- First day of ISO week (Monday)

EXTRACT(YEAR  FROM SYSDATE)         -- 2024
EXTRACT(MONTH FROM SYSDATE)         -- 1
EXTRACT(DAY   FROM SYSDATE)         -- 15
EXTRACT(HOUR  FROM SYSTIMESTAMP)    -- Hour (TIMESTAMP only)

-- Date formatting
TO_CHAR(SYSDATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD')              -- '2024-01-15'
TO_CHAR(SYSDATE, 'DD/MM/YYYY Hh34:MI:SS')   -- '15/01/2024 14:30:00'
TO_CHAR(SYSDATE, 'Day, DD Month YYYY')       -- 'Monday, 15 January 2024'
TO_CHAR(1234567.89, '99,999,990.00')         -- '1,234,567.89'
TO_CHAR(0.75, 'FM990.00%')                  -- '75.00%'

TO_DATE('2024-01-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD')
TO_DATE('15/01/2024', 'DD/MM/YYYY')
TO_TIMESTAMP('2024-01-15 14:30:00.123', 'YYYY-MM-DD Hh34:MI:SS.FF3')
TO_NUMBER('1,234.56', '999,990.99')         -- 1234.56

11.4 Aggregate Functions

COUNT(*)                       -- All rows including NULLs
COUNT(column)                  -- Non-NULL values
COUNT(DISTINCT column)         -- Distinct non-NULL values
SUM(salary)
SUM(DISTINCT salary)           -- Sum of distinct values
AVG(salary)                    -- Ignores NULLs
MIN(hire_date); MAX(hire_date)
MEDIAN(salary)                 -- 50th percentile
STATS_MODE(salary)             -- Most frequent value

-- LISTAGG — string aggregation
SELECT department_id,
       LISTAGG(last_name, ', ')
           WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY last_name) AS employees
FROM   employees
GROUP BY department_id;

-- Handle overflow (Oracle 12.2+)
SELECT department_id,
       LISTAGG(last_name, ', ' ON OVERFLOW TRUNCATE '...' WITHOUT COUNT)
           WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY last_name) AS employees
FROM   employees
GROUP BY department_id;

-- JSON aggregation (Oracle 21c+)
SELECT JSON_ARRAYAGG(
    JSON_OBJECT('id' VALUE employee_id, 'name' VALUE last_name)
    ORDER BY last_name
) FROM employees WHERE department_id = 10;

12. Window & Analytical Functions

12.1 OVER Clause — The Foundation

Definition

Analytical (window) functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row, without collapsing the result set (unlike GROUP BY).

function_name(args)
OVER (
    [PARTITION BY col1, col2]      -- Define window partitions (groups)
    [ORDER BY col3 [ASC|DESC]]     -- Ordering within window
    [ROWS|RANGE BETWEEN frame_start AND frame_end]  -- Frame definition
)

Frame Definitions

ROWS  BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW   -- All prior rows + current
ROWS  BETWEEN 1 PRECEDING AND 1 FOLLOWING            -- Sliding window: prev + curr + next
ROWS  BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING  -- Entire partition
RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '7' DAY PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW  -- Last 7 days (by value)

-- ROWS vs RANGE:
-- ROWS: physical rows (ignores duplicate values)
-- RANGE: rows with values in range (handles ties together)

12.2 Ranking Functions

SELECT employee_id, last_name, department_id, salary,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS row_num,
       RANK()       OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank,
       NTILE(4)     OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) AS quartile
FROM employees;

-- Difference between ranking functions:
-- Salaries:  10000, 8000, 8000, 6000
-- ROW_NUMBER:   1,    2,    3,    4  (unique, arbitrary for ties)
-- RANK:         1,    2,    2,    4  (skips after ties)
-- DENSE_RANK:   1,    2,    2,    3  (no skips)
-- NTILE(4):     1,    2,    3,    4  (distributes into N buckets)

-- Top-N per group (classic interview question)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT employee_id, last_name, department_id, salary,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) dr
    FROM   employees
)
WHERE dr <= 3;    -- Top 3 salaries per department (ties included)

12.3 Navigation Functions

SELECT employee_id, last_name, hire_date, salary,
       -- LAG/LEAD with offset and default
       LAG(salary, 1, 0)    OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY hire_date) AS prev_salary,
       LEAD(salary, 1, 0)   OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY hire_date) AS next_salary,
       salary - LAG(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY hire_date) AS salary_diff,

       -- FIRST_VALUE / LAST_VALUE
       FIRST_VALUE(salary)  OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC
                                  ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) AS max_in_dept,
       LAST_VALUE(salary)   OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC
                                  ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) AS min_in_dept,

       -- NTH_VALUE(expr, n) — Oracle 11g+
       NTH_VALUE(salary, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC
                                   ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS second_highest
FROM employees;

-- FIRST_VALUE pitfall: default frame is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING TO CURRENT ROW
-- For LAST_VALUE to work correctly, must specify full frame:
-- ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING

12.4 Aggregate Window Functions — Running Totals & Moving Averages

SELECT order_date, amount,
       -- Running total (cumulative sum)
       SUM(amount)   OVER (ORDER BY order_date
                            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total,

       -- Moving average (last 7 days including today)
       AVG(amount)   OVER (ORDER BY order_date
                            ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_7,

       -- Partition + running total
       SUM(amount)   OVER (PARTITION BY region_id ORDER BY order_date
                            ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS region_running_total,

       -- Running count
       COUNT(*)      OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS cumulative_count,

       -- Percentage of total
       ROUND(amount / SUM(amount) OVER () * 100, 2) AS pct_of_grand_total,

       -- Percentage of partition total
       ROUND(amount / SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY region_id) * 100, 2) AS pct_of_region
FROM   orders;

12.5 Statistical Functions

SELECT department_id, salary,
       PERCENT_RANK()   OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary) AS pct_rank,
       CUME_DIST()      OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary) AS cume_dist,
       STDDEV(salary)   OVER (PARTITION BY department_id)                 AS std_dev,
       VARIANCE(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id)                 AS variance,

       -- RATIO_TO_REPORT — proportion of value to total in partition
       RATIO_TO_REPORT(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id)          AS ratio
FROM employees;

-- PERCENT_RANK: (rank-1)/(rows-1) — 0 to 1
-- CUME_DIST:    rank/rows         — (0,1]  (no zeros)

12.6 Window Function Cheat Sheet

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               WINDOW FUNCTION QUICK REFERENCE                   │
├──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ RANKING          │ ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, NTILE         │
│ NAVIGATION       │ LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE, NTH_VALUE│
│ AGGREGATE        │ SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX (all with OVER)   │
│ STATISTICAL      │ STDDEV, VARIANCE, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST   │
│                  │ RATIO_TO_REPORT, MEDIAN                      │
├──────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ PARTITION BY     │ Divides rows into groups (like GROUP BY)     │
│ ORDER BY         │ Defines ordering within partition            │
│ ROWS BETWEEN     │ Physical row frame                           │
│ RANGE BETWEEN    │ Logical value-based frame                    │
└──────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Interview Questions

  • What is the difference between ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK?
  • Why does LAST_VALUE often return unexpected results? (Default frame stops at current row)
  • Can window functions appear in WHERE clause? (No — wrap in subquery or CTE)
  • What is the difference between ROWS and RANGE frame? (ROWS: physical; RANGE: value-based)

13. Oracle-Specific Features

13.1 ROWNUM and ROWID

-- ROWNUM — pseudo-column assigned BEFORE ORDER BY (common mistake!)
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE ROWNUM <= 10;   -- Top 10 by physical storage

-- ROWID — physical row address (fastest way to access a row)
SELECT ROWID, last_name FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100;
-- Use in multi-step updates:
DELETE FROM employees WHERE ROWID IN (
    SELECT MIN(ROWID) FROM employees GROUP BY last_name HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
);  -- Delete all but one duplicate

13.2 Hierarchical Queries (CONNECT BY)

-- CONNECT BY — Oracle's native hierarchical query
SELECT LEVEL,
       employee_id,
       LPAD(' ', (LEVEL-1)*4) || last_name AS org_chart,
       manager_id,
       SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH(last_name, ' / ')  AS full_path,
       CONNECT_BY_ISLEAF                       AS is_leaf,
       CONNECT_BY_ISCYCLE                      AS is_cycle
FROM   employees
START WITH  manager_id IS NULL                  -- Root node(s)
CONNECT BY  NOCYCLE PRIOR employee_id = manager_id  -- Parent → child relationship
ORDER SIBLINGS BY last_name;                    -- Sort within each level

-- Useful hierarchical functions
LEVEL                     -- Depth in tree (1 = root)
PRIOR column              -- Value from parent row
SYS_CONNECT_BY_PATH(col, delimiter)  -- Full path from root
CONNECT_BY_ROOT(col)      -- Value at root of current path
CONNECT_BY_ISLEAF         -- 1 if no children, 0 otherwise
CONNECT_BY_ISCYCLE        -- 1 if cycle detected (with NOCYCLE)

-- Generating rows with CONNECT BY
SELECT LEVEL AS n FROM DUAL CONNECT BY LEVEL <= 10;  -- 1 to 10
SELECT TRUNC(SYSDATE) + LEVEL - 1 AS cal_date
FROM   DUAL
CONNECT BY LEVEL <= 30;   -- Calendar of 30 days

13.3 PIVOT and UNPIVOT

-- PIVOT — rows to columns
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT department_id, job_id, salary
    FROM   employees
)
PIVOT (
    AVG(salary)
    FOR job_id IN (
        'IT_PROG'  AS it_prog,
        'SA_REP'   AS sa_rep,
        'ST_CLERK' AS st_clerk
    )
)
ORDER BY department_id;

-- Dynamic PIVOT (using XML — flexible column set)
SELECT * FROM (
    SELECT department_id, job_id, salary FROM employees
)
PIVOT XML (
    AVG(salary) FOR job_id IN (ANY)
);

-- UNPIVOT — columns to rows
SELECT department_id, quarter, sales_amount
FROM   quarterly_sales
UNPIVOT (
    sales_amount
    FOR quarter IN (q1 AS 'Q1', q2 AS 'Q2', q3 AS 'Q3', q4 AS 'Q4')
)
ORDER BY department_id, quarter;

-- UNPIVOT INCLUDE NULLS — include rows where value is NULL
UNPIVOT INCLUDE NULLS (sales_amount FOR quarter IN (q1, q2, q3, q4))

13.4 Flashback Features

-- Flashback Query — see data as of a past time
SELECT * FROM employees AS OF TIMESTAMP (SYSTIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '1' HOUR);
SELECT * FROM employees AS OF SCN 1234567;

-- Flashback Table — restore table to past state
FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO TIMESTAMP (SYSTIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '30' MINUTE);
FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO SCN 1234567;

-- Flashback drop — recover dropped table from Recycle Bin
SHOW RECYCLEBIN;
FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO BEFORE DROP;
FLASHBACK TABLE employees TO BEFORE DROP RENAME TO employees_recovered;

-- Purge recycle bin
PURGE TABLE employees;
PURGE RECYCLEBIN;
PURGE DBA_RECYCLEBIN;

14. Indexes & Performance

14.1 Index Types

-- B-tree index (default) — good for high cardinality, range scans, =, <, >, BETWEEN
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_lastname ON employees (last_name);
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_dept_sal ON employees (department_id, salary);  -- Composite

-- Unique index
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_emp_email ON employees (email);

-- Function-based index (FBI) — index result of expression
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_upper_name ON employees (UPPER(last_name));
-- Required for: WHERE UPPER(last_name) = 'SMITH' to use index

-- Descending index
CREATE INDEX idx_emp_sal_desc ON employees (salary DESC);

-- Bitmap index — low cardinality, read-heavy, DW environments
CREATE BITMAP INDEX idx_emp_gender ON employees (gender);
-- Good for: status, gender, type columns with few distinct values
-- BAD for: OLTP (row-level locking becomes block-level; DML contention)

-- Partial index (Oracle 12c+) — index subset of rows
CREATE INDEX idx_active_orders ON orders (created_date)
    WHERE status = 'ACTIVE';

-- Invisible index (Oracle 11g+) — exists but optimizer ignores it (testing)
CREATE INDEX idx_test ON employees (job_id) INVISIBLE;
ALTER INDEX idx_test VISIBLE;

-- Unusable index
ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lastname UNUSABLE;
ALTER INDEX idx_emp_lastname REBUILD;

14.2 Execution Plans

-- EXPLAIN PLAN
EXPLAIN PLAN FOR
    SELECT e.last_name, d.department_name
    FROM   employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id
    WHERE  e.salary > 10000;

SELECT * FROM TABLE(DBMS_XPLAN.DISPLAY(FORMAT => 'ALL'));

-- AUTOTRACE (SQL*Plus)
SET AUTOTRACE ON;
SET AUTOTRACE TRACEONLY EXPLAIN;

-- DBMS_XPLAN formats
-- 'BASIC'     — minimal info
-- 'TYPICAL'   — default, most useful info
-- 'ALL'       — everything
-- 'ADVANCED'  — with predicate information

-- Read execution plan: bottom-up, right-to-left at same level
-- Operation: TABLE ACCESS FULL, INDEX RANGE SCAN, NESTED LOOPS, HASH JOIN, SORT

-- Key statistics to check:
-- Rows (estimated cardinality vs actual)
-- Cost (optimizer's estimate)
-- Bytes
-- Predicate Information (access vs filter predicates)

14.3 Optimizer Hints

-- Hints go in comment block: /*+ hint */
SELECT /*+ FULL(e) */ * FROM employees e;         -- Force full table scan
SELECT /*+ INDEX(e idx_emp_dept_sal) */ *          -- Force specific index
FROM   employees e WHERE department_id = 20;

SELECT /*+ USE_NL(e d) */                          -- Force nested loop join
       e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

SELECT /*+ USE_HASH(e d) */                        -- Force hash join
       e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

SELECT /*+ LEADING(d e) */                         -- Control join order
       e.last_name, d.department_name
FROM   employees e JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(e 4) */ * FROM employees e;   -- 4 parallel processes
SELECT /*+ NO_PARALLEL */ * FROM employees;        -- Disable parallel

SELECT /*+ RESULT_CACHE */ * FROM employees;       -- Cache result set

-- Common performance hints:
-- /*+ ALL_ROWS */          — optimize for total throughput
-- /*+ FIRST_ROWS(n) */     — optimize for first n rows
-- /*+ NO_MERGE(v) */       — prevent view merging
-- /*+ MATERIALIZE */       — materialize CTE/view

14.4 Indexing Strategy

Index Selectivity Guidelines:
- HIGH cardinality (unique/near-unique): B-tree index — excellent
- MEDIUM cardinality: B-tree index — good
- LOW cardinality (gender, status): Bitmap in DW, skip for OLTP
- Composite index column order: most selective first (usually)
  EXCEPTION: equality columns before range columns in index
  e.g., WHERE dept=10 AND salary > 5000 → INDEX(dept, salary) NOT (salary, dept)

When NOT to index:
- Small tables (full scan is faster)
- Columns rarely used in WHERE/JOIN
- Tables with heavy DML (index maintenance overhead)
- Already covered by another index

Invisible indexes: Test impact before making permanent
Index monitoring: ALTER INDEX idx_name MONITORING USAGE;

15. Partitioning

-- Range partitioning — by date ranges (most common)
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id   NUMBER,
    order_date DATE,
    amount     NUMBER
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date) (
    PARTITION orders_2022 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2023-01-01'),
    PARTITION orders_2023 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2024-01-01'),
    PARTITION orders_2024 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2025-01-01'),
    PARTITION orders_future VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE)
);

-- Interval partitioning (Oracle 11g+) — auto-creates partitions
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id   NUMBER,
    order_date DATE,
    amount     NUMBER
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (order_date)
INTERVAL (NUMTOYMINTERVAL(1, 'MONTH'))  -- New partition monthly
(PARTITION orders_2024_01 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2024-02-01'));

-- List partitioning — by discrete values
CREATE TABLE employees (
    employee_id  NUMBER,
    last_name    VARCHAR2(25),
    region       VARCHAR2(20)
)
PARTITION BY LIST (region) (
    PARTITION p_north VALUES ('North', 'Northeast', 'Northwest'),
    PARTITION p_south VALUES ('South', 'Southeast', 'Southwest'),
    PARTITION p_other VALUES (DEFAULT)
);

-- Hash partitioning — even distribution
CREATE TABLE orders (order_id NUMBER, customer_id NUMBER, amount NUMBER)
PARTITION BY HASH (customer_id)
PARTITIONS 8;

-- Partition pruning — optimizer skips irrelevant partitions
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE order_date BETWEEN DATE '2024-01-01' AND DATE '2024-12-31';
-- Only scans orders_2024 partition — massive performance gain

-- Partition operations
ALTER TABLE orders ADD PARTITION orders_2025 VALUES LESS THAN (DATE '2026-01-01');
ALTER TABLE orders DROP PARTITION orders_2022;
ALTER TABLE orders TRUNCATE PARTITION orders_2022;
ALTER TABLE orders SPLIT PARTITION orders_2024
    AT (DATE '2024-07-01')
    INTO (PARTITION orders_2024_h2, PARTITION orders_2024_h3);
ALTER TABLE orders MERGE PARTITIONS orders_2022, orders_2023 INTO PARTITION orders_old;

16. Views, Synonyms & GTTs

16.1 Views

-- Simple view
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW vw_emp_dept AS
SELECT e.employee_id, e.last_name, e.salary,
       d.department_name
FROM   employees e
       JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

-- Force view creation (even if base table doesn't exist yet)
CREATE OR REPLACE FORCE VIEW vw_future AS
SELECT * FROM future_table;

-- WITH CHECK OPTION — ensure DML through view satisfies view condition
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW vw_dept_10 AS
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = 10
WITH CHECK OPTION;

UPDATE vw_dept_10 SET department_id = 20;  -- Error! Violates CHECK OPTION

-- WITH READ ONLY — prevent DML through view
CREATE VIEW vw_readonly AS
SELECT * FROM employees WITH READ ONLY;

-- Key-preserved tables allow DML through join views
-- DML only allowed on key-preserved side of join

-- Materialized View (snapshot — stores data physically)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_dept_summary
BUILD IMMEDIATE                              -- Populate now
REFRESH FAST ON COMMIT                       -- Refresh incrementally on commit
ENABLE QUERY REWRITE                         -- Allow optimizer to use this MV
AS
SELECT department_id,
       COUNT(*)    AS headcount,
       AVG(salary) AS avg_salary,
       SUM(salary) AS total_salary
FROM   employees
GROUP BY department_id;

-- REFRESH options
EXEC DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH('MV_DEPT_SUMMARY', 'C');  -- Complete refresh
EXEC DBMS_MVIEW.REFRESH('MV_DEPT_SUMMARY', 'F');  -- Fast refresh

16.2 Synonyms & GTTs

-- Synonym — alias for object (simplify or abstract location)
CREATE SYNONYM emp FOR hr.employees;
CREATE PUBLIC SYNONYM emp FOR hr.employees;  -- All users

-- Database link + synonym (remote table access)
CREATE DATABASE LINK prod_link CONNECT TO user IDENTIFIED BY pwd USING 'PROD';
SELECT * FROM employees@prod_link;
CREATE SYNONYM remote_emp FOR employees@prod_link;

-- Global Temporary Table
CREATE GLOBAL TEMPORARY TABLE gtt_session (
    id NUMBER, value VARCHAR2(100)
) ON COMMIT DELETE ROWS;    -- Data purged at COMMIT

CREATE GLOBAL TEMPORARY TABLE gtt_persist (
    id NUMBER, value VARCHAR2(100)
) ON COMMIT PRESERVE ROWS;  -- Data kept until session ends

-- GTT properties: session-specific data, no redo for data, structure is permanent

17. Sequences & Identity Columns

-- Create sequence
CREATE SEQUENCE emp_seq
    START WITH 207
    INCREMENT BY 1
    MAXVALUE 999999
    NOCACHE                          -- Or CACHE 20 (cache 20 values)
    NOCYCLE                          -- Or CYCLE
    NOORDER;                         -- Or ORDER (RAC)

-- Usage
SELECT emp_seq.NEXTVAL FROM DUAL;    -- Get next value (increments)
SELECT emp_seq.CURRVAL FROM DUAL;    -- Current value (must call NEXTVAL first in session)
INSERT INTO employees (employee_id, ...) VALUES (emp_seq.NEXTVAL, ...);

-- Alter sequence
ALTER SEQUENCE emp_seq INCREMENT BY 10;
ALTER SEQUENCE emp_seq RESTART START WITH 1;  -- Oracle 18c+

-- Identity columns (Oracle 12c+) — auto-generated by DB
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id   NUMBER GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,   -- Cannot insert manually
    order_date DATE DEFAULT SYSDATE
);

CREATE TABLE products (
    product_id NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY  -- Can insert manually
                START WITH 100 INCREMENT BY 1,
    product_name VARCHAR2(100)
);

-- Check sequence cache: large cache = faster, gaps on restart
-- NOCACHE: no gaps, slower (one redo per nextval)

18. PL/SQL Fundamentals

18.1 Block Structure

-- PL/SQL anonymous block
DECLARE
    -- Variable declarations
    v_employee_id  employees.employee_id%TYPE;    -- Anchored type
    v_salary       NUMBER(10, 2)  := 0;
    v_name         VARCHAR2(100);
    c_max_salary   CONSTANT NUMBER := 999999;     -- Constant
    v_emp_rec      employees%ROWTYPE;             -- Whole row
BEGIN
    -- Executable statements
    SELECT employee_id, salary, last_name
    INTO   v_employee_id, v_salary, v_name
    FROM   employees
    WHERE  employee_id = 100;

    IF v_salary > 10000 THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_name || ' is a high earner: ' || v_salary);
    END IF;

EXCEPTION
    -- Exception handling
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Employee not found');
    WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Multiple rows returned');
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Error: ' || SQLERRM);
        RAISE;   -- Re-raise
END;
/     -- Slash executes the block in SQL*Plus/SQLcl

-- Enable output (SQL*Plus / SQLcl)
SET SERVEROUTPUT ON SIZE UNLIMITED;

18.2 Variables & Types

DECLARE
    -- Scalar types
    v_count    NUMBER(6)         := 0;
    v_name     VARCHAR2(50)      := 'Default';
    v_active   BOOLEAN           := TRUE;
    v_today    DATE              := SYSDATE;

    -- %TYPE — inherit column type (adapts to schema changes)
    v_salary   employees.salary%TYPE;
    v_last_nm  employees.last_name%TYPE;

    -- %ROWTYPE — inherit entire row structure
    v_emp_row  employees%ROWTYPE;

    -- Record type (custom)
    TYPE t_emp_info IS RECORD (
        emp_id   NUMBER,
        name     VARCHAR2(100),
        salary   NUMBER
    );
    v_emp_info  t_emp_info;

BEGIN
    -- Assign anchored row
    SELECT * INTO v_emp_row FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_emp_row.last_name || ' earns ' || v_emp_row.salary);

    -- Assign record fields
    v_emp_info.emp_id := 100;
    v_emp_info.name   := 'King';
END;
/

19. PL/SQL Control Structures

DECLARE
    v_salary  NUMBER := 8000;
    v_grade   VARCHAR2(10);
    v_count   NUMBER := 0;
BEGIN
    -- IF / ELSIF / ELSE
    IF v_salary < 3000 THEN
        v_grade := 'LOW';
    ELSIF v_salary BETWEEN 3000 AND 8000 THEN
        v_grade := 'MED';
    ELSIF v_salary > 8000 THEN
        v_grade := 'HIGH';
    ELSE
        v_grade := 'UNKNOWN';
    END IF;

    -- CASE statement (vs CASE expression)
    CASE v_grade
        WHEN 'LOW'  THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Consider raise');
        WHEN 'MED'  THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('At market rate');
        WHEN 'HIGH' THEN DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Well paid');
        ELSE             DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('No grade');
    END CASE;

    -- Basic LOOP with EXIT
    LOOP
        v_count := v_count + 1;
        EXIT WHEN v_count >= 5;
    END LOOP;

    -- WHILE LOOP
    WHILE v_count < 10 LOOP
        v_count := v_count + 1;
    END LOOP;

    -- Numeric FOR LOOP (v_i declared implicitly, read-only)
    FOR v_i IN 1..10 LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Iteration: ' || v_i);
    END LOOP;

    -- Reverse FOR
    FOR v_i IN REVERSE 1..5 LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_i);   -- 5,4,3,2,1
    END LOOP;

    -- CONTINUE (skip iteration)
    FOR v_i IN 1..10 LOOP
        CONTINUE WHEN MOD(v_i, 2) = 0;  -- Skip even numbers
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_i);
    END LOOP;
END;
/

20. PL/SQL Cursors

20.1 Explicit Cursor

DECLARE
    CURSOR c_emp (p_dept_id NUMBER, p_min_sal NUMBER := 0) IS
        SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
        FROM   employees
        WHERE  department_id = p_dept_id
          AND  salary >= p_min_sal
        ORDER BY salary DESC
        FOR UPDATE OF salary NOWAIT;     -- Lock rows for update

    v_emp_rec  c_emp%ROWTYPE;
BEGIN
    OPEN c_emp(20, 5000);              -- Parameterized cursor

    LOOP
        FETCH c_emp INTO v_emp_rec;
        EXIT WHEN c_emp%NOTFOUND;       -- No more rows

        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_emp_rec.last_name || ': ' || v_emp_rec.salary);

        UPDATE employees
        SET    salary = salary * 1.05
        WHERE  CURRENT OF c_emp;       -- Update current fetched row
    END LOOP;

    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Rows processed: ' || c_emp%ROWCOUNT);
    CLOSE c_emp;

    -- Cursor attributes:
    -- %ISOPEN  — TRUE if cursor is open
    -- %FOUND   — TRUE if last FETCH returned a row
    -- %NOTFOUND — TRUE if last FETCH returned no row
    -- %ROWCOUNT — number of rows fetched/affected
EXCEPTION
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        IF c_emp%ISOPEN THEN CLOSE c_emp; END IF;
        RAISE;
END;
/

-- Cursor FOR LOOP (simplest — auto OPEN, FETCH, CLOSE)
BEGIN
    FOR rec IN (SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary FROM employees WHERE department_id = 20)
    LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.last_name || ': ' || rec.salary);
    END LOOP;
END;
/

-- Named cursor FOR LOOP
DECLARE
    CURSOR c_emp IS SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = 20;
BEGIN
    FOR rec IN c_emp LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(rec.last_name);
    END LOOP;
END;
/

20.2 BULK COLLECT and FORALL

-- BULK COLLECT — fetch all rows at once into collections (much faster than row-by-row)
DECLARE
    TYPE t_emp_id_tbl  IS TABLE OF employees.employee_id%TYPE;
    TYPE t_salary_tbl  IS TABLE OF employees.salary%TYPE;

    l_emp_ids   t_emp_id_tbl;
    l_salaries  t_salary_tbl;
BEGIN
    -- BULK COLLECT — single context switch
    SELECT employee_id, salary
    BULK COLLECT INTO l_emp_ids, l_salaries
    FROM   employees
    WHERE  department_id = 20;

    -- LIMIT clause — batch processing (avoids PGA overrun on huge tables)
    DECLARE
        CURSOR c_emp IS SELECT employee_id, salary FROM employees;
        TYPE t_emp_tbl IS TABLE OF c_emp%ROWTYPE;
        l_emps t_emp_tbl;
    BEGIN
        OPEN c_emp;
        LOOP
            FETCH c_emp BULK COLLECT INTO l_emps LIMIT 1000;  -- 1000 rows at a time
            EXIT WHEN l_emps.COUNT = 0;

            -- FORALL — bulk DML (single context switch for all rows)
            FORALL i IN 1..l_emps.COUNT
                UPDATE employees
                SET    salary = l_emps(i).salary * 1.05
                WHERE  employee_id = l_emps(i).employee_id;

            COMMIT;  -- Commit each batch
        END LOOP;
        CLOSE c_emp;
    END;

    -- SQL%BULK_ROWCOUNT — rows affected per FORALL iteration
    FOR i IN 1..l_emp_ids.COUNT LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(SQL%BULK_ROWCOUNT(i) || ' rows for emp ' || l_emp_ids(i));
    END LOOP;
END;
/

Performance Notes

  • Row-by-row processing = "slow by slow" — each DML = round trip to SQL engine
  • BULK COLLECT + FORALL reduces context switches from N to 1
  • LIMIT 1000–5000 is typical batch size to balance memory and performance
  • FORALL requires a dense collection (no gaps) unless INDICES OF or VALUES OF used

20.3 REF CURSOR

-- REF CURSOR — pointer to result set, can be passed between programs
-- Weak REF CURSOR (ANSI SYS_REFCURSOR)
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE get_employees (
    p_dept_id  IN  employees.department_id%TYPE,
    p_cursor   OUT SYS_REFCURSOR
) AS
BEGIN
    OPEN p_cursor FOR
        SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
        FROM   employees
        WHERE  department_id = p_dept_id
        ORDER  BY salary DESC;
END;
/

-- Call it
DECLARE
    v_cursor SYS_REFCURSOR;
    v_emp_id employees.employee_id%TYPE;
    v_name   employees.last_name%TYPE;
    v_salary employees.salary%TYPE;
BEGIN
    get_employees(20, v_cursor);
    LOOP
        FETCH v_cursor INTO v_emp_id, v_name, v_salary;
        EXIT WHEN v_cursor%NOTFOUND;
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_name || ': ' || v_salary);
    END LOOP;
    CLOSE v_cursor;
END;
/

21. PL/SQL Exception Handling

DECLARE
    v_salary  employees.salary%TYPE;
    e_salary_too_high  EXCEPTION;                    -- Custom exception
    PRAGMA EXCEPTION_INIT(e_salary_too_high, -20001); -- Bind to error number
BEGIN
    SELECT salary INTO v_salary FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 999;

    IF v_salary > 100000 THEN
        RAISE e_salary_too_high;
    END IF;

    -- Raise application error
    IF v_salary < 0 THEN
        RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20002, 'Salary cannot be negative: ' || v_salary);
        -- Error numbers: -20000 to -20999 (user-defined range)
    END IF;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        -- Implicit cursor returned no rows (SELECT INTO)
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Employee 999 not found');
        -- SQLERRM: error message, SQLCODE: error number
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('SQLCODE: ' || SQLCODE || ', SQLERRM: ' || SQLERRM);

    WHEN TOO_MANY_ROWS THEN
        -- SELECT INTO returned more than one row
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Multiple employees found');

    WHEN e_salary_too_high THEN
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Salary exceeds maximum');

    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        -- Catch-all — ALWAYS re-raise or log!
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Unexpected error: ' || DBMS_UTILITY.FORMAT_ERROR_STACK);
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Backtrace: ' || DBMS_UTILITY.FORMAT_ERROR_BACKTRACE);
        RAISE;   -- Re-raise — don't swallow errors silently
END;
/

22. Procedures, Functions & Packages

22.1 Stored Procedure

CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE update_salary (
    p_employee_id  IN  employees.employee_id%TYPE,
    p_new_salary   IN  employees.salary%TYPE,
    p_old_salary   OUT employees.salary%TYPE,
    p_success      OUT BOOLEAN
) AS
    e_invalid_salary EXCEPTION;
BEGIN
    IF p_new_salary <= 0 THEN
        RAISE e_invalid_salary;
    END IF;

    SELECT salary INTO p_old_salary
    FROM   employees
    WHERE  employee_id = p_employee_id
    FOR UPDATE;     -- Lock the row

    UPDATE employees
    SET    salary = p_new_salary
    WHERE  employee_id = p_employee_id;

    p_success := TRUE;
    COMMIT;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        p_success := FALSE;
        ROLLBACK;
    WHEN e_invalid_salary THEN
        p_success := FALSE;
        RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Salary must be positive');
    WHEN OTHERS THEN
        p_success := FALSE;
        ROLLBACK;
        RAISE;
END update_salary;
/

22.2 Stored Function

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_annual_salary (
    p_employee_id IN employees.employee_id%TYPE
) RETURN NUMBER
IS
    v_salary      employees.salary%TYPE;
    v_commission  employees.commission_pct%TYPE;
BEGIN
    SELECT salary, NVL(commission_pct, 0)
    INTO   v_salary, v_commission
    FROM   employees
    WHERE  employee_id = p_employee_id;

    RETURN (v_salary + v_salary * v_commission) * 12;

EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN
        RETURN NULL;
END get_annual_salary;
/

-- Use in SQL
SELECT employee_id, get_annual_salary(employee_id) AS annual
FROM   employees WHERE department_id = 90;

-- DETERMINISTIC — result depends only on inputs (enables FBI, query rewrite)
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION tax_rate (p_salary NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER
DETERMINISTIC IS
BEGIN
    RETURN CASE WHEN p_salary > 10000 THEN 0.35 ELSE 0.25 END;
END;
/

22.3 Packages

-- Package specification (interface/API)
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE emp_pkg AS
    -- Constants
    c_max_salary  CONSTANT NUMBER := 999999;

    -- Exception
    e_invalid_dept EXCEPTION;

    -- Subprogram signatures
    PROCEDURE hire_employee (
        p_name    IN VARCHAR2,
        p_job_id  IN VARCHAR2,
        p_salary  IN NUMBER,
        p_dept_id IN NUMBER
    );

    FUNCTION get_salary (p_emp_id IN NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER;

    -- Overloaded function
    FUNCTION format_name (p_emp_id IN NUMBER) RETURN VARCHAR2;
    FUNCTION format_name (p_first IN VARCHAR2, p_last IN VARCHAR2) RETURN VARCHAR2;

END emp_pkg;
/

-- Package body (implementation)
CREATE OR REPLACE PACKAGE BODY emp_pkg AS

    -- Private variable (only accessible within package)
    g_last_hired_id NUMBER;

    PROCEDURE hire_employee (
        p_name    IN VARCHAR2,
        p_job_id  IN VARCHAR2,
        p_salary  IN NUMBER,
        p_dept_id IN NUMBER
    ) AS
    BEGIN
        -- Implementation
        INSERT INTO employees (employee_id, last_name, job_id, salary, department_id, hire_date, email)
        VALUES (emp_seq.NEXTVAL, p_name, p_job_id, p_salary, p_dept_id, SYSDATE, UPPER(p_name))
        RETURNING employee_id INTO g_last_hired_id;
        COMMIT;
    END hire_employee;

    FUNCTION get_salary (p_emp_id IN NUMBER) RETURN NUMBER AS
        v_sal NUMBER;
    BEGIN
        SELECT salary INTO v_sal FROM employees WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id;
        RETURN v_sal;
    EXCEPTION
        WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN RETURN NULL;
    END get_salary;

    FUNCTION format_name (p_emp_id IN NUMBER) RETURN VARCHAR2 AS
        v_name VARCHAR2(100);
    BEGIN
        SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name INTO v_name
        FROM employees WHERE employee_id = p_emp_id;
        RETURN v_name;
    END;

    FUNCTION format_name (p_first IN VARCHAR2, p_last IN VARCHAR2) RETURN VARCHAR2 AS
    BEGIN
        RETURN p_first || ' ' || p_last;
    END;

END emp_pkg;
/

-- Usage
BEGIN
    emp_pkg.hire_employee('Smith', 'IT_PROG', 5000, 60);
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('Salary: ' || emp_pkg.get_salary(100));
END;
/

23. Triggers

-- BEFORE INSERT trigger — auto-populate columns
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_emp_before_insert
    BEFORE INSERT ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW   -- Row-level (vs statement-level without this)
BEGIN
    IF :NEW.employee_id IS NULL THEN
        :NEW.employee_id := emp_seq.NEXTVAL;
    END IF;
    :NEW.hire_date      := NVL(:NEW.hire_date, SYSDATE);
    :NEW.created_date   := SYSDATE;
    :NEW.created_by     := SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV', 'SESSION_USER');
    :NEW.email          := UPPER(SUBSTR(:NEW.first_name, 1, 1) || :NEW.last_name);
END;
/

-- AFTER UPDATE trigger — audit log
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_emp_salary_audit
    AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON employees
    FOR EACH ROW
    WHEN (NEW.salary <> OLD.salary)    -- Conditional trigger
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO salary_audit_log (
        employee_id, old_salary, new_salary, changed_by, changed_date
    ) VALUES (
        :OLD.employee_id, :OLD.salary, :NEW.salary,
        SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','SESSION_USER'), SYSDATE
    );
END;
/

-- INSTEAD OF trigger — on views (enables DML on complex views)
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_emp_dept_view_insert
    INSTEAD OF INSERT ON vw_emp_dept
    FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO employees (employee_id, last_name, department_id)
    VALUES (:NEW.employee_id, :NEW.last_name, :NEW.department_id);
END;
/

-- DDL trigger
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_ddl_log
    AFTER DDL ON SCHEMA
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO ddl_log (
        event_type, object_type, object_name, performed_by, event_date
    ) VALUES (
        ORA_SYSEVENT, ORA_DICT_OBJ_TYPE, ORA_DICT_OBJ_NAME,
        USER, SYSDATE
    );
END;
/

-- Compound trigger (Oracle 11g+) — shared state across timing points
CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER trg_bulk_salary_check
    FOR UPDATE OF salary ON employees
    COMPOUND TRIGGER

    -- Package-level section (global to trigger)
    TYPE t_sal_tbl IS TABLE OF NUMBER INDEX BY PLS_INTEGER;
    l_new_sals t_sal_tbl;
    l_idx PLS_INTEGER := 0;

    BEFORE EACH ROW IS
    BEGIN
        l_idx := l_idx + 1;
        l_new_sals(l_idx) := :NEW.salary;
    END BEFORE EACH ROW;

    AFTER STATEMENT IS
    BEGIN
        FOR i IN 1..l_new_sals.COUNT LOOP
            IF l_new_sals(i) > 200000 THEN
                RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Salary too high');
            END IF;
        END LOOP;
    END AFTER STATEMENT;

END trg_bulk_salary_check;
/

24. Dynamic SQL

-- EXECUTE IMMEDIATE — for DDL and simple DML
DECLARE
    v_sql     VARCHAR2(4000);
    v_count   NUMBER;
    v_table   VARCHAR2(30) := 'EMPLOYEES';
BEGIN
    -- DDL
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'CREATE TABLE temp_' || v_table || ' AS SELECT * FROM ' || v_table;

    -- DML with bind variable (prevent SQL injection!)
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'UPDATE employees SET salary = :1 WHERE employee_id = :2'
        USING 6000, 100;

    -- SELECT INTO
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ' || v_table
        INTO v_count;

    -- DDL (no bind variables possible in DDL)
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'DROP TABLE temp_' || v_table || ' PURGE';
END;
/

-- RETURNING clause with dynamic SQL
DECLARE
    v_sql  VARCHAR2(200);
    v_id   NUMBER;
BEGIN
    v_sql := 'INSERT INTO orders (order_id, amount) VALUES (:1, :2) RETURNING order_id INTO :3';
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE v_sql USING ord_seq.NEXTVAL, 500 RETURNING INTO v_id;
    DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE('New order ID: ' || v_id);
END;
/

-- Dynamic REF CURSOR
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE query_table (
    p_table_name IN VARCHAR2,
    p_where      IN VARCHAR2,
    p_cursor     OUT SYS_REFCURSOR
) AS
BEGIN
    -- WARNING: validate p_table_name against data dictionary to prevent injection
    IF NOT validate_table_name(p_table_name) THEN
        RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR(-20001, 'Invalid table');
    END IF;
    OPEN p_cursor FOR 'SELECT * FROM ' || p_table_name || ' WHERE ' || p_where;
END;
/

25. PL/SQL Collections

DECLARE
    -- 1. Associative Array (index by table) — like hash map
    TYPE t_name_by_id IS TABLE OF VARCHAR2(100) INDEX BY PLS_INTEGER;
    TYPE t_count_by_name IS TABLE OF NUMBER INDEX BY VARCHAR2(100);

    l_names    t_name_by_id;
    l_counts   t_count_by_name;

    -- 2. Nested Table — ordered list, can be column type in SQL
    TYPE t_number_list IS TABLE OF NUMBER;
    l_nums t_number_list := t_number_list(10, 20, 30, 40);

    -- 3. VARRAY — fixed max size
    TYPE t_top5 IS VARRAY(5) OF VARCHAR2(50);
    l_top5 t_top5 := t_top5('Gold', 'Silver', 'Bronze');

BEGIN
    -- Associative array usage
    l_names(100) := 'King';
    l_names(101) := 'Kochhar';

    -- Iterate
    DECLARE v_id PLS_INTEGER;
    BEGIN
        v_id := l_names.FIRST;
        WHILE v_id IS NOT NULL LOOP
            DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(v_id || ': ' || l_names(v_id));
            v_id := l_names.NEXT(v_id);
        END LOOP;
    END;

    -- Nested table methods
    l_nums.EXTEND(2);               -- Add 2 null slots
    l_nums.EXTEND(1, 3);            -- Add 1 copy of element 3
    l_nums.DELETE(2);               -- Delete element 2 (creates sparse)
    l_nums.DELETE(2, 4);            -- Delete elements 2 through 4
    l_nums.TRIM(2);                 -- Remove last 2 elements

    -- Collection attributes
    l_nums.COUNT    -- Number of elements
    l_nums.FIRST    -- First index
    l_nums.LAST     -- Last index
    l_nums.LIMIT    -- Max elements (VARRAY only)
    l_nums.EXISTS(3) -- TRUE if element 3 exists
    l_nums.NEXT(2)   -- Index after 2
    l_nums.PRIOR(3)  -- Index before 3
END;
/

-- Table of records — process result sets
DECLARE
    TYPE t_emp_tbl IS TABLE OF employees%ROWTYPE;
    l_emps t_emp_tbl;
BEGIN
    SELECT * BULK COLLECT INTO l_emps
    FROM   employees WHERE department_id = 90;

    FOR i IN 1..l_emps.COUNT LOOP
        DBMS_OUTPUT.PUT_LINE(l_emps(i).last_name || ': ' || l_emps(i).salary);
    END LOOP;
END;
/

26. Advanced Oracle Features

26.1 Autonomous Transactions

-- Runs in its own transaction — commits/rolls back independently of caller
CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE log_error (
    p_module  IN VARCHAR2,
    p_message IN VARCHAR2
) AS
    PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION;  -- Must be in DECLARE section
BEGIN
    INSERT INTO error_log (module, message, logged_at, logged_by)
    VALUES (p_module, p_message, SYSTIMESTAMP, SYS_CONTEXT('USERENV','SESSION_USER'));
    COMMIT;  -- Required — autonomous tx must commit or rollback
END;
/

-- Use case: log errors even when main transaction rolls back
BEGIN
    -- Main transaction
    INSERT INTO orders ...
    log_error('ORDER_PROC', 'Processing order');  -- This commits independently
    -- If this ROLLBACK happens, log entry still exists
    ROLLBACK;
END;
/

26.2 Pipelined Functions

-- Returns rows on-the-fly (like a streaming result set)
CREATE OR REPLACE TYPE t_emp_row AS OBJECT (
    employee_id  NUMBER,
    last_name    VARCHAR2(25),
    salary       NUMBER
);
/

CREATE OR REPLACE TYPE t_emp_table AS TABLE OF t_emp_row;
/

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_high_earners (p_threshold NUMBER)
RETURN t_emp_table PIPELINED AS
BEGIN
    FOR rec IN (SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
                FROM   employees WHERE salary > p_threshold)
    LOOP
        PIPE ROW (t_emp_row(rec.employee_id, rec.last_name, rec.salary));
    END LOOP;
    RETURN;   -- Must have RETURN but no value
END;
/

-- Use in SQL like a table
SELECT * FROM TABLE(get_high_earners(10000)) ORDER BY salary DESC;

-- Join with other tables
SELECT e.last_name, e.salary, d.department_name
FROM   TABLE(get_high_earners(10000)) e
       JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.department_id;

26.3 Result Cache

-- Function result cache — cache based on input parameters
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION get_dept_name (p_dept_id NUMBER)
RETURN VARCHAR2 RESULT_CACHE RELIES_ON (departments)
IS
    v_name departments.department_name%TYPE;
BEGIN
    SELECT department_name INTO v_name
    FROM   departments WHERE department_id = p_dept_id;
    RETURN v_name;
EXCEPTION
    WHEN NO_DATA_FOUND THEN RETURN 'Unknown';
END;
/

-- SQL Result Cache (via hint)
SELECT /*+ RESULT_CACHE */ department_id, COUNT(*), AVG(salary)
FROM   employees
GROUP BY department_id;

27. JSON & XML in Oracle

27.1 JSON (Oracle 12c+)

-- JSON column (VARCHAR2 or JSON type in 21c+)
CREATE TABLE json_orders (
    id    NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    data  CLOB CHECK (data IS JSON)
);

-- Oracle 21c+ native JSON type
CREATE TABLE json_orders_21 (
    id   NUMBER PRIMARY KEY,
    data JSON
);

-- JSON functions
SELECT JSON_OBJECT(
    'employee_id' VALUE employee_id,
    'name'        VALUE first_name || ' ' || last_name,
    'salary'      VALUE salary
    ABSENT ON NULL
) AS emp_json
FROM employees WHERE department_id = 90;

SELECT JSON_ARRAY(1, 'hello', NULL ABSENT ON NULL) FROM DUAL;

-- JSON_TABLE — parse JSON into relational format
SELECT jt.*
FROM   json_orders jo,
       JSON_TABLE(jo.data, '$'
           COLUMNS (
               order_id    NUMBER       PATH '$.id',
               customer    VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$.customer.name',
               total       NUMBER       PATH '$.total',
               NESTED PATH '$.items[*]'
                   COLUMNS (
                       item_name  VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$.name',
                       quantity   NUMBER       PATH '$.qty'
                   )
           )
       ) jt;

-- Dot notation (simplest)
SELECT jo.data.customer.name, jo.data.total
FROM   json_orders jo
WHERE  jo.data.status = 'ACTIVE';

-- JSON path expressions
SELECT json_value(data, '$.customer.name') AS cust_name,
       json_value(data, '$.total' RETURNING NUMBER) AS total,
       json_exists(data, '$.discount') AS has_discount
FROM   json_orders;

28. Security & Access Control

28.1 Users and Profiles

-- Create user
CREATE USER app_user IDENTIFIED BY "SecureP@ss123"
    DEFAULT TABLESPACE users
    TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp
    QUOTA 100M ON users
    ACCOUNT UNLOCK
    PASSWORD EXPIRE;       -- Force password change on first login

-- Password profile
CREATE PROFILE strong_pass LIMIT
    FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS   5
    PASSWORD_LOCK_TIME      1/24     -- 1 hour (fraction of day)
    PASSWORD_LIFE_TIME      90       -- Days
    PASSWORD_REUSE_TIME     365
    PASSWORD_REUSE_MAX      10
    PASSWORD_VERIFY_FUNCTION ora12c_strong_verify_function;

ALTER USER app_user PROFILE strong_pass;

-- Auditing (Oracle standard auditing)
AUDIT SELECT TABLE, INSERT TABLE, UPDATE TABLE BY app_user;
AUDIT CONNECT;
AUDIT CREATE SESSION BY ACCESS;    -- Log every connect

-- Unified Auditing (Oracle 12c+)
CREATE AUDIT POLICY emp_salary_policy
    ACTIONS SELECT, UPDATE ON hr.employees
    WHEN 'SYS_CONTEXT(''USERENV'',''SESSION_USER'') != ''HR'''
    EVALUATE PER SESSION;

AUDIT POLICY emp_salary_policy;

29. SQL Tuning & Optimization

29.1 SQL Tuning Checklist

✅ Query Analysis Checklist:

1. CHECK execution plan
   - Look for TABLE ACCESS FULL on large tables
   - Look for high cardinality estimates (should match actual)
   - Look for unexpected join methods
   - Check predicate information section

2. INDEXES
   - Is there an index on WHERE/JOIN columns?
   - Is the index being used? (not suppressed by function on column)
   - Is the index selective enough?
   - Would a composite index help?
   - Are stats up to date? (DBMS_STATS)

3. JOIN OPTIMIZATION
   - Drive from smallest result set (after filters applied)
   - Nested loop: good for small inner sets with index
   - Hash join: good for large sets, no sort order needed
   - Sort-merge: good when both sides sorted

4. BIND VARIABLES
   - Are literals being used instead of bind vars? (hard parse storm)

5. STATISTICS
   EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_TABLE_STATS('HR','EMPLOYEES',CASCADE=>TRUE);
   EXEC DBMS_STATS.GATHER_SCHEMA_STATS('HR');

6. SQL ACCESS ADVISOR
   EXEC DBMS_ADVISOR.QUICK_TUNE(DBMS_ADVISOR.SQLACCESS_ADVISOR,'task1',sql_text);

7. CHECK v$ views
   SELECT * FROM V$SQL WHERE sql_text LIKE '%employees%' ORDER BY elapsed_time DESC;
   SELECT * FROM V$SQL_PLAN WHERE sql_id = '...' ORDER BY id;

29.2 Common Performance Anti-Patterns

-- ❌ Function on indexed column (suppresses index)
WHERE UPPER(last_name) = 'SMITH'
-- ✅ Fix: Function-based index or store data in consistent case
CREATE INDEX idx_upper_name ON employees(UPPER(last_name));

-- ❌ Implicit type conversion (suppresses index)
WHERE employee_id = '100'          -- employee_id is NUMBER, '100' is VARCHAR2
-- ✅ Fix: use correct type
WHERE employee_id = 100

-- ❌ NOT NULL check on nullable column (no index on NULLs!)
WHERE commission_pct IS NOT NULL
-- Note: B-tree indexes don't store NULL values; IS NOT NULL may full-scan

-- ❌ OR on different indexed columns (can prevent index use)
WHERE department_id = 10 OR job_id = 'IT_PROG'
-- ✅ Fix: UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = 10
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE job_id = 'IT_PROG' AND department_id <> 10;

-- ❌ Wildcard at start (no index support)
WHERE last_name LIKE '%MITH'
-- ✅ Fix: Use Oracle Text or reverse key index

-- ❌ SELECT * — fetches unnecessary data, breaks view stability
SELECT * FROM employees
-- ✅ Fix: name only required columns

-- ❌ Correlated subquery executed for every row
SELECT e.*, (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE department_id = e.department_id)
FROM employees e
-- ✅ Fix: Window function
SELECT e.*, MAX(salary) OVER (PARTITION BY department_id) FROM employees e;

-- ❌ Using ROWNUM before ORDER BY (gets wrong rows)
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE ROWNUM <= 5 ORDER BY salary DESC;  -- Wrong!
-- ✅ Fix: inline view
SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC) WHERE ROWNUM <= 5;

29.3 Bind Variables

-- ❌ Literal values — causes hard parse each time
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 100;
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = 101;
-- Different SQL hash = 2 hard parses, 2 entries in shared pool

-- ✅ Bind variables — one hard parse, many soft parses
-- In PL/SQL (automatically uses bind variables)
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM employees WHERE employee_id = :1' USING v_emp_id;

-- Check for literals in shared pool (finds literal SQL)
SELECT sql_text, executions, parse_calls
FROM   v$sql
WHERE  parse_calls > executions * 0.5  -- Many hard parses
  AND  sql_text LIKE '%employees%'
ORDER BY parse_calls DESC;

30. Database Design Concepts

30.1 Normalization

1NF: Atomic values, no repeating groups
     - Each cell contains single value
     - Each row uniquely identifiable

2NF: 1NF + no partial dependencies
     - Every non-key column depends on ENTIRE primary key
     - Applies only to tables with composite PKs

3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependencies
     - Every non-key column depends ONLY on the primary key
     - No non-key column determines another non-key column

BCNF: 3NF + every determinant is a candidate key
      (handles some anomalies 3NF misses)

4NF: BCNF + no multi-valued dependencies
5NF: 4NF + no join dependencies

30.2 OLTP vs OLAP

Aspect OLTP OLAP / Data Warehouse
Purpose Transactional Analytical
Normalization Highly normalized (3NF+) Denormalized (Star/Snowflake)
Query type Short, frequent DML Long, complex SELECT
Data volume Current data Historical data
Indexing B-tree on PK/FK Bitmap, partitioned
Design ER model Dimensional model
Joins Few tables Fact + many dimensions
Example Order entry Sales analytics

31. Interview SQL Scenarios

31.1 Top-N Queries

-- Top 3 salaries per department (interview classic)
SELECT department_id, last_name, salary, dense_rnk
FROM (
    SELECT department_id, last_name, salary,
           DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY department_id ORDER BY salary DESC) dense_rnk
    FROM   employees
)
WHERE dense_rnk <= 3
ORDER BY department_id, dense_rnk;

-- 2nd highest salary overall
SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);
-- Better:
SELECT salary FROM (
    SELECT DISTINCT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) dr FROM employees
) WHERE dr = 2;

31.2 Duplicate Removal

-- Find duplicates
SELECT email, COUNT(*) FROM employees GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

-- Delete duplicates, keep one (lowest ROWID = physically first)
DELETE FROM employees
WHERE ROWID NOT IN (
    SELECT MIN(ROWID)
    FROM   employees
    GROUP BY email
);

-- Or with ROW_NUMBER (cleaner)
DELETE FROM employees WHERE ROWID IN (
    SELECT rid FROM (
        SELECT ROWID rid,
               ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY employee_id) rn
        FROM   employees
    ) WHERE rn > 1
);

31.3 Gaps and Islands

-- Find gaps in sequential IDs
SELECT a.employee_id + 1 AS gap_start,
       b.employee_id - 1 AS gap_end
FROM (SELECT employee_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY employee_id) rn FROM employees) a
JOIN (SELECT employee_id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY employee_id) rn FROM employees) b
  ON a.rn + 1 = b.rn
WHERE b.employee_id - a.employee_id > 1;

-- Find islands (consecutive groups)
SELECT MIN(employee_id) AS island_start, MAX(employee_id) AS island_end
FROM (
    SELECT employee_id,
           employee_id - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY employee_id) AS grp
    FROM   employees
)
GROUP BY grp
ORDER BY island_start;

31.4 Running Totals and Cumulative Calculations

-- Running total by date
SELECT order_date, amount,
       SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_total,
       AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS ma_7,
       amount - LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS day_over_day_change,
       ROUND((amount - LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date))
             / NULLIF(LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date), 0) * 100, 2) AS pct_change
FROM   daily_sales
ORDER BY order_date;

31.5 Pagination (Production Pattern)

-- ANSI standard — clean, readable (Oracle 12c+)
SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
OFFSET :page_offset ROWS FETCH NEXT :page_size ROWS ONLY;

-- Keyset pagination (avoids OFFSET performance degradation on large tables)
-- Page 1
SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
FROM   employees
ORDER BY salary DESC, employee_id DESC
FETCH FIRST :page_size ROWS ONLY;

-- Subsequent pages (using last seen values)
SELECT employee_id, last_name, salary
FROM   employees
WHERE  (salary, employee_id) < (:last_salary, :last_emp_id)  -- Keyset condition
ORDER BY salary DESC, employee_id DESC
FETCH FIRST :page_size ROWS ONLY;

31.6 MERGE (SCD Type 1 and Type 2)

-- SCD Type 1: Overwrite (MERGE for simple upsert)
MERGE INTO dim_customer tgt
USING staging_customers src ON (tgt.customer_id = src.customer_id)
WHEN MATCHED THEN
    UPDATE SET tgt.name    = src.name,
               tgt.email   = src.email,
               tgt.updated = SYSDATE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (customer_id, name, email, created, updated)
    VALUES (src.customer_id, src.name, src.email, SYSDATE, SYSDATE);

-- SCD Type 2: Keep history (expire old, insert new)
MERGE INTO dim_customer tgt
USING staging_customers src ON (tgt.customer_id = src.customer_id AND tgt.is_current = 'Y')
WHEN MATCHED AND (tgt.name <> src.name OR tgt.email <> src.email) THEN
    UPDATE SET tgt.is_current  = 'N',
               tgt.end_date    = SYSDATE
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
    INSERT (sk, customer_id, name, email, start_date, end_date, is_current)
    VALUES (dim_cust_seq.NEXTVAL, src.customer_id, src.name, src.email, SYSDATE, DATE '9999-12-31', 'Y');
-- Then insert new current rows for updated records
