  • Incremental Backup → Only changes since last backup; faster; must chain to full for restore.
  • Differential Backup → Changes since last full; faster restore than incremental; larger than incremental.
  • Logical Backup → SQL dump (pg_dump, mysqldump); portable; slow for large DBs; version-agnostic.
  • Physical Backup → Copy of data files (pg_basebackup, xtrabackup); fast; version-dependent.
  • WAL Archiving → Archive WAL segments continuously; enables point-in-time recovery.
  • Snapshots → Storage-level (EBS snapshot, filesystem snapshot); near-instant; consistent if DB quiesced.

Recovery

  • Restore → Recreate DB from backup; test regularly (untested backup = no backup).
  • Point-in-Time Recovery (PITR) → Restore to any moment using base backup + WAL archive; minimum RPO.
  • RTO (Recovery Time Objective) → Max acceptable downtime; drives HA architecture decisions.
  • RPO (Recovery Point Objective) → Max acceptable data loss; drives backup frequency + replication.
  • Disaster Recovery → Cross-region failover capability; regular DR drills required.
  • High Availability → Automatic failover with minimal downtime; Patroni, AWS Multi-AZ, always-on AG.
  • Geo-Redundant Backup → Backups stored in different region; protects against regional outage.
  • Backup Verification → Automated restore test; validate checksum; "backup that's never restored is untested".
  • Backup Encryption → Encrypt backups at rest; manage keys separately from data.
  • Keywords: RPO drives backup frequency; RTO drives HA tier; always test restoration; PITR requires continuous WAL archiving.

29. Observability & Database Monitoring

Key Metrics

  • Cache Hit Ratio → shared_buffers hits / total reads; target >99% for OLTP; low = add RAM or tune.
  • Replication Lag → Bytes or seconds behind primary; alert if growing; causes stale reads on replica.
  • Connection Count → Active + idle + idle-in-transaction; alert when approaching max_connections.
  • Lock Wait Time → Time transactions spend waiting for locks; high value = contention problem.
  • Deadlock Rate → Deadlocks per second; investigate application lock ordering.
  • Bloat → Dead tuple accumulation in tables/indexes; causes I/O waste; indicates autovacuum issues.
  • Autovacuum Activity → Track tables with high dead tuple counts; tune autovacuum thresholds.
  • Slow Queries → Queries exceeding threshold; first thing to check for performance issues.
  • Wait Events → What queries are waiting on (I/O, CPU, Lock, LWLock); PostgreSQL pg_stat_activity.
  • Disk Usage Trends → Monitor growth rate; alert before full; include WAL and temp space.

Diagnostic Tools

  • EXPLAIN / EXPLAIN ANALYZE → Query plan inspection; most important DB performance tool.
  • pg_stat_statements → Aggregate stats per query fingerprint; find top CPU/IO-consuming queries.
  • pg_stat_user_indexes → Track index usage; find unused indexes (drop them — write overhead for free).
  • pg_stat_activity → Live view of running queries; identify long-running transactions.
  • Query Performance Insight → AWS RDS/Aurora managed tool; top queries + wait events dashboard.
  • pgBadger → PostgreSQL log analyzer; generates HTML reports from slow query logs.
  • Slow Query Log → MySQL/PostgreSQL log queries over threshold; essential for production tuning.
  • Percona Monitoring and Management (PMM) → Open-source MySQL/PostgreSQL monitoring stack.

Alerting

  • Alert on: Replication lag > threshold, connections > 80% max, long-running transactions > N minutes, lock waits spiking, dead tuple ratio > threshold, disk > 80% full, cache hit ratio < 95%.
  • Plan Regression → Query plan changes after statistics update or upgrade; compare EXPLAIN output over time.
  • Keywords: Monitoring without alerting is useless; EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the first tool for any slow query; pg_stat_statements reveals hidden query patterns.

30. Security

Authentication & Authorization

  • Authentication → Verify identity; password, certificate, IAM role, Kerberos, LDAP.
  • Authorization → Control what authenticated user can do; GRANT/REVOKE; roles.
  • RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) → Permissions assigned to roles; users assigned to roles.
  • ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) → Permissions based on attributes (department, clearance level).
  • Principle of Least Privilege → Grant minimum permissions needed; service accounts should have minimal rights.
  • Row-Level Security (RLS) → Policies filter rows per user/role; CREATE POLICY in PostgreSQL; tenant isolation.
  • Column-Level Security → GRANT SELECT on specific columns only; hide sensitive columns.
  • Dynamic Data Masking → Show masked values (e.g. XXXX-1234) to low-privilege users; actual data unchanged.

Encryption

  • Encryption at Rest → Encrypt data files, backups, WAL; AES-256; transparent DB encryption (TDE).
  • Encryption in Transit → TLS/SSL for client-DB connections; enforce sslmode=require.
  • Key Management → Rotate encryption keys; use KMS (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault); never hard-code keys.

Hardening

  • SQL Injection Prevention → Always use parameterized queries / prepared statements; never concatenate user input.
  • Secrets Management → Store credentials in Vault, AWS Secrets Manager; rotate automatically.
  • Auditing → Log who accessed/modified what; pgaudit extension; required for compliance.
  • Privileged Access Management (PAM) → Control and audit DBA access; just-in-time elevation.
  • Network Isolation → DB not publicly accessible; VPC/private subnet; firewall rules; security groups.
  • Keywords: Parameterized queries are non-negotiable; RLS is the right way to enforce tenant isolation at DB level.

31. Compliance & Data Regulations

Major Regulations

  • GDPR → EU; protects personal data of EU residents; consent required; right to erasure; data portability; fines up to 4% of global revenue.
  • CCPA → California; similar to GDPR for California residents; opt-out of data sale.
  • HIPAA → US healthcare; Protected Health Information (PHI); encryption required; audit logs required.
  • PCI-DSS → Payment card data; encrypt cardholder data; restrict access; quarterly scans.
  • SOC 2 → Security/availability/processing integrity/confidentiality/privacy controls; audit report.

Data Handling

  • PII (Personally Identifiable Information) → Name, email, SSN, IP address; special handling required.
  • Data Classification → Public / Internal / Confidential / Restricted; drives controls applied.
  • Anonymization → Remove all identifying info; irreversible; GDPR-compliant.
  • Pseudonymization → Replace identifiers with tokens; reversible; still personal data under GDPR.
  • Right to Erasure (Right to be Forgotten) → Delete all personal data on request; complex with backups + event stores.
  • Data Retention Policy → Define and enforce max storage duration; auto-purge after expiry.
  • Data Residency / Sovereignty → Data must stay within a country/region; affects DB region choice.
  • Consent Management → Track user consent; only process data with valid consent.
  • Audit Trail → Immutable log of data access and changes; required by HIPAA, PCI-DSS.
  • Cross-Border Transfer → GDPR restricts EU data transfers to non-adequate countries; Standard Contractual Clauses needed.
  • Keywords: Anonymization is the only way to fully escape GDPR scope; pseudonymization is not enough; right to erasure is hardest in event-sourced systems.

32. Cloud Databases

Managed Services

  • Managed Database → Cloud provider handles patching, backups, HA, scaling; trade control for convenience.
  • Amazon RDS → Managed MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQL Server, Oracle; Multi-AZ for HA; Read Replicas for scale.
  • Amazon Aurora → MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible; disaggregated storage; 6-way replication across 3 AZs; faster failover.
  • Google Cloud Spanner → Globally distributed; strong consistency; horizontal scaling; TrueTime for external consistency.
  • Google AlloyDB → PostgreSQL-compatible; columnar engine for analytics; ML-accelerated.
  • Azure Cosmos DB → Multi-model; globally distributed; 5 consistency levels; turnkey multi-region writes.
  • Cloud SQL → Managed MySQL/PostgreSQL/SQL Server on GCP.
  • Neon → Serverless PostgreSQL; scale-to-zero; branching (Git-like DB branching for dev/test).
  • PlanetScale → Serverless MySQL; Vitess-based; schema branching; horizontal sharding.

Cloud Patterns

  • Multi-AZ Deployment → Synchronous standby in another AZ; automatic failover; ~1 min RTO.
  • Auto Scaling → Aurora Serverless scales compute automatically; DynamoDB on-demand mode.
  • Read Endpoint / Write Endpoint → Route reads to replica, writes to primary via proxy DNS.
  • Database Proxy → RDS Proxy; pools connections; reduces failover time; important for Lambda workloads.
  • IOPS Provisioning → Allocate dedicated I/O throughput; burst vs provisioned; gp3 vs io1/io2 on AWS.
  • Maintenance Windows → Scheduled patching window; configure for low-traffic period.
  • Cost Optimization → Right-size instances; use reserved instances (1-3yr); S3-backed cold storage; delete unused snapshots.
  • Keywords: Aurora's storage is separate from compute — scale independently; RDS Proxy is essential for serverless DB connections; Multi-AZ ≠ Multi-Region.

33. Database Tools & Ecosystem

Migration & Transformation

  • Flyway → Version-controlled SQL migrations; simple; SQL-first.
  • Liquibase → XML/YAML/SQL changesets; diff-based; more features.
  • dbt → SQL transformation layer on DWH; modular models; lineage; tests; documentation.
  • Apache Airflow → Workflow orchestration; DAG-based ETL pipelines.
  • Great Expectations → Data quality framework; validate data at pipeline checkpoints.

Connectivity & Pooling

  • PgBouncer → Lightweight PostgreSQL connection pooler; transaction mode recommended.
  • ProxySQL → MySQL proxy + pooler + router; query caching; read/write splitting.
  • Vitess → MySQL horizontal sharding middleware; used by YouTube, Slack.
  • Citus → PostgreSQL extension for horizontal sharding; distributed SQL.

Administration & Monitoring

  • DBeaver → Universal DB GUI; open-source; supports 80+ DBs.
  • DataGrip → JetBrains DB IDE; smart SQL completion; schema migration.
  • pgAdmin → PostgreSQL-specific web UI; built-in EXPLAIN visualizer.
  • Percona Toolkit → MySQL/PostgreSQL command-line tools; pt-osc, pt-query-digest.
  • pgBadger → PostgreSQL log analyzer; slow query report.

Data Discovery & Governance

  • Apache Atlas → Metadata management + governance; lineage tracking.
  • DataHub → LinkedIn open-source data catalog; search + lineage + ownership.
  • Amundsen → Lyft data discovery; metadata catalog; popularity ranking.
  • Data Lineage → Track data from source to destination; critical for compliance + debugging.
  • Data Catalog → Centralized inventory of data assets; searchable; owned.

34. Anti-Patterns

Query Anti-Patterns

  • N+1 Query Problem → Loop in app code fetching one related row per iteration; use JOIN or batch fetch instead.
  • SELECT * → Fetches unused columns; breaks covering indexes; fragile with schema changes.
  • Full Table Scan Abuse → Query without appropriate index; no WHERE or low-selectivity WHERE.
  • Unbounded Queries → No LIMIT; one bad query returns millions of rows and crashes app.
  • Correlated Subquery in SELECT → Re-executes per row; use JOIN or window function instead.
  • Implicit Type Cast → WHERE created_at = '2024-01-01' when column is TIMESTAMP; breaks index use.
  • ORM-Generated Inefficient Queries → Lazy loading, findAll() without pagination; inspect generated SQL.

Schema Anti-Patterns

  • Missing Index → FK columns without index; foreign key lookups cause full scan.
  • Over-Normalization → Too many joins for simple queries; denormalize hot paths.
  • Under-Normalization → Redundant data; update anomalies; inconsistency.
  • EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) → Flexible but un-queryable at scale; use JSONB instead.
  • Storing BLOBs in DB → Images/files in DB; use object storage (S3) + store reference URL.
  • Magic Numbers / Untyped Columns → status = 1; use enum or lookup table.
  • Growing Arrays → Unbounded array columns or subdocuments; causes document/row bloat.
  • Soft Delete Without Index → WHERE deleted_at IS NULL full scan; add partial index.

Architecture Anti-Patterns

  • DB as Message Queue → Polling a table for work; doesn't scale; use Kafka/SQS instead.
  • Shared DB Across Microservices → Tight coupling; shared schema = deployment dependency.
  • Hot Partition → Sequential shard key causes all writes to one node; add entropy to key.
  • Single Point of Failure → No replica, no HA; one crash = total downtime.
  • Long-Running Transactions → Hold locks; bloat MVCC; block autovacuum; kill proactively.
  • Ignoring Query Plan Regressions → Plan changes after upgrade/stats update; monitor with query store.
  • Keywords: N+1 is the most common ORM problem; never allow unbounded queries in production; shared DB is the microservices anti-pattern that kills independence.

35. Advanced / Staff+ Level Topics

Global Scale

  • Multi-Tenant Database → Shared schema (row-level isolation via tenant_id), shared DB (schema per tenant), or dedicated DB per tenant; each has cost/isolation trade-offs.
  • Global Replication → Multi-region active-active or active-passive; latency vs consistency trade-off.
  • Data Residency → Store EU data in EU; routing layer enforces data geography.
  • Active-Active → Both regions serve reads + writes; conflict resolution required; hardest consistency.
  • Active-Passive → One region active, one hot standby; simpler; 30–60s failover typical.
  • Conflict Resolution → Last-write-wins (LWW), vector clocks, application-level merge; must be defined for multi-master.
  • Spanner TrueTime → Google's globally synchronized clock using GPS+atomic clocks; enables external consistency with bounded uncertainty.

Emerging Architectures

  • Data Mesh → Domain-owned data products; federated governance; decentralized; each domain owns its pipeline.
  • Lakehouse → Data lake + warehouse features; open formats (Parquet/ORC) + ACID transactions (Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi).
  • Disaggregated Storage → Compute and storage separated; Aurora, Socrates (Azure SQL), AlloyDB; scale each independently.
  • HTAP Systems → Single DB for OLTP + OLAP; TiDB, SingleStore; eliminates ETL lag.
  • Tiered Storage → Hot (SSD/NVMe), warm (HDD), cold (S3/GCS/Blob); auto-tiering by age/access frequency.
  • Serverless Databases → Scale to zero; instant start; pay per request; Neon, Aurora Serverless v2, PlanetScale.

Advanced Internals

  • CockroachDB → Distributed SQL; Raft per range; serializable isolation; auto-rebalancing; Postgres-compatible.
  • YugabyteDB → PostgreSQL protocol + Cassandra API; Raft-based; multi-master OLTP.
  • FoundationDB → Ordered KV store; ACID; used as metadata layer (Snowflake, FoundationDB Record Layer).
  • Federated Query → Query across multiple heterogeneous DBs as if one (AWS Athena Federated, Trino, Presto).
  • Chaos Engineering for Databases → Inject failures (kill primary, corrupt network); validate HA + recovery procedures.
  • Database SLOs → Define and measure: p99 query latency, replication lag, availability %; tie to error budgets.
  • Cost Modeling → IOPS cost × volume + storage tier cost + data transfer; optimize query patterns to reduce cost.

36. Interview Master Keywords

Trade-off Language

  • Read vs Write Optimization → Index-heavy = fast reads, slow writes; denormalized = fast reads, complex writes.
  • Consistency vs Availability → CAP: choose CP (HBase, Spanner) or AP (Cassandra, DynamoDB).
  • Latency vs Throughput → Low latency needs dedicated IOPS; high throughput needs batching + parallelism.
  • Normalization vs Denormalization → Normalize to remove anomalies; denormalize for query speed.
  • Push vs Pull Replication → Push: primary sends; Pull: replica fetches; WAL streaming is push.
  • Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication → Sync = zero data loss, higher latency; Async = lower latency, possible data loss.
  • Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking → Optimistic = low contention, retry on conflict; Pessimistic = high contention, hold lock.
  • Fan-out on Write vs Read → Write: precompute feed (fast read, storage cost); Read: compute at query time (slow read, storage efficient).

Decision Frameworks

  • Index Trade-offs → Every index speeds reads but slows writes; remove unused indexes.
  • Partition Strategy → Range for time-series; hash for even distribution; list for known categories.
  • Scaling Strategy → Read scale first (replicas); then write scale (sharding); vertical before horizontal.
  • When to Denormalize → After profiling shows join cost is dominant; never as a first step.
  • When to use NoSQL → Schema flexibility, extreme scale, specific access patterns (graph, time-series, document).
  • Idempotency → Design DB operations to be safely retried; use unique constraint or version column.
  • Backpressure → Signal upstream when DB is overwhelmed; connection pool exhaustion = natural backpressure.
  • Hot vs Cold Data Tiering → Move aged data to cheaper storage; query routing separates hot/cold access.

One-Liners for Interviews

  • "ACID is a spectrum controlled by isolation level — higher isolation = lower concurrency"
  • "CAP theorem says pick 2, but partition tolerance is mandatory — really choose CP or AP"
  • "Indexes are free for reads but never free for writes — always measure the write overhead"
  • "Replication lag is the gap between your consistency promise and reality"
  • "Autovacuum is your friend — a database without working autovacuum will eventually fall over"
  • "The N+1 problem is ORM's original sin — always check generated SQL"
  • "Schema migration is a product risk, not just a dev task — expand-contract saves your deployment"
  • "Eventual consistency is a spectrum — define how eventual before accepting it"
  • "Connection pooling is mandatory at scale — raw connections are expensive and limited"
  • "Right tool for the right job: RDBMS for integrity, Kafka for events, Redis for speed, graph DB for relationships"

Cloud Engineering

1. Cloud Fundamentals

Cloud computing concepts

The overarching idea: delivering on-demand IT resources over the internet instead of relying solely on local hardware. It rests on five essential characteristics defined by NIST—on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. Understanding these fundamentals underpins everything else in the list.

Evolution of cloud computing

The concept didn’t appear overnight.

  • 1950s–60s: Mainframe computing with time-sharing allowed multiple users to share a single large machine’s resources.
  • 1970s: Virtual machines emerged (IBM VM/370), letting one physical host run multiple isolated operating systems.
  • 1990s: Application Service Providers (ASPs) hosted third-party applications, but mostly in a single-tenant, non-elastic fashion. Telecommunications companies began offering virtual private networks, coining the “cloud” symbol for the network boundary.
  • Early 2000s: Grid computing and utility computing proposed pay-per-use IT resources. Amazon internally developed a service-oriented architecture that led to the launch of Amazon Web Services (EC2/S3) in 2006, making IaaS broadly available.
  • 2010s: PaaS and containerisation (Docker, Kubernetes) flourished; serverless computing (FaaS) abstracted infrastructure entirely.
  • Today: Edge computing, AI/ML as a service, sustainability mandates, and FinOps practices shape the next generation of cloud evolution.

IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS

These are the three classic service categories, defined by who manages what:

  • IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service): Gives you virtual machines, storage, and networking on demand. You control the OS, middleware, runtime, and applications. The provider manages the physical hardware, hypervisor, and network fabric. Example: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines.
  • PaaS (Platform as a Service): Provides a managed runtime environment. You supply the application code and data; the provider handles OS patches, scaling, and load balancing. This dramatically reduces operational work but limits low-level control. Example: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service.
  • SaaS (Software as a Service): Delivers a complete, ready-to-use application over the internet. You only configure user settings and data, with no visibility into the underlying infrastructure. Example: Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Slack.

The key trade-off is control versus convenience: IaaS offers maximum flexibility, SaaS minimum effort.

FaaS and BaaS

These are more granular service abstractions often grouped as “serverless”:

  • FaaS (Function as a Service): Run a single piece of logic (a function) in response to an event. You upload the code, and the platform manages the runtime, scaling to zero when idle, and scaling out automatically under load. Billing is per invocation and execution duration (sub-second granularity). Examples: AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions.
  • BaaS (Backend as a Service): Offload backend capabilities—databases, authentication, push notifications, file storage—to a managed API. This is particularly common in mobile and web app development, where the frontend can directly consume services without building a custom backend. Examples: Firebase (Firestore, Auth), Auth0, Supabase.

Both reduce server management, but FaaS focuses on custom logic execution, while BaaS replaces entire backend components.

Public vs private vs hybrid cloud

These are the three core deployment models:

  • Public cloud: Resources are owned and operated by a third-party provider and shared across multiple tenants. You access services via the internet, paying only for what you use. Strengths: scalability, cost-efficiency, no upfront capital.
  • Private cloud: Infrastructure is dedicated to a single organisation. It can be hosted on-premises or by a third party, but the hardware is not shared. Strengths: greater control, customisation, and compliance for sensitive workloads.
  • Hybrid cloud: A combination of public and private environments that are connected (via VPN, Direct Connect) and orchestrated as a unified infrastructure. This allows data and applications to move between them based on cost, compliance, or performance requirements.

Community cloud

A deployment model where infrastructure is shared among several organisations that have shared concerns—regulatory compliance, security requirements, mission objectives, or policy. It can be managed internally or by a provider. A classic example is a group of healthcare organisations or government agencies sharing a cloud that meets their specific legal frameworks (e.g., HIPAA, FedRAMP). Costs are divided, offering a middle ground between the exclusivity of private cloud and the lower cost of public cloud.

Multi-cloud strategies

Deliberately using two or more public cloud providers (e.g., AWS and Azure) to avoid single-vendor dependency, boost resilience, or take advantage of best-of-breed services. Strategies include:

  • Redundant multi-cloud: Run identical workloads on two clouds for high availability.
  • Distributed multi-cloud: Place different application components on different clouds (e.g., data analytics on GCP, customer-facing app on AWS) based on native service strengths.
  • Avoidance of lock-in: Use open-source and portable technologies (Kubernetes, Terraform) to switch providers if needed.
    Challenges include increased operational complexity, inconsistent security postures, and egress costs.

Cloud service models

While “IaaS, PaaS, SaaS” are the well-known ones, the full spectrum of cloud service models includes everything from the physical facility (Colocation) up to full software. The NIST definition of service models is broader: you can also have Compute as a Service, Database as a Service (DBaaS), Desktop as a Service (DaaS), Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS), etc. The core idea is that each model abstracts a different layer of the stack, and understanding which layer you want to manage determines the model you choose.

Cloud deployment models

A framework for where and how cloud resources are physically provided and consumed. The main models—public, private, hybrid, community—describe the ownership, location, and tenancy of infrastructure. More recent variations include dedicated cloud (single-tenant public cloud hardware) and sovereign cloud (public cloud restricted to a specific country’s legal jurisdiction). Deployment model choices affect security boundaries, latency, data residency, and cost structure.

Shared responsibility model

A crucial security and compliance framework delineating exactly which controls the cloud provider is responsible for and which are the customer’s. The line moves based on service model:

  • IaaS: Customer responsible for OS, applications, data, identity; provider for physical and network infrastructure.
  • PaaS: Customer responsible for application code and data; provider for OS, runtime, and infrastructure.
  • SaaS: Customer primarily responsible for data, access management; provider handles everything else.
    Ignoring this model often leads to misconfigured resources (e.g., open storage buckets), the #1 cause of cloud breaches.

Cloud economics

A discipline examining the financial impact of cloud adoption. It moves IT spending from CapEx (buying hardware upfront) to OpEx (paying for consumption). Key concepts:

  • Total cost of ownership (TCO): Includes not just infrastructure cost but also operational overhead, licensing, and migration.
  • Return on investment (ROI): Business value gained (agility, innovation speed) versus cost.
  • Unit economics: Understanding cost per transaction/customer, enabling precise optimisation.
    Cloud economics also covers value levers like elasticity, time-to-market, and avoiding over-provisioning.

Pay-as-you-go pricing

The foundational billing mechanism where you pay only for the capacity you actually use, with no minimums or upfront commitments. Compute may be charged per second or per hour; storage per GB-month; serverless functions per invocation and execution time. This model ties cost directly to demand, but without proper governance it can lead to unpredictable bills if resources are left running idle.

Reserved and spot pricing models

Beyond on-demand, providers offer discount models:

  • Reserved Instances (or Savings Plans): Commit to 1–3 years of consistent usage (e.g., a specific instance family, or a dollar-per-hour spend) in exchange for 30%–75% discount over on-demand. Useful for stable production workloads.
  • Spot Instances: Purchase unused excess capacity at up to 90% discount, but the provider can reclaim the resource on short notice (often 2 minutes). Ideal for stateless, fault-tolerant, batch, or containerised workloads that can handle interruptions.
    Combining these models with on-demand gives a highly cost-optimised architecture.

Elasticity and scalability

Both relate to handling changing load, but they’re distinct:

  • Elasticity is the ability of a system to automatically match resource allocation to current demand in real time—adding resources when load spikes and removing them when it drops. It’s a short-term, dynamic behaviour often powered by auto-scaling groups.
  • Scalability is the system’s capacity to handle growth over time. A scalable system can be expanded (manually or automatically) to accommodate increased base load without architectural changes.

Elasticity solves the “bursty” problem; scalability ensures long-term viability.

Horizontal vs vertical scaling

  • Horizontal scaling (scaling out/in): Adding more instances (e.g., going from 3 web servers to 6). This approach is inherently cloud-friendly, supports distribution, and can scale almost infinitely if the application is stateless. It requires a load balancer and often a shared-nothing architecture.
  • Vertical scaling (scaling up/down): Increasing the size of a single instance (more vCPUs, RAM). Simpler because no code changes are needed, but it has a physical ceiling (the largest VM size) and creates a single point of failure. Often used for legacy or stateful applications that can’t be easily distributed.

High availability concepts

High availability (HA) is designing a system to remain operational even when individual components fail. It’s measured as a percentage of uptime (99.9% = ~8.76 hours downtime/year; 99.999% = ~5 minutes). HA is achieved by:

  • Eliminating single points of failure (redundant components).
  • Automated failover (using health checks, floating IPs).
  • Geographic redundancy across availability zones or regions.
    HA focuses on minimising downtime, not preventing all failures, so it’s tightly linked with detection and recovery speed.

Fault tolerance

The next level beyond high availability: a fault-tolerant system continues to function correctly, with no service interruption, even when a component fails. This usually requires active-active redundant components running in lockstep (e.g., mirrored databases, RAID storage, twin engines on an aircraft). In the cloud, a truly fault-tolerant setup might involve a multi-AZ active-active compute layer with synchronous data replication. It’s more expensive than HA because it requires fully duplicated hardware/software capacity with zero failover time.

Disaster recovery fundamentals

Disaster recovery (DR) is the set of policies, tools, and procedures to recover critical IT systems after a major disruptive event (natural disaster, cyberattack). DR plans define how to restore operations to a secondary site. Cloud brings DR within reach for smaller organisations via:

  • Cold, warm, or hot standby sites in a different region.
  • Continuous data replication and automated recovery runbooks. Regular testing (fire drills) is mandatory; an untested DR plan is no plan.

Backup and restore strategies

The simplest form of data protection. Key decisions:

  • Frequency: How often backups are taken (RPO influence).
  • Retention: How long backups are kept and how many versions.
  • Type: Full, incremental, differential, or snapshot-based.
  • Storage: Immutability (WORM) to defend against ransomware; cross-region replication for disaster isolation.
  • Testing: Regularly restore backups to verify integrity; automation helps.

RPO and RTO concepts

Two key metrics that drive disaster recovery design:

  • RPO (Recovery Point Objective): The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time. If RPO is 1 hour, you must back up (or replicate) at least every hour. Smaller RPOs require more frequent synchronisation (e.g., continuous replication).
  • RTO (Recovery Time Objective): The maximum acceptable time to restore operations after an outage. A short RTO demands automated failover and pre-provisioned resources; a longer RTO might allow manual recovery from cold backups.
    Balancing RPO/RTO with cost is a core business decision.

Regions and availability zones

  • Region: A completely independent geographic area containing multiple, isolated data centres. Regions exist to minimise latency for users and meet data residency requirements.
  • Availability Zone (AZ): Within a region, AZs are one or more physically separate data centres with independent power, cooling, and networking, connected by high-speed, low-latency redundant fibre. Placing resources across AZs protects against single-data-centre failures.
    Most HA architectures are built by deploying across AZs within a region.

Edge locations

Points of presence (PoPs) distributed globally that sit outside the main cloud regions. Their primary purpose is to cache content and terminate user connections closer to end users, reducing latency. Commonly used for Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) like Amazon CloudFront, Azure Front Door, and Cloudflare. Edge locations also host DNS services, Web Application Firewalls (WAF), and serverless edge compute (e.g., CloudFront Functions).

Cloud SLAs

Service Level Agreements are contractual commitments made by the provider, typically guaranteeing a certain monthly uptime percentage for a given service (e.g., 99.9% for single-instance VMs, 99.99% for multi-AZ database). If the service falls below the SLA, the customer receives service credits (financial compensation). Crucially, SLAs define remedies, not prevention. They also specify exclusions (e.g., customer-caused outages, planned maintenance) and require the customer to implement certain best practices (multi-AZ deployment) to qualify.

Cloud compliance basics

Cloud providers operate under a shared responsibility model for compliance. The provider achieves certifications like ISO 27001, SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, HIPAA, FedRAMP, GDPR, etc., and makes reports available. However, the customer must architect their applications and configure services to be compliant. Key customer tasks: data encryption, access controls, audit logging, region selection (data residency), and building a compliant IAM structure. Providers offer compliance dashboards and guardrails to help.

Cloud governance

A framework of rules, roles, and processes that ensure cloud usage aligns with business objectives, risk appetite, and regulatory requirements. Governance includes:

  • Financial controls (budgets, spending caps).
  • Security policies (encryption required, public access blocked).
  • Operational consistency (approved services, naming conventions).
    Governance is enforced through a combination of policy-as-code, centralised logging, and periodic audits, often automated via the cloud’s management plane.

Cloud policy management

The mechanism for defining and enforcing governance rules programmatically. Policies are typically declarative statements (e.g., “Deny creation of resources in unapproved regions”). Tools like AWS Organizations SCPs, Azure Policy, and Open Policy Agent allow you to create a hierarchy of policies that cascade down to accounts/subscriptions. Policy evaluation happens at API call time, blocking non-compliant actions before they occur (preventive guardrails) or detecting and remediating them afterward (detective/reactive).

Cloud tagging strategies

Tags are key-value metadata attached to cloud resources (e.g., Environment: Production, CostCenter: 1234). A well-planned tagging strategy enables:

  • Cost allocation: Group costs by tag in billing dashboards.
  • Automation: Scripts and policies filter resources by tags (e.g., backup all Backup:Daily volumes).
  • Access control: ABAC policies grant/deny based on tags.
  • Operations: Identify owners for incident response.
    The strategy must define mandatory tags, allowed values, and lifecycle enforcement; otherwise, inconsistent tags become useless noise.

FinOps fundamentals

Financial Operations (FinOps) is a cultural practice that brings finance, technology, and business together to maximise cloud value. It follows an iterative lifecycle:

  1. Inform: Gain visibility into spending (tagging, cost dashboards).
  2. Optimise: Take action—rightsizing, reservations, scheduling, deleting waste.
  3. Operate: Continuously monitor and adjust, building cost-awareness into engineering processes.
    FinOps treats cloud spending as a variable cost to be managed in real-time, not an annual budget line.

Cloud adoption frameworks

Structured guidance provided by cloud providers and industry bodies to help organisations plan and execute cloud migration. Examples:

  • AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF): Perspectives—Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, Operations.
  • Microsoft Azure CAF: Stages—Strategy, Plan, Ready, Adopt, Govern, Manage.
    Frameworks guide capability assessment, cloud readiness, and iterative migration waves, reducing the risk of ad-hoc, unsuccessful cloud journeys.

Cloud migration strategies

The classic “7 Rs” model describes options for moving workloads to the cloud:

  1. Retire – Decommission applications that are no longer needed.
  2. Retain – Keep on-premises and revisit later.
  3. Rehost – Lift-and-shift without change.
  4. Replatform – Make slight adjustments (e.g., move to managed database).
  5. Refactor – Rewrite/re-architect to be cloud-native.
  6. Repurchase – Move to a SaaS product (e.g., CRM to Salesforce).
  7. Relocate – Move an entire hypervisor cluster or bare-metal to cloud.

Each balances speed vs. optimisation.

Lift-and-shift vs refactor

  • Lift-and-shift (rehost): Migrate applications to cloud VMs exactly as they run today, with no code or architecture changes. Fastest path to exit data centres, but may run inefficiently and miss cloud-native benefits.
  • Refactor (re-architect): Significantly alter the application to use cloud-native services—breaking a monolith into microservices, moving to serverless, or using managed containers. High upfront investment but yields the best scalability, resilience, and cost optimisation.
    They represent opposite ends of the migration spectrum.

Replatforming strategies

Sitting between lift-and-shift and refactor, replatforming makes intentional but minimal changes. For example:

  • Moving from self-hosted PostgreSQL to a managed cloud database service (e.g., Amazon RDS) to reduce administrative overhead.
  • Switching from on-premises message queue to a fully managed cloud queue service.
    The application core remains intact; only a few underlying components are swapped out. Replatforming provides a quick win in operational efficiency without the risk of a full refactor.

Cloud modernization

An ongoing, iterative process of updating applications, infrastructure, and processes to leverage cloud-native capabilities after initial migration. Modernisation can involve adopting containers, serverless, event-driven architectures, CI/CD pipelines, and observability tools. It’s not a one-time project but a continuous improvement loop, often prioritising the systems that deliver the highest business value.

Vendor lock-in considerations

Vendor lock-in is the degree to which a customer becomes dependent on a single cloud provider’s proprietary services, making migration difficult or costly. Factors that increase lock-in: using highly specialised, non-standard services (e.g., AWS DynamoDB’s specific API, Google BigQuery), proprietary SDKs, and data egress fees. Mitigation strategies include using open-source (Kubernetes, PostgreSQL), multicloud tooling (Terraform), and abstraction layers. Lock-in is a business risk that must be weighed against the innovation speed of proprietary services.

Cloud interoperability

The ability of services and tools from different cloud environments to work together seamlessly, exchanging data and triggering actions. Interoperability can be achieved via open standards (REST APIs, OAuth 2.0, OpenTelemetry) and middleware. For example, a workload on AWS might read from a GCP BigQuery dataset, or monitoring tools can ingest logs from multiple clouds. Interoperability is the enabler of genuine multi-cloud operations.

Cloud portability

The ease with which an application or its data can be moved from one cloud provider to another (or back on-premises) with minimal modification. Portability requires avoiding proprietary dependencies—using containers (Docker images), standard SQL databases, and infrastructure-as-code that supports multiple clouds. Note that true portability often sacrifices the advantages of provider-specific managed services, creating a trade-off between portability and productivity.

Cloud security fundamentals

A layered defence-in-depth approach tailored to the cloud’s distributed nature. Core pillars:

  • Identity-centric security: IAM is the new perimeter, not network firewalls.
  • Data protection: Encryption at rest (server-side/client-side) and in transit (TLS).
  • Network controls: Security groups, network ACLs, private subnets, WAF.
  • Threat detection: Continuous monitoring and anomaly detection (e.g., Amazon GuardDuty).
  • Compliance & governance: Automated policy enforcement.
    Security is a continuous process, not a product, following “secure by design” principles.

Identity and access management basics

IAM is the control plane for cloud security, answering “who can access what?” It consists of:

  • Authentication: Proving identity (passwords, MFA, federation, API keys).
  • Authorization: Determining what actions an authenticated principal can take, expressed via policies.
    Cloud IAM systems allow extremely fine-grained policies (e.g., “Allow this user to read objects in this specific bucket from this IP range”). The principle of least privilege is paramount: grant only the permissions needed for a task.

Role-based access control (RBAC)

A method of assigning permissions not directly to individual users, but to roles that represent a job function (e.g., “Database Administrator”, “Billing Viewer”). Users then inherit the role’s permissions. RBAC simplifies management at scale because you assign a role once instead of configuring permissions for each user. Changes to the role’s permissions automatically apply to all members.

Attribute-based access control (ABAC)

A more dynamic access control model where permissions depend on attributes of the principal, resource, action, and environment. For example: “Allow an S3 PutObject action only if the user’s department tag matches the bucket’s department tag”. ABAC enables highly scalable, context-aware policies without creating thousands of static roles. It relies on consistent tagging and attribute management to function.

Cloud networking fundamentals

The virtual equivalent of traditional networking, delivered as software-defined services. You create isolated virtual networks (VPC/VNet) with configurable IP ranges, subnets, route tables, and gateways. Core components include load balancers, firewalls (security groups/ACLs), NAT gateways, and peering connections. Cloud networking is fully programmable, so entire network topologies can be built through code.

DNS fundamentals

The Domain Name System translates human-readable domain names (www.example.com) into machine-routable IP addresses. Cloud providers offer highly scalable, global DNS services (e.g., Route 53, Azure DNS) that support:

  • Public zones: for internet-facing domains.
  • Private zones: for internal resolution within virtual networks.
  • Routing policies: geolocation, latency-based, weighted, failover for intelligent traffic steering.
    DNS is a critical control point for availability, performance, and security.

Virtual networking

The foundation of cloud infrastructure isolation. A virtual network (VPC, VNet) is a logically isolated section of the cloud where you launch resources. Key properties:

  • You define the private IP address space (RFC 1918 ranges or public block).
  • It is segmented into subnets for security and traffic management.
  • It can be connected to on-premises networks via VPN or dedicated links, or to other cloud networks via peering.
    Virtual networks enforce a boundary that gives you control over routing and access.

Subnets and CIDR

  • Subnets: A range of IP addresses within a virtual network. Subnets partition a VPC into smaller, isolated segments. You can designate subnets as public (accessible from the internet via an Internet Gateway) or private (no direct inbound internet).
  • CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing): The notation used to define subnets, e.g., 10.0.1.0/24. The /24 indicates the subnet mask (256 IPs). CIDR allows flexible, efficient allocation of IP space, avoiding the waste of traditional classful networking.
    Subnet design is fundamental to network security and high availability (spreading subnets across AZs).

VPN and Direct Connect concepts

Both connect on-premises environments to cloud virtual networks, but differ significantly:

  • VPN (Virtual Private Network): Encrypted tunnels over the public internet. Typically site-to-site IPSec VPN between an on-premises firewall and a cloud VPN Gateway. Cheaper, quick to set up, but bandwidth is limited and latency varies.
  • Direct Connect (AWS) / ExpressRoute (Azure): A dedicated, private fibre connection between your network and the cloud provider, bypassing the public internet. Provides more consistent latency, higher throughput, and stronger compliance; however, it takes time to provision and is more expensive. Often used together: Direct Connect for primary, VPN as backup.

Cloud monitoring basics

Cloud monitoring involves collecting, visualising, and alerting on metrics to understand the health and performance of resources. Providers offer native monitoring services (e.g., CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) that expose default metrics (CPU, disk I/O, network traffic) and allow custom metrics (application latency, order count). Alarms on these metrics trigger automated actions (scaling, notifying). Monitoring is the “know” part of “knowing and fixing”.

Cloud logging fundamentals

Logging captures detailed, time-stamped records of events for debugging, auditing, and analytics. Cloud logging services (e.g., CloudWatch Logs, Azure Log Analytics, Google Cloud Logging) centralise logs from applications, containers, and cloud platform APIs (management events). Features include retention policies, structured querying, and log-based metrics. In a well-architected cloud, every API call and application error is a log entry somewhere.

Cloud automation basics

Using software to perform operational tasks without manual intervention. Simple examples: auto-scaling groups spin up instances based on CPU; scheduled Lambda functions stop idle development instances at night. More advanced automation uses event-driven runbooks that automatically remediate security findings (e.g., automatically restoring a modified security group rule). Automation reduces human error and enables rapid response at cloud scale.

Infrastructure as Code fundamentals

IaC means defining infrastructure (servers, networks, databases) in machine-readable configuration files rather than clicking in a web console. The code is versioned, reviewed, and deployed through a pipeline. Benefits:

  • Consistency: Eliminates configuration drift.
  • Repeatability: Identical environments (dev, stage, prod) from the same code.
  • Speed: Deploy entire stacks in minutes.
    Tools: Terraform (multi-cloud, declarative), AWS CloudFormation, Azure Bicep, Pulumi.

Cloud-native principles

A set of design philosophies that fully exploit the cloud’s capabilities:

  • Microservices: Applications composed of small, independently deployable services.
  • Containers: Lightweight, consistent runtime packaging (Docker, Kubernetes).
  • DevOps & CI/CD: Automated build, test, and deployment pipelines.
  • Declarative APIs & IaC: All infrastructure and policies in code.
  • Observability: Deep monitoring, distributed tracing, structured logging.
  • Resilience & self-healing: Graceful degradation, automated restarts, circuit breakers.
    Cloud-native is about building systems that thrive on change, not break under it.

Green cloud computing

The practice of designing and operating cloud workloads to minimise environmental impact. This includes:

  • Rightsizing instances to avoid wasted energy.
  • Choosing cloud regions powered by a high percentage of renewable energy.
  • Utilising serverless and containerisation to maximise hardware utilisation density.
  • Demand shaping: Shifting non-time-critical workloads to times/regions with low carbon intensity.
    It’s a subset of overall IT sustainability.

Sustainability in cloud

A broader corporate commitment where providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) aim for carbon neutrality/net zero, offer carbon footprint dashboards to customers, and invest in renewable energy and water stewardship. For the customer, the Shared Responsibility for sustainability means providers give the tools and transparent metrics, but the customer must design efficient architectures and consciously select services. Sustainability metrics are becoming a new dimension of FinOps.

Cloud operating models

How teams, processes, and technology are organised to run cloud operations effectively. Common models evolve from:

  • Centralised: A single cloud/platform team manages everything (good for governance, slow for innovation).
  • Decentralised: Each business unit manages its own cloud (fast but risk of inconsistency).
  • Federated: A platform team builds shared services (networking, identity, golden images) while application teams have autonomy within guardrails.
    The operating model is supported by DevOps and SRE practices, enabling rapid, safe deployment.

Confidential computing

An emerging cloud security paradigm that protects data while it is being processed in memory, complementing existing protections for data at rest and in transit. It uses hardware-based Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs)—CPU-level enclaves (Intel SGX, AMD SEV, ARM TrustZone)—to isolate code and data from the host OS, hypervisor, and cloud provider personnel. Use cases include multi-party computation where multiple organisations process combined sensitive datasets without exposing raw data to each other, confidential AI/ML inference, and blockchain smart contract execution. Cloud services: Azure Confidential Computing (Confidential VMs, Confidential Containers), AWS Nitro Enclaves, GCP Confidential VMs.

Cloud bursting

A hybrid cloud pattern where an application runs primarily on private infrastructure but automatically "bursts" into the public cloud when on-premises capacity is exhausted. Traffic is handled on-premises during normal load; when demand spikes (seasonal traffic, batch jobs, CI builds), workloads scale into cloud VMs or containers seamlessly. Cloud bursting requires consistent workload packaging (containers), shared identity, and network connectivity (VPN/Direct Connect). It allows organisations to right-size on-premises hardware for baseline load while avoiding the capital cost of peak-capacity provisioning.

Landing zone concepts

A pre-configured, secure, scalable multi-account/multi-subscription environment serving as the foundation for all cloud workloads. A landing zone typically sets up:

  • Account/Subscription structure: Separate accounts for dev, prod, security, logging.
  • Networking: Hub-and-spoke VPC/VNet design, connectivity to on-premises.
  • Identity & access: Federated SSO, baseline roles, least-privilege policies.
  • Security: Centralised logging, guardrail policies (SCPs/Azure Policy), encryption.
  • Cost management: Budgets, mandatory tags.
    Landing zones are the “well-architected” starting point that lets teams deploy applications quickly, safely, and compliantly.

2. Cloud Architecture

Cloud architecture principles

Foundational design rules that guide cloud system construction. They include:

  • Design for failure (nothing is 100% reliable; assume components will fail).
  • Decouple components (loose coupling increases agility and resilience).
  • Implement elasticity (scale in and out automatically).
  • Think parallel (distribute workloads for scalability).
  • Automate everything (IaC, CI/CD, operations).
  • Security by design (embed identity, encryption, and least privilege from the start).
  • Optimise for cost (right‑size, use pay‑as‑you‑go and spot instances).
  • Leverage managed services (don’t re‑invent the undifferentiated heavy lifting).

Well‑architected frameworks

Structured evaluation tools published by cloud providers to help architects build secure, reliable, efficient, and cost‑effective systems. Examples:

  • AWS Well‑Architected Framework (six pillars): Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.
  • Azure Well‑Architected Framework (five pillars): Reliability, Security, Cost Optimization, Operational Excellence, Performance Efficiency.
  • Google Cloud Architecture Framework: pillars around reliability, cost, performance, security, operations.
    Each pillar contains design principles, best practices, and a set of questions used to audit workloads.

Scalability patterns

Ways to handle increasing load without degrading performance:

  • Scale‑out (horizontal): add more instances behind a load balancer; often stateless.
  • Scale‑up (vertical): increase the power of a single instance (CPU/RAM).
  • Predictive scaling: use machine learning to anticipate demand and scale proactively.
  • Sharding: split data across multiple databases to scale writes and reads.
  • Queue‑based load levelling: a queue absorbs bursts and smooths traffic to back‑end workers.
  • Functionally decompose: break a monolith into independently scalable microservices.

Resilience patterns

Design techniques that allow a system to withstand and recover from failures:

  • Retries with exponential backoff – transient failure recovery.
  • Circuit breaker – prevent cascading failures by failing fast when a remote service is unhealthy.
  • Bulkhead – isolate resources so that one failing component does not exhaust resources of others (e.g. separate thread pools).
  • Timeouts – set appropriate deadlines; avoid hanging.
  • Fail‑fast – validate inputs early and reject invalid requests.
  • Health checks & watchdogs – detect failure and trigger replacements.
  • Graceful degradation – return reduced functionality rather than complete failure.

Reliability engineering

The discipline of building systems that can be trusted to perform as expected, even under adverse conditions. It incorporates Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) practices: defining Service Level Indicators (SLIs) and Objectives (SLOs), managing error budgets, conducting blameless post‑mortems, and building automation to reduce toil. Reliability is measured by availability, durability, and correctness over time.

Distributed systems fundamentals

A distributed system is a set of independent computers that appear to its users as a single coherent system. Core concepts:

  • Communication (synchronous REST/gRPC, asynchronous messaging).
  • Coordination (leader election, consensus protocols like Paxos/Raft).
  • Time and ordering (logical clocks, vector clocks).
  • Fault tolerance (replication, checkpointing).
  • Scalability (partitioning, data sharding). The fallacies of distributed computing (the network is reliable, latency is zero, etc.) remind architects to always plan for partial failures.

CAP theorem

States that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of the following three guarantees:

  • Consistency (C) – every read receives the most recent write.
  • Availability (A) – every request receives a (non‑error) response, without guarantee of freshness.
  • Partition tolerance (P) – the system continues to operate despite network partitions.
    Because network partitions are inevitable, a system must choose between CP (strong consistency, sacrifice availability during partition) and AP (always available, but may return stale data). It’s a trade‑off model, not a binary “pick two”.

PACELC theorem

An extension of CAP that adds a second trade‑off dimension. It states: Partitioned → trade off A and C; Else → trade off L (Latency) and C (Consistency).

  • When a network partition occurs, the system chooses Availability or Consistency (same as CAP).
  • Under normal operation (no partition), the system chooses between low Latency and strong Consistency.
    Example: a system can be PA/EL (during partition: available; normally: low‑latency, eventually consistent) or PC/EC (during partition: consistent; normally: strong consistency at cost of latency).

Consistency models

Contracts between a data store and its clients about the visibility of writes:

  • Strong consistency – after a write completes, all subsequent reads see that value (linearizability).
  • Eventual consistency – if no new writes, eventually all reads return the last written value.
  • Causal consistency – causally related operations are seen in the same order by all nodes.
  • Read‑your‑writes – a client always sees its own updates.
  • Monotonic reads – a client never sees older data than it has already seen.
  • Session consistency – consistency guarantees within a single session.
  • Bounded staleness – reads may lag but the lag is limited (e.g. by time or version count).

Event‑driven architecture

A paradigm where components communicate by producing and consuming events (immutable records of something that happened). Producers (publishers) emit events to a bus/router; consumers (subscribers) react to them asynchronously. This decouples services in time and space, enables elastic scaling, and supports patterns like event choreography. Common in serverless and microservices, often implemented with message queues (SQS, RabbitMQ) or streaming platforms (Kafka, Kinesis).

Event sourcing

Storing all changes to an application’s state as a sequence of immutable events, rather than only the current state. The current state is rebuilt by replaying the events. Benefits:

  • Complete audit trail.
  • Temporal queries (state at any point in time).
  • Easy event‑driven integration (events can be republished).
  • De‑bugging and replay for new projections.
    Trade‑offs: increased storage, complexity of event schema evolution, and eventual consistency of read models.

CQRS architecture

Command Query Responsibility Segregation separates read and write operations into different models:

  • Commands (writes) modify state, processed by the write model (often with event sourcing).
  • Queries (reads) retrieve data from one or more optimised read models (materialised views).
    This allows independent scaling, optimization, and security policies for reads and writes. Frequently used with event sourcing, where events are projected into denormalised, query‑friendly views.

Microservices architecture

An application is built as a suite of small, independently deployable services, each owning its own business capability and data. Services communicate over the network via lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC, messaging). Key characteristics:

  • Decentralised data management (database per service).
  • Smart endpoints, dumb pipes.
  • Automated CI/CD and infrastructure.
  • Design for failure (circuit breakers, retries).
  • Organised around business domains (Domain‑Driven Design).

Service‑oriented architecture (SOA)

An earlier architectural style where applications are composed of reusable, coarse‑grained services that communicate via an Enterprise Service Bus (ESB). SOA typically shares common data models, uses heavy middleware, and is often tied to SOAP/XML and WS‑* standards. Microservices can be seen as an evolution of SOA that avoids the central bus, promotes decentralised governance, and uses lightweight protocols.

Serverless architecture

Applications built on FaaS (Lambda, Azure Functions) and BaaS (managed databases, queues, storage) where the cloud provider fully manages server provisioning, scaling, and patching. Code runs in ephemeral, stateless containers triggered by events. Characteristics:

  • No server management.
  • Automatic, instantaneous scaling (including to zero).
  • Pay per invocation (fine‑grained billing).
  • Event‑driven, short‑lived execution (timeouts apply).
    Not ideal for long‑running or high‑stateful workloads without careful design.

Container‑based architecture

Packaging application code, dependencies, and runtime into lightweight, portable images that run as isolated processes (containers) on a shared OS kernel. Containers provide consistency across environments. Orchestrators (Kubernetes, ECS) handle scheduling, scaling, service discovery, and networking. This architecture supports microservices, enables immutable deployments, and improves resource density compared to VMs.

Monolithic vs microservices

  • Monolith: Single deployable unit containing all functionality. Simpler to develop, test, and deploy initially, but as it grows, it becomes harder to understand, scale, and maintain. Changes require redeploying the whole application.
  • Microservices: Distributed system of small, focused services. Offers independent deployability, technology diversity, and team autonomy, but introduces complexity in networking, data consistency, monitoring, and operational overhead. The choice depends on organisational maturity, team size, and application complexity; many advocate starting with a modular monolith and splitting later.

API‑first architecture

An approach where APIs are treated as first‑class citizens, designed and agreed upon before implementation begins. An API contract (often using OpenAPI/Swagger) serves as the interface between frontend and backend, and between services. This enables parallel development, automated testing and mocking, and a consistent, developer‑friendly surface. It is central to microservices and ecosystem integration.

Backend‑for‑frontend (BFF)

A pattern where a dedicated backend layer is created for each specific frontend client (web, mobile, IoT). Rather than having a single general‑purpose API gateway that serves all, BFFs tailor the API to the exact needs of the client, aggregating data from multiple downstream services and optimising the payload. This reduces over‑fetching, under‑fetching, and client complexity.

Domain‑driven design

A software design approach that aligns technical architecture with business domain experts’ mental models. Core concepts:

  • Bounded contexts – explicit boundaries where a domain model applies (these often map to microservices).
  • Ubiquitous language – a shared, precise vocabulary between developers and domain experts.
  • Entities, Value Objects, Aggregates – building blocks that model core business rules. DDD helps decompose a complex domain into understandable, loosely coupled services.

Hexagonal architecture (Ports and Adapters)

A pattern that isolates the core business logic from external concerns (UI, databases, APIs, queues). The application core exposes ports (interfaces) for primary (driving) and secondary (driven) operations. Adapters implement these ports for specific technologies. This makes the core technology‑agnostic, testable, and easy to modify without changing business logic.

Clean architecture

Proposed by Robert C. Martin, it is a layered architecture where dependencies point inward toward the central domain (entities and use cases). Outer layers (frameworks, UI, databases) are replaceable and have no impact on the inner layers. It formalises the dependency rule: source code dependencies can only point inward. It unifies concepts from hexagonal, onion, and DDD.

Layered architecture

The traditional n‑tier model: Presentation, Application/Business, Persistence, Database. Each layer depends on the layer directly below it. It is simple and widely understood, but can lead to tightly coupled “lasagna” code if strictly enforced without dependency inversion. In cloud‑native systems, purely layered architectures are often replaced by more modular, domain‑oriented structures.

Sidecar pattern

A container design pattern where a helper service (the sidecar) is deployed alongside the main application container within the same pod (Kubernetes) or task. The sidecar provides cross‑cutting capabilities such as logging, monitoring, service mesh proxy (Envoy), or configuration refresh. The application remains unchanged; the sidecar intercepts network traffic or shares volumes. This embodies the single‑responsibility principle at the infrastructure level.

Service mesh architecture

A dedicated infrastructure layer that manages service‑to‑service communication transparently. It consists of a control plane (e.g. Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect) and a data plane of lightweight proxies (sidecars) beside each service. It provides features like:

  • Traffic splitting and routing (canary deployments).
  • Mutual TLS (mTLS) and identity‑based security.
  • Observability (metrics, distributed tracing).
  • Resilience (retries, circuit breakers, timeouts) without changing application code.

Load balancing patterns

Methods for distributing incoming traffic across multiple backend instances:

  • Round‑robin / Least connections – simple algorithms.
  • Session affinity (sticky sessions) – user requests always go to the same backend (useful for stateful apps).
  • Layer 4 (transport) load balancing – based on IP/port, fast but content‑unaware.
  • Layer 7 (application) load balancing – routes based on URL, headers, cookies; enables blue/green and A/B testing.
  • Global server load balancing – DNS‑based traffic management across regions.
  • Weighted routing – direct a percentage of traffic to specific versions.

Reverse proxy architecture

A server that sits in front of web servers and forwards client requests to the appropriate backend. It provides:

  • Security (hiding backend topology, TLS termination, WAF integration).
  • Caching & compression.
  • Load balancing.
  • Authentication and authorisation enforcement. Popular reverse proxies: NGINX, HAProxy, Envoy, cloud ALB/Application Gateway.

API Gateway patterns

An API gateway is a single entry point for external clients that routes requests to internal microservices. Common patterns include:

  • Gateway routing – map endpoints to services.
  • Gateway aggregation – call multiple services and combine results.
  • Gateway offloading – handle cross‑cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, request/response transformation, logging).
  • Backend‑for‑frontend (BFF) as a specialised gateway. API gateways must not become a heavyweight ESB; they should remain lightweight.

Caching strategies

Techniques to temporarily store frequently accessed data for faster retrieval:

  • Client‑side caching (browser, mobile) using HTTP cache‑control headers, ETags.
  • CDN caching – edge caching of static and dynamic content.
  • Application caching – in‑memory stores (Redis, Memcached) for database queries, session state, computed results.
  • Database caching – built‑in query caches, materialised views.
  • Write‑through, write‑behind, write‑around – cache update policies.
  • Cache‑aside – application checks cache first; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache.
  • TTL & invalidation – time‑to‑live or event‑driven invalidation to prevent stale data.

CDN architecture

A distributed network of edge servers (Points of Presence) that cache content closer to users to reduce latency and offload origin. Architecture components:

  • Origin server – the source of truth.
  • Edge caches – serve content from the nearest location.
  • Cache hierarchy – some CDNs use mid‑tier caches to reduce origin load.
  • Dynamic site acceleration – optimised routing and connection keep‑alive for non‑cacheable content. Modern CDNs also run edge compute (Lambda@Edge, Cloudflare Workers) for personalisation and security logic.

Data partitioning

Dividing a database table into smaller, more manageable chunks to improve scalability, performance, and availability. Partitioning can be:

  • Vertical: splitting columns (e.g. frequently accessed columns in one table, large blobs in another).
  • Horizontal (sharding): splitting rows across multiple database instances.
    Partitioning key selection is critical to avoid hotspots and enable efficient query routing.

Sharding strategies

A specific form of horizontal partitioning where each shard is a separate database instance holding a subset of the data:

  • Key‑based / Hash sharding – hash the shard key to determine shard; uniform distribution but poor range query support.
  • Range‑based sharding – partition by value ranges (e.g. CustomerID A‑M, N‑Z); enables range scans but can cause hotspots.
  • Directory‑based sharding – a lookup service maps data items to shards, allowing flexible movement.
  • Geo‑sharding – data partitioned by geographic region to meet data residency or latency requirements. Sharding increases application complexity (joins across shards, transactions are limited).

Replication strategies

Copying data across multiple nodes for availability, durability, and read scalability:

  • Single‑leader (master‑slave) – all writes go to a primary; replicas handle reads. Asynchronous or synchronous.
  • Multi‑leader – writes can go to multiple primaries; conflict detection/resolution needed.
  • Leaderless – any node can accept writes (Dynamo‑style, using quorum reads/writes).
  • Synchronous vs asynchronous: synchronous guarantees data consistency at the cost of latency; asynchronous provides better performance but can lose data on failure.
  • Read replicas – dedicated copies for scaling reads and analytical queries.

Distributed caching

An in‑memory data store spread across a cluster of nodes to cache large datasets that do not fit on a single machine. Examples: Redis Cluster, Memcached (sharding at client), Hazelcast. Key techniques:

  • Consistent hashing to minimise redistribution when nodes join/leave.
  • Replication for high availability.
  • Eviction policies (LRU, LFU, TTL).
  • Invalidation via events or time‑out. Distributed caching reduces database load and improves response times dramatically.

Stateless architecture

An application design where each request is processed independently without relying on server‑side session state. Any instance can handle any request. State, if needed, is stored externally (in a shared database, distributed cache, or token). Stateless services are inherently easier to scale horizontally, fault‑tolerant, and simple to deploy because they can be replaced or restarted at any time.

Stateful architecture

An application that maintains data about client sessions or interactions between requests on the server. Examples: a database server, a stateful stream processor (Kafka Streams), or a WebSocket chat server with in‑memory user connections. Stateful workloads require careful handling: persistent volumes, sticky sessions, and instance‑specific identities. They are harder to scale and recover but are necessary for many core functions.

Data lake architecture

A centralised repository that stores raw, unprocessed data in its native format (structured, semi‑structured, unstructured) at any scale. Key characteristics:

  • Separation of storage and compute (e.g. S3/ADLS + query engines like Presto/Spark).
  • Schema‑on‑read – data is interpreted when read, not when ingested.
  • Layered zones: raw (bronze), cleansed (silver), curated (gold).
  • Governed catalog (AWS Glue, Apache Atlas) for metadata management. Data lakes enable advanced analytics, machine learning, and exploration without upfront modelling.

Data mesh architecture

A socio‑technical paradigm that treats data as a product, owned by domain teams that serve it to the rest of the organisation. Core principles:

  • Domain‑oriented ownership – the team that understands the data publishes it.
  • Data as a product – discoverable, trustworthy, self‑describing, interoperable, and secure datasets.
  • Self‑serve data platform – a common infrastructure (catalog, pipelines, storage) that domains use to build and serve data products.
  • Federated computational governance – global standards enforced automatically. Data mesh addresses the scaling bottleneck of a centralised data lake/warehouse team.

Stream processing architecture

Processing data as a continuous, unbounded flow of events in near‑real‑time. Systems like Kafka Streams, Apache Flink, or Kinesis Data Analytics perform operations such as filtering, aggregations, joins, and windowing on the fly. Key concepts:

  • Event time vs processing time.
  • Windowing (tumbling, sliding, session).
  • State management (RocksDB for fault‑tolerant local state).
  • Exactly‑once semantics. Stream processing enables fraud detection, real‑time dashboards, and event‑driven microservices.

Batch processing architecture

Processing large volumes of static, bounded data on a schedule or on demand. Apache Spark, Hadoop MapReduce, and cloud‑native services (AWS Glue, Azure Data Factory) run jobs that extract, transform, and load (ETL/ELT) data. Characteristics:

  • High throughput, higher latency (minutes to hours).
  • Optimised for complex aggregations and joins.
  • Handles data reprocessing easily. Batch remains the backbone of daily reporting and large‑scale data integration.

Edge computing

Moving compute and data processing closer to the source of data generation (users, IoT devices) rather than relying solely on central cloud regions. This reduces latency, bandwidth consumption, and enables local decision‑making. Implemented via:

  • CDN edge functions (CloudFront Functions, Cloudflare Workers).
  • Edge gateways and appliances (AWS Outposts, Azure Stack Edge).
  • 5G MEC (Multi‑access Edge Computing). Crucial for AR/VR, autonomous systems, and industrial IoT.

Global architecture design

Designing a system to serve users across multiple geographic regions, considering latency, data sovereignty, disaster recovery, and compliance. It involves DNS‑based routing, multi‑region active‑active or active‑passive deployments, global databases (Spanner, DynamoDB Global Tables, Cosmos DB), and geo‑replication of static and dynamic content.

Active‑active architecture

Multiple geographically distributed sites are all actively serving production traffic simultaneously. Users are routed to the nearest or most optimal site. Requires:

  • Global load balancing with health checks.
  • Data synchronisation (bi‑directional replication) with conflict resolution.
  • Stateless or synchronised state across regions. This setup provides the highest availability and lowest latency but is complex and costly.

Active‑passive architecture

Only one site (the active) serves all traffic; one or more passive (standby) sites are kept synchronised (data replication) but do not handle requests. When the active site fails, traffic is redirected to a passive site after a failover procedure. It is simpler to implement and cheaper than active‑active, but the failover may introduce downtime (RTO) and potential data loss (RPO).

Multi‑region design

An architecture that spans two or more cloud regions to achieve:

  • Disaster recovery (surviving a regional outage).
  • Low latency for global users.
  • Data residency compliance. Key elements: global DNS/routing, inter‑region VPC peering or Transit Gateway, synchronous/asynchronous data replication, and careful orchestration of failover (manual or automated).

Multi‑tenant architecture

A single application instance serves multiple customers (tenants), with data isolation and customisability per tenant. Tenancy models:

  • Database per tenant – strongest isolation, highest cost.
  • Schema per tenant – separate schemas within one database.
  • Shared database, shared schema – tenant discriminator column; lowest cost but requires rigorous query filtering. Multi‑tenancy requires identity and access control, tenant‑aware monitoring, and isolation of noisy neighbours.

Failover strategies

Plans for transitioning from a failed component to a redundant standby:

  • Cold failover – secondary infrastructure is provisioned from scratch or from backups; higher RTO.
  • Warm failover – secondary runs at minimal capacity; scaled up during failover.
  • Hot failover – fully provisioned and running; nearly instant cutover.
  • DNS failover – change DNS records (slow propagation).
  • Load balancer health‑based failover – fast, automatic redirection.
  • Database failover – promote a read replica or synchronous standby to primary.

Chaos engineering

The discipline of experimenting on a system in production (or pre‑production) to uncover weaknesses and build confidence in the system’s ability to withstand turbulent conditions. Steps:

  1. Define a steady‑state hypothesis.
  2. Inject controlled faults (server crash, network latency, region down).
  3. Measure the impact and compare against hypothesis.
  4. Fix vulnerabilities. Tools: Chaos Monkey, Gremlin, LitmusChaos, AWS Fault Injection Simulator. It is proactive reliability testing.

Observability architecture

The practice and tooling that allows you to understand a system’s internal state by analysing its external outputs. The three pillars:

  • Logs: timestamped discrete events.
  • Metrics: numeric data aggregated over time (CPU, request rate, error rate).
  • Traces: end‑to‑end journey of a single request through distributed services. Observability requires instrumentation, correlation of telemetry, and dashboards with alerting. It surpasses traditional monitoring by enabling ad‑hoc exploration of unknown issues.

Logging and tracing design

  • Logging design: Structured logging (JSON) with consistent schemas (timestamp, service name, trace ID, log level, message). Centralised collection to a log aggregator (Elasticsearch, Loki, CloudWatch Logs). Log levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR) and retention policies.
  • Tracing design: Distributed tracing using OpenTelemetry or similar; assign a unique trace ID at the ingress and propagate it across all service calls. Each service records spans (child‑of relationships). Visualisation in tools like Jaeger, Zipkin, or AWS X‑Ray helps pinpoint latency bottlenecks.

Metrics and telemetry architecture

Metrics capture time‑series data representing system and business performance. Architecture includes:

  • Collection – agents or SDKs emitting metrics to a central time‑series database (Prometheus, InfluxDB, cloud services).
  • Aggregation – counters, gauges, histograms, summaries.
  • Dashboards (Grafana) and alerting (Alertmanager).
  • Golden signals: Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation (from Google SRE). Telemetry also includes business KPIs (orders per minute, signups) integrated into the same observability pipeline.

Security architecture patterns

Proven, reusable solutions for common cloud security problems:

  • Federated identity – central IdP (SAML/OIDC) for SSO.
  • Policy‑as‑code – infrastructure guardrails (AWS SCPs, Azure Policy, OPA).
  • Data perimeters – protecting data with encryption, DLP, and access controls.
  • Continuous compliance – automated checks that resources adhere to standards (AWS Config, Azure Policy).
  • Secure by default – deny all, allow only explicitly needed permissions and network paths.

Zero trust architecture

A security model that assumes no implicit trust based on location (network perimeter). Every access request must be authenticated, authorised, and encrypted, regardless of whether it originates inside or outside the corporate network. Principles:

  • Verify explicitly – use all available signals (identity, device health, data classification) before granting access.
  • Least privilege access – just‑in‑time and just‑enough‑access (JIT/JEA).
  • Assume breach – segment networks, log and monitor continuously, minimise blast radius. Architecture uses micro‑segmentation, IAM, and continuous monitoring.

Defense‑in‑depth

Layered security controls that protect the system even if one layer fails. Layers include:

  • Perimeter (DDoS protection, firewalls).
  • Network (security groups, subnet segmentation, NACLs).
  • Host (hardened OS, anti‑malware, patch management).
  • Application (input validation, WAF, code scanning).
  • Data (encryption at rest/transit, access logging).
  • Identity (MFA, strong authentication, RBAC/ABAC). It’s a fundamental principle in cloud security design.

Secrets management architecture

Securely storing and controlling access to sensitive information like API keys, database passwords, and TLS certificates. Best‑practice architecture:

  • Centralised vault – AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault.
  • Automatic rotation – secrets are changed periodically without human intervention.
