    • Balanced hierarchy — all leaves at same level, Year → Quarter → Month → Day
    • Ragged hierarchy — variable depth, World → Country → [State →] City, IS-NULL skips
    • Parent-child hierarchy — recursive, EmployeeId, ManagerId, variable depth
    • Level-based hierarchy — fixed columns per level, L1_Desc, L2_Desc, L3_Desc
    • Surrogate key in dimension — integer, system-generated, insulates from source changes
    • Business key — source system identifier, CustomerID from CRM
    • Late-arriving dimension — fact arrives before dimension record, inferred member
    • Late-arriving fact — fact arrives after expected, backfill
    • Early-arriving fact — placeholder dimension row, update when dimension arrives
    • Inferred member — temporary placeholder for unknown dimension key
    • Bus matrix — data warehouse architecture chart, facts vs dimensions, conformance check
    • Kimball lifecycle — project planning → requirements → tech architecture → dimensional modelling → physical design → ETL → BI app → deploy
    • Data mart — subject-specific, departmental, sourced from EDW or directly
    • Enterprise Data Warehouse (EDW) — integrated, subject-oriented, centralized
    • Independent data mart — standalone, risk of inconsistency
    • Dependent data mart — sourced from EDW, consistent
    • Aggregate table — pre-computed summary, AggWeeklySales, transparent via aggregate navigator

    6. NoSQL & Polyglot Data Modelling

    • Polyglot persistence — right database for right workload, multi-store architecture
    • Document model — embed vs reference, denormalize for read performance, MongoDB
    • Embedding — nested documents, Order contains OrderLines, one read
    • Referencing — ObjectId FK, separate collections, normalized
    • Working set — documents accessed frequently, fits in RAM, index design
    • Collection design — one collection per entity type vs. polymorphic collection
    • Polymorphic schema — mixed document types in collection, type field discriminator
    • Key-Value model — key → value, no schema, Redis, DynamoDB simple access
    • Key design — {tenant}:{entity}:{id}, namespace, composite key patterns
    • Wide-column model — Cassandra, HBase, partition key + clustering key + columns
    • Partition key — determines node, even distribution, hotspot avoidance
    • Clustering key — ordering within partition, range queries, ORDER BY substitute
    • Single-table design (DynamoDB) — PK, SK, GSI, LSI, entity adjacency
    • Entity adjacency list — PK=CustomerId, SK=ORDER#OrderId — multiple entity types
    • GSI overloading — GSI1PK, GSI1SK serve multiple access patterns
    • Access pattern first — NoSQL design starts with queries, not entities
    • Hierarchical data in NoSQL — materialized path, nested sets, closure table equivalent
    • Graph model — Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, nodes + relationships + properties
    • Property graph — labeled nodes, labeled relationships, properties on both
    • RDF graph — triples (subject, predicate, object), SPARQL, semantic web
    • Cypher — MATCH (n:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(m:Person) RETURN n, m
    • Time-series model — InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, measurement + tags + fields + timestamp
    • Tag — indexed metadata, low cardinality, sensor_id, region
    • Field — measured value, not indexed, temperature, pressure
    • Retention policy — automatic deletion, DURATION 30d, storage management
    • Continuous aggregate — materialized rollup, TimescaleDB, refresh interval
    • Search model — Elasticsearch, inverted index, _source, _score, mappings
    • Mapping — field types, keyword vs text, analyzers, dynamic: strict
    • Index template — default mappings for pattern-matched indices
    • Alias — abstract index name, zero-downtime reindex, read/write alias
    • Event store model — AggregateId + Version, append-only, CQRS read model
    • Vector database — embedding storage, ANN search, pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate
    • Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) — HNSW, IVF, cosine/dot product/L2 distance

    7. Data Vault Modelling

    • Data Vault 2.0 — Dan Linstedt, auditable, scalable, agile DW methodology
    • Hub — unique business keys, HubCustomer, CustomerHashKey, LoadDate, RecordSource
    • Link — relationships between hubs, LinkOrderCustomer, OrderHashKey + CustomerHashKey
    • Satellite — descriptive attributes, history, SatCustomerProfile, LoadDate, LoadEndDate
    • Hash key — MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256 of business key, deterministic, surrogate key replacement
    • Business key — source system identifier, loaded into Hub unchanged
    • LoadDate — when record was loaded, never updated, audit trail
    • RecordSource — which system provided this record, CRM, ERP, FILE
    • LoadEndDate — when record was superseded, null = current, Satellite history
    • Same-As Link (SAL) — deduplication, link two Hub records representing same entity
    • Hierarchical Link — recursive relationship, parent-child in same Hub
    • Non-Historized Link (NHL) — current relationship only, no dates
    • Point-in-Time (PIT) table — snapshot of Satellite valid keys at a point in time
    • Bridge table — combine multiple Links + PIT, query performance helper
    • Reference table — static lookup data, RefCountry, RefCurrency
    • Computed Satellite — derived data, business rules applied, separate from raw
    • Business Vault — derived/transformed layer, soft business rules applied
    • Raw Vault — untransformed, as-loaded, audit-ready
    • Virtual Data Mart — views over Raw + Business Vault, no physical mart
    • Data Vault on cloud — Snowflake + DV2.0, MERGE for Hub/Link/Sat loads
    • AutoVault / DV automation — WhereScape, Vaultspeed, dbtvault (dbt)
    • dbtvault macros — hub(), link(), sat(), eff_sat(), pit(), bridge()
    • Effectivity Satellite — tracks when a Link relationship was active, EffStartDate, EffEndDate
    • Multi-active Satellite — multiple active rows per Hub key, SatEmployeeAddress
    • Status tracking Satellite — track status over time, OrderStatus
    • DV 2.0 principles — model everything as Hub/Link/Sat, use hash keys, parallel load
    • Pattern-based loading — standardized load patterns, auditable, repeatable

    8. Temporal & Spatial Data Modelling

    • Temporal data — data changes over time, history preservation
    • Valid time (VT) — when fact was true in real world, valid_from, valid_to
    • Transaction time (TT) — when fact was stored in DB, inserted_at, deleted_at
    • Bi-temporal model — both valid time and transaction time, AS OF queries
    • Bi-temporal table — valid_start, valid_end, txn_start, txn_end, four timestamps
    • Point-in-time query — WHERE valid_from <= :date AND valid_to > :date
    • Sequenced semantics — operations respect time dimension
    • Non-sequenced semantics — operations ignore time dimension
    • Temporal normalization — normalize within time periods
    • Temporal FK — FK valid during overlapping periods
    • SQL:2011 temporal — PERIOD FOR VALID_TIME, FOR VALID_TIME AS OF, BETWEEN
    • System-versioned temporal table — SQL Server, PERIOD FOR SYSTEM_TIME, auto-managed
    • Application-time period table — PERIOD FOR app_period, user-managed
    • Slowly Changing Dimension — temporal dimension history, see §5
    • Retroactive change — change historical valid time, bi-temporal required
    • Spatial data — geographic, geometric, location-aware
    • Geometry types — POINT, LINESTRING, POLYGON, MULTIPOLYGON, GEOMETRYCOLLECTION
    • SRS (Spatial Reference System) — SRID 4326 (WGS84 lat/lng), SRID 3857 (Web Mercator)
    • PostGIS — PostgreSQL spatial extension, ST_Distance, ST_Within, ST_Intersects
    • Spatial index — GiST, SP-GiST, BRIN for PostgreSQL, R-tree
    • R-tree index — bounding rectangle hierarchy, spatial query acceleration
    • Geohash — drg5x8, hierarchical, proximity search, LIKE 'drg%'
    • H3 — Uber hexagonal grid, resolution 0–15, h3_geo_to_h3(), uniform area
    • QuadTree — recursive 4-partition, geospatial indexing, ST_GeoHash
    • KD-tree — multi-dimensional, nearest neighbor search
    • Spatial joins — ST_DWithin, ST_Contains, ST_Overlaps, bounding box pre-filter

    9. Data Management Fundamentals (DAMA)

    • DAMA-DMBOK — Data Management Body of Knowledge, v2, 11 knowledge areas
    • Data management definition — development, execution, supervision of plans/policies/programs to deliver, control, protect, enhance value of data/information assets
    • Data as an asset — economic value, lifecycle management, stewardship
    • Data management goals — understand data, manage lifecycle, protect, deliver quality
    • 11 DAMA Knowledge Areas — Data Governance, Data Architecture, Data Modelling & Design, Data Storage & Operations, Data Security, Data Integration & Interoperability, Document & Content Management, Reference & Master Data, Data Warehousing & BI, Metadata Management, Data Quality
    • DAMA Wheel — Data Governance at center, 10 knowledge areas surrounding
    • Data lifecycle — create/acquire, store, maintain, use, archive, purge
    • Information lifecycle management (ILM) — policies for each lifecycle stage
    • Data stewardship — accountability for data assets, business steward vs technical steward
    • Data ownership — authoritative source, accountability for quality and access
    • Data custodian — IT role, physical storage, technical implementation
    • Data consumer — uses data, provides requirements, quality feedback
    • RACI for data — Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed — per data asset
    • Data value chain — raw data → information → knowledge → wisdom → decisions
    • Unstructured data — documents, email, images, video, audio — 80% of enterprise data
    • Semi-structured data — JSON, XML, CSV, log files — implicit schema
    • Structured data — RDBMS, defined schema, tabular
    • Dark data — collected but unused, risk and cost without value
    • Data proliferation — uncontrolled copies, versions, silos
    • Single source of truth (SSOT) — authoritative system of record per data element
    • System of record (SOR) — authoritative source, Customer in CRM
    • System of engagement (SOE) — interaction system, channels, less structured
    • System of insight (SOI) — analytics, AI, decision support
    • Golden record — master data, best-version, survived MDM matching/merging

    10. DAMA CDMP — Data Governance

    • Data Governance definition — exercise of authority, control, shared decision-making over data assets
    • Data governance program — policies, processes, organization, technology, metrics
    • Data governance council — steering committee, cross-functional, executive sponsorship
    • Chief Data Officer (CDO) — executive accountability, data strategy, governance program
    • Data governance office (DGO) — program management, policy development, coordination
    • Data governance framework — operating model, roles, decision rights, processes
    • Data stewardship council — operational level, domain stewards, data quality issues
    • Decision rights — who can define, create, read, update, delete, approve, reject
    • Data policies — mandatory rules, data must be classified, PII encrypted at rest
    • Data standards — naming conventions, data types, reference codes, formats
    • Data procedures — step-by-step how-to, operational guides
    • Business glossary — approved business terms, definitions, stewards, synonyms
    • Term lifecycle — DRAFT → PROPOSED → APPROVED → DEPRECATED → RETIRED
    • Governance operating model — centralized, federated, hybrid, decentralized
    • Centralized governance — single DGO, consistent standards, slower decisions
    • Federated governance — domain teams own data, center provides frameworks
    • Hybrid governance — central for enterprise standards, federated for domain specifics
    • Data mesh governance — federated computational governance, policy as code
    • Policy as code — OPA (Open Policy Agent), automated enforcement, rego language
    • Data classification — Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted, Top Secret
    • Data handling rules — per classification, encryption, access, retention, disposal
    • Data ethics — fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, bias
    • Data ethics framework — principles, review board, AI ethics guidelines
    • Regulatory compliance — GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOX, BCBS 239
    • GDPR key concepts — data subject, controller, processor, lawful basis, consent, DPA
    • Right to erasure — forget-me request, soft delete, pseudonymization
    • Right to access — DSAR (Data Subject Access Request), 30-day response
    • Data residency — where data stored, EU data must stay in EU, contractual
    • Data sovereignty — legal jurisdiction over data, cross-border transfer restrictions
    • Privacy by design — embed privacy in design, data minimization, purpose limitation
    • Data minimization — collect only what's needed, GDPR Article 5
    • Purpose limitation — use data only for stated purpose
    • Consent management — collect, store, withdraw consent, CMP (Consent Management Platform)
    • Pseudonymization — replace PII with pseudonym, reversible with key
    • Anonymization — irreversible removal of identifying info, not personal data under GDPR
    • Tokenization — replace sensitive data with token, vault stores mapping
    • PII (Personally Identifiable Information) — direct (SSN, Name) vs indirect (IP, Device ID)
    • Sensitive personal data — health, race, biometric, political opinion, GDPR special categories
    • Data lineage — origin, transformations, where data flows, impact analysis
    • Impact analysis — what downstream systems/reports affected by change
    • Governance maturity model — Initial → Repeatable → Defined → Managed → Optimizing (CMM-based)
    • KPIs for governance — policy adoption rate, steward coverage, issue resolution time, quality scores

    11. DAMA CDMP — Data Architecture

    • Data architecture definition — specifications used to describe existing state, define data requirements, guide data integration, align with enterprise strategy
    • Enterprise data architecture — all data assets, flows, stores, technology landscape
    • Data architecture artifacts — current state (as-is), target state (to-be), roadmap
    • Conceptual data architecture — subject areas, high-level flows, business entities
    • Logical data architecture — entities, relationships, canonical data model, integration
    • Physical data architecture — databases, files, APIs, storage technologies
    • Data architecture principles — data is an asset, shared data, accessible data, trusted data
    • Enterprise data model (EDM) — cross-functional, canonical, subject areas
    • Canonical data model — common data format for integration, reduces N×M to N+M
    • Conceptual-to-logical-to-physical traceability — mapping between model levels
    • Reference architecture — reusable pattern, technology choices, integration patterns
    • Data flow diagram (DFD) — external entities, processes, data stores, flows
    • Context diagram (DFD Level 0) — system boundary, external entities
    • Data flow level 1 — major processes, data stores, flows between them
    • Application portfolio — inventory of applications, data inputs/outputs, ownership
    • System of record mapping — authoritative source per subject area
    • Golden source — SOR for master data, Customer → CRM, Product → PIM
    • Data at rest — stored data, encryption, backup, archival
    • Data in motion — streaming, ETL, APIs, encryption in transit
    • Data in use — active processing, memory, compute encryption (confidential computing)
    • Data topology — where data lives, replication, synchronization patterns
    • Data mesh architecture — domain-oriented, product thinking, self-serve platform
    • Data fabric — unified data management layer, automated integration, AI-powered metadata
    • Data lakehouse — ACID on data lake, Delta/Iceberg/Hudi
    • Lambda architecture — batch + speed + serving layers
    • Kappa architecture — streaming only, reprocess from log

    12. DAMA CDMP — Data Modelling & Design

    • All topics from Sections 1–8 (Data Modelling Fundamentals through Temporal & Spatial)
    • Data modelling lifecycle — requirements → conceptual → logical → physical → deployed
    • Modelling standards — IDEF1X, UML, ORM2, Barker, ISO/IEC 11179
    • ISO/IEC 11179 — metadata registry standard, data element naming, definition
    • Naming conventions — Pascal case, snake_case, abbreviation glossary, reserved words
    • Data element naming — {Object Class}_{Property}_{Representation}, Customer_Birth_Date
    • Definition quality — precise, non-circular, in business terms, agreed
    • Data model review — peer review, steward approval, DBA review, business sign-off
    • Model versioning — v1.0, v1.1, v2.0, change log, baseline
    • Forward engineering — model → DDL script, CREATE TABLE, constraints, indexes
    • Reverse engineering — existing DB → model, import schema, normalize understanding
    • Schema comparison / diff — compare model vs DB, drift detection
    • Modelling tools — erwin Data Modeler, PowerDesigner, ER/Studio, Hackolade, dbdiagram.io
    • Model repository — central storage, version control, search, impact analysis
    • Metadata capture — definitions, lineage, ownership in model tool

    13. DAMA CDMP — Data Storage & Operations

    • Database administration (DBA) — physical design, performance, backup, recovery
    • DBMS types — RDBMS, NoSQL (document, key-value, columnar, graph), NewSQL, time-series
    • Storage architecture — SAN, NAS, DAS, object storage, cloud storage
    • High availability (HA) — 99.99%, active-active, active-passive, clustering
    • Backup types — full, incremental, differential, continuous, pg_dump, mysqldump
    • Recovery types — point-in-time recovery (PITR), crash recovery, media recovery
    • RTO (Recovery Time Objective) — maximum tolerable downtime
    • RPO (Recovery Point Objective) — maximum tolerable data loss
    • WAL (Write-Ahead Log) — durability, PITR, pg_wal, binlog
    • Replication lag — sync vs async replication, slave lag, monitoring
    • Disaster recovery (DR) — off-site, different AZ/region, DR test frequency
    • Database versioning — Flyway, Liquibase, Alembic, migration scripts
    • Capacity planning — growth projections, storage, IOPS, CPU, memory
    • Performance monitoring — pg_stat_statements, slow query log, AWR (Oracle)
    • Query optimization — execution plan, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, index usage, stats
    • Statistics — ANALYZE, VACUUM, RUNSTATS, optimizer decisions
    • Connection management — pooling (PgBouncer), max connections, idle timeout
    • Locking and concurrency — deadlock detection, lock wait timeout, MVCC
    • Archiving strategy — archive vs delete, legal hold, cold storage
    • Data purging — GDPR erasure, retention policies, cascading deletes
    • Data masking — non-prod environments, faker, dynamic masking
    • Dynamic data masking — mask at query time, role-based, Azure SQL, Oracle VPD
    • Column encryption — Always Encrypted (SQL Server), pgcrypto, AES-256
    • Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) — file-level, at-rest encryption, key management
    • Audit trail — AUDIT statement, change data capture, temporal tables

    14. DAMA CDMP — Data Security

    • Data security definition — ensure privacy, confidentiality, prevent unauthorized access/modification
    • Data security framework — classification → access control → encryption → monitoring → response
    • Access control models — DAC (Discretionary), MAC (Mandatory), RBAC, ABAC, ReBAC
    • DAC — owner grants access, flexible, risk of over-sharing
    • MAC — system-enforced clearance levels, military, TOP SECRET ≥ SECRET ≥ CONFIDENTIAL
    • RBAC — role-based, GRANT SELECT ON table TO role, most common enterprise
    • ABAC — attribute-based, policy language (OPA), fine-grained
    • ReBAC — relationship-based, Google Zanzibar, user:X is editor of doc:Y
    • Zero Trust data — verify every access, microsegmentation, least privilege
    • Principle of least privilege — minimum access needed for role, review regularly
    • Privileged access management (PAM) — CyberArk, BeyondTrust, vault credentials
    • Data masking — non-prod, development, testing environments
    • Tokenization — PCI DSS, payment cards, token vault, Vault by HashiCorp
    • Encryption key management — KMS, HSM, key rotation, envelope encryption
    • Column-level security — grant/deny per column, VPD (Oracle), RLS (PostgreSQL)
    • Row-level security (RLS) — filter rows by user, CREATE POLICY, USING clause
    • Database firewall — monitor SQL, block SQL injection, Imperva, IBM Guardium
    • SQL injection prevention — parameterized queries, ORM, stored procedures, WAF
    • Data loss prevention (DLP) — detect/prevent sensitive data leaving org, Microsoft Purview DLP
    • Security information and event management (SIEM) — aggregate logs, detect threats
    • Database activity monitoring (DAM) — real-time monitoring, anomaly detection
    • Vulnerability scanning — Nessus, Qualys, CVE patching cadence
    • Penetration testing — authorized attack simulation, SQL injection, privilege escalation
    • GDPR data security — Article 32, appropriate technical measures, breach notification 72h
    • Data breach response — detect, contain, notify, remediate, lessons learned

    15. DAMA CDMP — Data Integration & Interoperability

    • Data integration definition — acquiring, filtering, transforming, delivering consistent data
    • ETL — Extract, Transform, Load — transform before load, staging area
    • ELT — Extract, Load, Transform — load raw, transform in target (cloud DW)
    • CDC (Change Data Capture) — log-based, query-based, trigger-based
    • Log-based CDC — Debezium, binlog, WAL, LogMiner (Oracle)
    • Query-based CDC — high-water mark, last_modified polling, intrusive
    • API integration — REST, SOAP, GraphQL, OpenAPI, contract-first
    • Messaging integration — Kafka, MQ, event-driven, loose coupling
    • File-based integration — SFTP, S3, CSV, Parquet, Avro, XML
    • Batch integration — scheduled, full or delta load, ETL jobs
    • Real-time integration — streaming, CDC, event-driven, sub-second latency
    • Near-real-time — micro-batch, 5–60 second lag, Spark Structured Streaming
    • Data virtualization — federated query, no data movement, Denodo, Dremio, Starburst
    • Enterprise Integration Patterns (EIP) — canonical model, message router, translator
    • Canonical data model — enterprise-wide common format, reduces point-to-point
    • Data mapping — source field → target field, transformation rules
    • Transformation types — filter, aggregate, join, split, derive, lookup, format
    • Lookup transformation — enrich with reference data, SCD Type 2 version resolution
    • Slowly Changing Dimension handling — ETL detection, Type 1/2/3 strategies
    • Data lineage tracking — OpenLineage, Apache Atlas, Marquez, DataHub
    • OpenLineage — open standard, Facets, RunEvent, DatasetFacet, JobFacet
    • Data quality in integration — validate on ingest, quarantine bad records, DQ rules
    • Error handling — quarantine, DLQ, alerting, retry, manual review
    • Idempotent loading — safe re-run, MERGE, INSERT IGNORE, ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING
    • Exactly-once delivery — transactional, idempotency key, at-least-once + dedup
    • Schema evolution — backward/forward compatibility, Avro schema registry
    • API versioning — URI, header, media type, deprecation process
    • Data federation — query across sources without moving data, Presto/Trino
    • Data mesh integration — domain APIs as data products, contracts
    • Integration testing — schema validation, volume testing, regression testing

    16. DAMA CDMP — Reference & Master Data Management

    • Reference data (RD) — small, stable, shared lookup values, Country, Currency, Status
    • Master data (MD) — core business entities shared across systems, Customer, Product, Employee
    • Master data management (MDM) — consolidate, govern, and provide single view of master entities
    • MDM styles — Registry, Consolidation, Coexistence, Centralized, Hybrid
    • Registry style — index of pointers, no data movement, lightweight
    • Consolidation style — golden record in MDM hub, read-only for operational
    • Coexistence style — MDM + source systems synchronized, bi-directional
    • Centralized style — MDM is system of record, operational systems consume MDM
    • Hybrid MDM — mix of styles per domain
    • Golden record — best-version master, survived from matching/merging
    • Match and merge — identify duplicates, calculate match score, merge into golden record
    • Matching algorithms — exact match, fuzzy match, phonetic (Soundex, Metaphone), ML-based
    • Jaro-Winkler — string similarity, prefix weighting, name matching
    • Levenshtein distance — edit distance, number of single-character edits
    • Probabilistic matching — Fellegi-Sunter model, threshold, m-probability, u-probability
    • Deterministic matching — rule-based, exact on key fields, no uncertainty
    • Deduplication — identify and consolidate duplicate records
    • Entity resolution — link records representing same real-world entity across sources
    • Survivorship rules — most recent wins, most complete, most trusted source wins
    • Trust scoring — source reliability score, recency score, completeness score
    • Golden record propagation — publish to downstream systems, event or batch
    • Hub-and-spoke MDM — central hub, spoke = source systems
    • MDM reference architecture — source systems → ETL → staging → matching → golden record → distribution
    • MDM tools — Informatica MDM, SAP Master Data Governance, IBM InfoSphere MDM, Reltio, Profisee
    • Product Information Management (PIM) — product master data, attributes, media, channels
    • Customer Data Platform (CDP) — unified customer profile, identity resolution, activation
    • Reference data management — lifecycle governance, DRAFT → APPROVED → DEPRECATED
    • Controlled vocabulary — standardized terms, taxonomy, authority file
    • Taxonomy — hierarchical classification, Category → SubCategory → Product
    • Ontology — formal representation of concepts and relationships, OWL, RDF
    • Code set management — ISO 3166 (country), ISO 4217 (currency), SNOMED CT (medical)
    • Cross-domain reference data — enterprise-wide code sets, single authority
    • Reference data federation — domain-owned, published via API, centrally governed
    • Data steward for master data — accountable for quality, match rules, survivorship

    17. DAMA CDMP — Metadata Management

    • Metadata definition — data about data, context, meaning, structure, provenance
    • Metadata types — business, technical, operational, process, administrative
    • Business metadata — definitions, glossary, ownership, stewardship, business rules
    • Technical metadata — schemas, data types, indexes, constraints, lineage, code
    • Operational metadata — run times, row counts, error counts, SLA metrics
    • Process metadata — ETL job metadata, pipeline runs, performance
    • Administrative metadata — access controls, audit logs, retention
    • Metadata repository — central catalog, search, browse, lineage
    • Data catalog — discoverable, searchable, collaborative, AWS Glue, Apache Atlas, DataHub, Alation, Collibra
    • Active metadata — metadata used at runtime to orchestrate pipelines, enforce policies
    • Passive metadata — documentation, discovery only, not used operationally
    • Metadata standards — Dublin Core, DCAT (Data Catalog Vocabulary), PROV-O, OWL, RDF
    • Dublin Core — 15 elements: title, creator, subject, description, date, type, format…
    • DCAT — W3C standard, dcat:Dataset, dcat:Distribution, dcat:Catalog
    • PROV-O — W3C provenance ontology, prov:Entity, prov:Activity, prov:Agent
    • ISO/IEC 11179 — metadata registry, data element concept, value domain, representation
    • OWL (Web Ontology Language) — formal ontology, classes, properties, restrictions
    • SKOS (Simple Knowledge Organization System) — thesaurus, taxonomy, skos:Concept
    • Metadata harvesting — automated extraction from DBMS, ETL tools, BI tools
    • Metadata scanning — crawl databases/files, extract schema, lineage, profile
    • Data lineage — column-level lineage, impact analysis, where does this data come from
    • Column-level lineage — source.column → transformation → target.column
    • Impact analysis — if I change X, what downstream is affected
    • Data provenance — origin, custody chain, transformations applied
    • Metadata quality — completeness, accuracy, timeliness of metadata
    • Semantic layer — business-friendly metrics, LookML, dbt Semantic Layer, Cube
    • Knowledge graph — entities, relationships, metadata, Neo4j, Amazon Neptune
    • Metadata-driven architecture — pipelines driven by metadata config, not hard-coded
    • Automated metadata management — AI-powered classification, auto-tagging, similarity

    18. DAMA CDMP — Data Quality

    • Data quality definition — fitness for purpose, meets requirements of data consumers
    • Data quality dimensions — completeness, accuracy, consistency, timeliness, validity, uniqueness, integrity
    • Completeness — all required values present, NULL rate, coverage
    • Accuracy — data correctly represents real-world entity, validated against ground truth
    • Consistency — same data element consistent across systems, no contradictions
    • Timeliness — data available when needed, freshness, latency
    • Validity — data conforms to defined format/range/domain
    • Uniqueness — no duplicates within a dataset
    • Integrity — referential integrity, relationship consistency
    • Fitness for purpose — context-dependent, what's quality for one use may not be another
    • Data quality rule — formal expression of expectation, age BETWEEN 0 AND 150
    • Data quality score — aggregate metric per dataset, pass/fail/warning
    • Data profiling — statistical analysis, null %, distinct values, min/max, distribution
    • Column profiling — cardinality, nullability, min/max/avg, pattern analysis
    • Cross-column profiling — correlation, conditional distribution, dependency
    • Cross-dataset profiling — referential integrity check, consistency across systems
    • Data quality tools — Great Expectations, Deequ (Amazon), Soda, Monte Carlo, Informatica DQ
    • Great Expectations — Expectation, ExpectationSuite, Checkpoint, DataDocs
    • Deequ — VerificationSuite, Check, Constraint, Spark-based
    • Data observability — monitor pipelines for quality issues, Monte Carlo, Acceldata
    • Data SLA — timeliness, quality thresholds, availability commitments
    • Root cause analysis — trace quality issue to source, lineage traversal
    • Data quality remediation — fix at source, ETL cleansing, steward review
    • Master data quality — matching completeness, survivorship accuracy
    • Quality gate — pipeline checkpoint, fail-fast on quality breach
    • Anomaly detection — statistical, ML-based, Z-score, IQR, Isolation Forest
    • Schema drift — unexpected schema change, data type change, new/missing column
    • Volume anomaly — row count drop/spike, expected_rows BETWEEN lower AND upper
    • Freshness check — MAX(updated_at) < NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour'
    • Data quality dashboard — trend, per domain, per dataset, per owner
    • Data quality as a product — owned by domain, SLA published, measured externally
    • TDQM (Total Data Quality Management) — MIT TDQM framework, product view, service view
    • IQ (Information Quality) — Huang, Lee & Wang dimensions, 15 IQ dimensions

    19. DAMA CDMP — Data Warehousing & BI

    • Data warehouse definition — subject-oriented, integrated, non-volatile, time-variant (Inmon)
    • Inmon vs Kimball — EDW normalized (3NF) top-down vs dimensional bottom-up
    • Operational Data Store (ODS) — near-real-time, operational reporting, not historical
    • Data mart — departmental subset, conformed or independent
    • EDW (Enterprise Data Warehouse) — integrated, historical, single version of truth
    • MPP (Massively Parallel Processing) — Redshift, Snowflake, BigQuery, Synapse
    • Columnar storage — Parquet, ORC, columnar scan, compression, predicate pushdown
    • Star schema — see §5, Kimball, fact + dimension
    • Slowly Changing Dimension — see §5, Type 0–6
    • ETL pipeline — extract from ODS/source, transform in staging, load to DW
    • Staging area — temporary landing zone, raw data, no transformations yet
    • Persistent staging — keep history of loads, audit, reprocess
    • Data vault — see §7, auditable, scalable, raw + business vault
    • OBT (One Big Table) — fully denormalized, analytics-optimized, Databricks pattern
    • BI tools — Tableau, Power BI, Looker, Qlik, MicroStrategy, Superset
    • Semantic layer / BI layer — metric definitions, LookML (Looker), DAX (Power BI)
    • OLAP cube — pre-aggregated, MDX queries, SSAS, Essbase
    • MOLAP — multidimensional OLAP, proprietary cube, fast query
    • ROLAP — relational OLAP, query on RDBMS, flexible
    • HOLAP — hybrid, aggregates in cube, detail in RDBMS
    • DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) — Power BI formula language, CALCULATE, FILTER, ALL
    • MDX (Multidimensional Expressions) — OLAP query language, [Measures].[Sales Amount]
    • SQL analytics functions — ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG, LEAD, NTILE
    • Window functions — OVER (PARTITION BY … ORDER BY … ROWS/RANGE BETWEEN …)
    • CTE (Common Table Expression) — WITH cte AS (…), recursive CTE for hierarchies
    • Materialized view — pre-computed, auto-refresh, REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW
    • Query federation — Athena, Presto/Trino, query across heterogeneous stores
    • Data freshness — DW update frequency, batch daily vs streaming near-real-time
    • Change data capture for DW — CDC → staging → SCD Type 2 logic
    • Aggregate navigator — route queries to aggregate tables, transparent to BI tool
    • Slowly Changing Measure — changing fact values, accumulating snapshot

    20. DCAM (Data Management Capability Assessment Model)

    • DCAM overview — EDM Council framework, assess data management maturity
    • DCAM purpose — financial industry origin, now cross-industry, capability assessment
    • DCAM components — 8 capability areas, sub-capabilities, capability statements
    • 8 DCAM Capability Areas — Data Management Strategy, Data Architecture, Data Technology Architecture, Data Governance, Data Quality, Data Operations, Data Risk & Compliance, Stakeholder Management
    • Capability statement — observable, measurable practice at a capability level
    • DCAM maturity levels — 1-Initial, 2-Developing, 3-Defined, 4-Managed, 5-Optimized
    • Data Management Strategy — vision, mission, objectives, roadmap, funding model
    • Strategy alignment — data strategy aligned with business strategy, measurable outcomes
    • Business case for data management — ROI, risk reduction, regulatory compliance
    • Data architecture capability — conceptual, logical, physical models, standards, principles
    • Canonical data model — enterprise-wide common model, integration backbone
    • Data Technology Architecture — tech stack, tools, infrastructure, cloud strategy
    • Data platform reference architecture — ingest, store, process, serve, govern
    • Tool rationalization — consolidate overlapping tools, license cost, skills
    • Data Governance capability — policies, standards, stewardship, decision rights
    • Governance operating model — centralized vs federated, council structure
    • Data policy hierarchy — enterprise policy → domain policy → process policy
    • Data Quality capability — DQ dimensions, profiling, rules, remediation, monitoring
    • Data quality program — systematic, measured, continuously improved
    • Data Operations capability — data lifecycle, pipelines, SLAs, incident management
    • Operational metadata — job runs, row counts, latency, error rates
    • Data Risk & Compliance capability — regulatory inventory, controls, audit readiness
    • Risk register — data risks identified, likelihood, impact, mitigation, owner
    • Audit readiness — evidence of controls, BCBS 239, SOX 302/404, DORA
    • BCBS 239 — Basel Committee, risk data aggregation, 14 principles, financial sector
    • BCBS 239 principles — governance, data architecture, accuracy, completeness, timeliness, adaptability, resolution data, risk reporting
    • Stakeholder Management capability — executive sponsorship, change management, communication
    • Data literacy — organization-wide understanding of data, training programs
    • DCAM assessment process — survey, evidence collection, scoring, benchmarking, roadmap
    • DCAM gap analysis — current vs target capability, prioritized improvement actions
    • DCAM benchmark — compare against industry peers, EDM Council database
    • DCAM certification — organizational assessment by EDM Council, formal certification

    21. CIMP (Certified Information Management Professional)

    • CIMP overview — AIIM certification, information management, unstructured + structured
    • AIIM — Association for Intelligent Information Management
    • Information management scope — documents, records, content, processes, data
    • CIMP domains — Information Strategy, Information Governance, Records Management, Content Management, Process Improvement, Technology & Tools
    • Information lifecycle — capture, manage, store, preserve, deliver, dispose
    • Document management — version control, check-in/check-out, metadata, search
    • Records management — declare record, retention schedule, disposition, legal hold
    • Record — fixed, final, authentic, reliable document with business/legal value
    • Retention schedule — per record type, legal/regulatory requirement, business need
    • Legal hold — suspend normal disposition, litigation, eDiscovery
    • eDiscovery — electronic discovery, search, collect, review for legal proceedings
    • Disposition — destroy, transfer, permanent preservation after retention period
    • Content management system (CMS) — SharePoint, Alfresco, Documentum, OpenText
    • Enterprise Content Management (ECM) — capture, manage, store, preserve, deliver content
    • Digital asset management (DAM) — media files, images, video, brand assets
    • Business process management (BPM) — model, automate, monitor processes, BPMN
    • BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation) — tasks, gateways, events, pools, lanes
    • Workflow — sequence of steps, approval routing, escalation
    • Robotic Process Automation (RPA) — automate repetitive tasks, UiPath, Automation Anywhere
    • Intelligent Document Processing (IDP) — OCR, ML extraction, classification, validation
    • OCR (Optical Character Recognition) — scanned documents to text, Tesseract, AWS Textract
    • Natural Language Processing (NLP) — entity extraction, classification, summarization
    • Knowledge management — capture, organize, share organizational knowledge
    • Taxonomy and classification — controlled vocabulary, auto-classification, ML tagging
    • Search and retrieval — enterprise search, relevance, faceted search, Elasticsearch
    • Information architecture (IA) — organize, label, navigation, findability
    • Information governance — policies, accountability, risk, value, compliance
    • IG maturity model — awareness, reactive, proactive, strategic, transformational
    • SharePoint governance — site policies, lifecycle, taxonomy, permissions
    • Cloud content management — Box, Google Drive, OneDrive, governance in cloud
    • Email management — auto-classification, retention, eDiscovery, journaling
    • Social media records — capture, preserve, governance of social content
    • Privacy in content — PII in documents, redaction, GDPR compliance
    • Audit trail — who accessed, modified, deleted what document/record, when
    • Chain of custody — evidence handling, tamper-evident, legal admissibility

    22. TOGAF (The Open Group Architecture Framework)

    • TOGAF overview — enterprise architecture framework, Open Group standard
    • TOGAF versions — TOGAF 9.2 (current stable), TOGAF 10 (new structure)
    • Architecture Development Method (ADM) — iterative, phased EA development process
    • ADM phases — Preliminary → A (Vision) → B (Business) → C (Information Systems) → D (Technology) → E (Opportunities) → F (Migration Planning) → G (Implementation Governance) → H (Architecture Change Management) → Requirements Management (central)
    • Preliminary Phase — customization, framework adoption, governance, principles
    • Architecture principles — business continuity, data is an asset, technology independence
    • Phase A — Architecture Vision — scope, stakeholders, concerns, high-level vision, Statement of Architecture Work
    • Statement of Architecture Work — scope, schedule, resource, acceptance criteria
    • Architecture vision document — high-level aspirational future state
    • Phase B — Business Architecture — business strategy, governance, organization, processes
    • Business capability map — Plan, Acquire, Manufacture, Sell, Deliver, Support
    • Value stream — end-to-end flow of activities creating value for customer
    • Business motivation model (BMM) — ends, means, influencers, assessments
    • Phase C — Information Systems Architecture — Data + Application architectures
    • Data architecture (TOGAF) — data entities, relationships, principles, data models
    • Application architecture — application components, interactions, standards
    • Application portfolio — inventory, lifecycle, capability mapping, rationalization
    • Phase D — Technology Architecture — hardware, software, network, infrastructure
    • Technology standards — approved technologies, versions, lifecycle status
    • Phase E — Opportunities & Solutions — identify work packages, gaps, solutions
    • Gap analysis — baseline vs target, capability gaps, technology gaps
    • Work packages — discrete deliverables, transition architectures
    • Phase F — Migration Planning — roadmap, project portfolio, priorities
    • Architecture roadmap — sequenced work packages, timelines, milestones
    • Transition architecture — intermediate state architectures toward target
    • Phase G — Implementation Governance — oversee implementation, compliance
    • Architecture compliance review — check implementation against architecture
    • Architecture board — governance body, approve/reject architecture decisions
    • Phase H — Architecture Change Management — monitor, assess change requests
    • Architecture change request — trigger for ADM re-entry, impact assessment
    • Architecture Repository — store for all architecture artifacts
    • Architecture Repository layers — Architecture Metamodel, Architecture Capability, Architecture Landscape, Standards Information Base, Reference Library, Governance Log
    • Architecture Landscape — Strategic, Segment, Capability architectures
    • Strategic architecture — long-range direction, 5+ years
    • Segment architecture — business area, 2–5 years
    • Capability architecture — specific capability, 1–2 years
    • Content Framework — artifacts, deliverables, building blocks organized by metamodel
    • Architecture Building Block (ABB) — generic, reusable component
    • Solution Building Block (SBB) — specific implementation of ABB
    • Deliverable — contractual output, formally reviewed
    • Artifact — architectural work product — matrix, diagram, catalog
    • Views and Viewpoints — stakeholder concerns addressed by structured views
    • Viewpoint — template, rules for creating a view
    • View — representation of architecture from specific perspective
    • Stakeholder — individual with interest in outcome, concern-based engagement
    • Architectural Significant Requirements (ASR) — non-functional, quality attributes
    • TOGAF ADM + TOGAF 10 — modular, updated content, Standards Architecture Guide
    • Enterprise Continuum — Foundation Architectures → Common Systems → Industry → Organization
    • Architecture Patterns — proven solutions in Enterprise Continuum
    • Foundation Architecture — TOGAF Foundation, Technical Reference Model (TRM)
    • Technical Reference Model (TRM) — generic services, open standards
    • Standards Information Base (SIB) — approved standards, IEEE, ISO, W3C
    • Architecture Maturity Model — CMMI-based, levels 0–5
    • Architecture Governance Framework — contracts, compliance, dispensations, audit
    • Architecture Contract — joint agreement, deliverables, quality, fitness
    • Architecture Compliance — irrelevant, consistent, compliant, conformant, fully conformant
    • TOGAF certifications — Level 1 (Foundation), Level 2 (Certified), Combined exam
    • ADM guidelines — security architecture, SOA, agile enterprise architecture
    • TOGAF + Agile — Architecture Runway, just-enough architecture, iteration alignment
    • TOGAF + ITIL — service management alignment, service catalog mapping
    • TOGAF + COBIT — IT governance alignment, control objectives
    • TOGAF + Zachman — complementary, TOGAF process + Zachman structure

    23. Zachman Framework

    • Zachman Framework overview — enterprise architecture ontology, John Zachman 1987
    • Ontology — complete, definitive schema for describing any complex object
    • Not a methodology — no process, no sequence, descriptive not prescriptive
    • 6 × 6 matrix — 6 perspectives × 6 interrogatives = 36 cells
    • 6 Perspectives (rows) — Executive (Scope), Business Management (Business Concepts), Architect (System Logic), Engineer (Technology Physics), Technician (Component Assemblies), User (Operations Instances)
    • Row 1 — Executive (Scope/Contextual) — planner view, ballpark, context
    • Row 2 — Business Management (Enterprise/Conceptual) — owner view, business concepts
    • Row 3 — Architect (System/Logical) — designer view, logical design
    • Row 4 — Engineer (Technology/Physical) — builder view, physical design
    • Row 5 — Technician (Detailed/Component) — sub-contractor view, out-of-context components
    • Row 6 — User (Functioning Enterprise/Actual) — working instances, deployed system
    • 6 Interrogatives (columns) — What, How, Where, Who, When, Why
    • What (Inventory/Data) — things that matter, entities, data, objects
    • How (Process/Function) — processes, functions, transformations, how things work
    • Where (Distribution/Network) — locations, geography, network topology
    • Who (Responsibility/People) — people, organizations, roles, responsibilities
    • When (Timing/Time) — timing, schedule, events, lifecycle
    • Why (Motivation/Motivation) — goals, strategy, business rules, motivations
    • Cell naming — Row 2 × What = Business Data Model, Row 3 × How = Application Architecture
    • Row 2 × What — Semantic Model / Business Data Model
    • Row 3 × What — Logical Data Model
    • Row 4 × What — Physical Data Model
    • Row 5 × What — Table/Object definitions (DDL, Class definitions)
    • Row 2 × How — Business Process Model
    • Row 3 × How — Application Architecture / Data Flow Diagram
    • Row 4 × How — System Design / Program Specifications
    • Row 2 × Why — Business Goals/Strategy / Motivation Model
    • Row 3 × Why — Business Rules Model
    • Primitive model — single cell, one perspective one interrogative, no mixing
    • Composite model — multiple cells combined, implementation artifacts
    • Transformation rules — moving from row to row, column to column is transformation not the thing itself
    • No implicit dependencies between columns — each column is independent
    • Completeness — if cell is empty, unknown or not designed, not just undocumented
    • Uniqueness — each cell contains a unique type of artifact, no overlap
    • Universal — applies to any complex object, not just IT
    • Zachman v3 — 2011 update, renamed rows, cells have descriptive names
    • Zachman + TOGAF — Zachman = what (ontology), TOGAF = how (process)
    • Zachman + FEAF — FEAF provides federal government application of Zachman-like structure
    • Zachman use cases — scope definition, communication, impact analysis, completeness check
    • Inverse vs Integrated — Zachman warns against mixing perspectives

    24. FEAF (Federal Enterprise Architecture Framework)

    • FEAF overview — US Federal Government EA framework, OMB mandate
    • FEAF history — FEAF 1.0 (1999) → FEA (2002–2012) → FEAF-II (2013) → FEAF 2 (current)
    • Purpose — guide EA development across federal agencies, facilitate collaboration
    • Federal CIO Council — sponsors FEAF, oversees federal IT strategy
    • OMB Circular A-130 — federal information resources management
    • FITARA (Federal IT Acquisition Reform Act) — CIO authority, IT investment oversight
    • FEAF Consolidated Reference Model (CRM) — 6 reference models
    • 6 Reference Models — Performance RM, Business RM, Data RM, Application RM, Infrastructure RM, Security RM
    • Performance Reference Model (PRM) — IT investment performance, outcomes, measures
    • Business Reference Model (BRM) — government functions, Lines of Business (LOB)
    • Lines of Business — Defense, Health, Financial Management, Human Resources, Justice
    • Data Reference Model (DRM) — data standardization, sharing, data assets
    • DRM components — Data Description, Data Context, Data Sharing
    • Application Reference Model (ARM) — IT services, components, service areas
    • Infrastructure Reference Model (IRM) — platforms, networks, hosting, TIC (Trusted Internet Connection)
    • Security Reference Model (SRM) — security controls, FISMA, NIST SP 800-53
    • FISMA (Federal Information Security Management Act) — security requirements, C&A
    • FedRAMP — cloud security authorization, Authorized, In Process, FedRAMP+
    • NIST SP 800-53 — security and privacy controls, control families
    • FEAF Practice Guidance — four sub-architectures: business, data, application, technology
    • Business Architecture (FEAF) — mission, vision, strategy, services, processes
    • Data Architecture (FEAF) — data assets, data flows, master data, standards
    • Application Architecture (FEAF) — application inventory, E-Gov initiatives
    • Technology Architecture (FEAF) — infrastructure, network, platforms, standards
    • FEAF segments — Core Mission Area, Business Services, Enterprise Services
    • Segment architecture — sub-set of EA, specific mission area
    • EA assessment framework (EAAF) — measure EA maturity, 1–5 scale
    • EA management plan — document EA governance, process, artifact plan
    • EA repository — central store, agency artifacts, CRM mappings
    • Shared services — USA.gov, Login.gov, FedPay, reduce duplication
    • E-Government initiatives — e-Authentication, Geospatial One-Stop, cross-agency
    • Cloud Smart — cloud adoption strategy, security, procurement, workforce
    • Digital modernization — legacy migration, API-first, user-centered design
    • FEAF + TOGAF — agencies use TOGAF ADM with FEAF reference models
    • Capital Planning and Investment Control (CPIC) — IT investment lifecycle, OMB Exhibit 300
    • IT Dashboard — public IT investment tracking, usaspending.gov, red/yellow/green

    25. DoDAF (Department of Defense Architecture Framework)

    • DoDAF overview — US DoD EA framework, MIL-HDBK-3950
    • DoDAF purpose — describe, analyze, visualize defense architectures, acquisition support
    • DoDAF versions — DoDAF 1.0 → 1.5 → DoDAF 2.0 (current), DM2 (DoDAF Meta-Model)
    • Architecture description — collection of products (views) describing architecture
    • Six Viewpoints (DoDAF 2.0) — All Viewpoint (AV), Capability Viewpoint (CV), Data & Information Viewpoint (DIV), Operational Viewpoint (OV), Project Viewpoint (PV), Services Viewpoint (SvcV), Standards Viewpoint (StdV), Systems Viewpoint (SV)
    • All Viewpoint (AV) — overview, scope, AV-1 Overview, AV-2 Integrated Dictionary
    • AV-1 Overview & Summary — scope, purpose, intended users, environment, key findings
    • AV-2 Integrated Dictionary — definitions of all terms used in architecture
    • Capability Viewpoint (CV) — capabilities required, CV-1 Vision, CV-2 Taxonomy
    • CV-1 Vision — strategic context, capability requirements
    • CV-2 Capability Taxonomy — hierarchy of capabilities
    • CV-3 Capability Phasing — timeline for capability development
    • Data & Information Viewpoint (DIV) — data requirements, logical/physical data
    • DIV-1 Conceptual Data Model — high-level data concepts and relationships
    • DIV-2 Logical Data Model — detailed entities, attributes, relationships
    • DIV-3 Physical Data Model — DBMS-specific, storage structures
    • Operational Viewpoint (OV) — operational activities, information flows
    • OV-1 High-Level Operational Concept — graphical overview of operations
    • OV-2 Operational Resource Flow Description — resource flows between nodes
    • OV-3 Operational Resource Flow Matrix — resource + needline matrix
    • OV-4 Organizational Relationships — command relationships, org chart
    • OV-5a/5b Activity Model — IDEF0 activity hierarchy, process flows
    • OV-6a Operational Rules — business rules governing operations
    • OV-6b State Transition Description — FSM for operational objects
    • OV-6c Event-Trace Description — sequence diagram of operational events
    • Services Viewpoint (SvcV) — service interactions, lines of development
    • SvcV-1 Services Context Description — services, service connections
    • SvcV-4 Services Functionality Description — service function hierarchy
    • Systems Viewpoint (SV) — legacy systems (deprecated in DoDAF 2.0, use SvcV)
    • Standards Viewpoint (StdV) — standards, policies applying to architecture
    • StdV-1 Standards Profile — applicable standards per technology area
    • StdV-2 Standards Forecast — emerging standards, retirement dates
    • Project Viewpoint (PV) — projects, milestones, relationships to capability
    • PV-1 Project Portfolio Relationships — projects enabling capabilities
    • DoDAF Meta-Model (DM2) — formal metamodel, UML, ontology, machine-readable
    • UPDM (Unified Profile for DoDAF and MODAF) — UML/SysML profile for both
    • MODAF — UK Ministry of Defence Architecture Framework, similar to DoDAF
    • NAF (NATO Architecture Framework) — NATO version, NATO Architecture Views
    • DODAF + TOGAF — DoDAF for defense-specific, TOGAF for commercial mapping
    • JCIDS (Joint Capabilities Integration and Development System) — requirements process
    • Defense Acquisition System (DAS) — program acquisition, Milestone A/B/C
    • Mission Thread — end-to-end operational scenario, KPP (Key Performance Parameter)
    • Architecture-Centric Acquisition — EA drives requirements, contracts, testing
    • System of Systems (SoS) — multiple independent systems, emergent behavior
    • Net-Centric — information sharing, GIG (Global Information Grid), interoperability

    26. Gartner Enterprise Architecture

    • Gartner EA overview — practitioner-focused, business outcome driven, pragmatic
    • Gartner EA definition — translates business vision and strategy into effective enterprise change
    • EA as a practice — not just documentation, drives business outcomes
    • Gartner EA framework components — Business Architecture, Information Architecture, Technology Architecture, Solution Architecture
    • Gartner Magic Quadrant — EA tools — Leaders, Challengers, Visionaries, Niche Players
    • EA tools (Gartner leaders) — LeanIX, Ardoq, Alfabet (Software AG), Sparx Enterprise Architect, Mega HOPEX
    • Business Architecture (Gartner) — capabilities, value streams, operating model
    • Business Capability Map — Plan, Build, Run, Market, Deliver — common taxonomy
    • Operating Model — how org delivers value, standardization vs integration matrix
    • Operating Model quadrants — Diversification (low std/low int), Coordination (low std/high int), Replication (high std/low int), Unification (high std/high int)
    • Business Motivation Model — ends (vision, goals, objectives), means (mission, strategy, tactics)
    • Value chain analysis — Porter's Value Chain, primary + support activities
    • Information Architecture (Gartner) — data assets, information flows, MDM, governance
    • Logical Information Architecture — domains, entities, relationships, canonical model
    • Physical Information Architecture — platforms, databases, integration patterns
    • Information Supply Chain — source → integrate → govern → serve → consume
    • Technology Architecture (Gartner) — infrastructure, platforms, APIs, cloud strategy
    • Technology Lifecycle Management — Current, Strategic, Sunset, Emerging
    • Technology Radar — adopt, trial, assess, hold — ThoughtWorks inspired
    • Cloud Architecture — multi-cloud, hybrid cloud, landing zones, governance
    • Pace Layering — Systems of Record, Systems of Differentiation, Systems of Innovation
    • Pace Layers — different lifecycle speeds, governance levels, investment rates
    • Solution Architecture — project-level, fills gap between EA and delivery
    • Solution architecture patterns — reusable, vetted, standard approaches
    • Architecture Review Board (ARB) — approve/reject solution architectures vs EA standards
    • EA Governance (Gartner) — principles, standards, decision rights, compliance, exceptions
    • Governance operating model — centralized ARB, federated domain architects, advisory
    • EA maturity (Gartner) — Level 0 (No Process), Level 1 (Initial), Level 2 (Under Development), Level 3 (Defined), Level 4 (Managed), Level 5 (Optimizing)
    • Business Value of EA — faster time-to-market, lower IT costs, risk reduction, agility
    • EA metrics — application rationalization savings, project on-time delivery, tech debt reduction
    • Gartner Hype Cycle — innovation trigger → peak of inflated expectations → trough of disillusionment → slope of enlightenment → plateau of productivity
    • Gartner Critical Capabilities — product evaluation, use case scoring
    • Bimodal IT — Mode 1 (stable, reliable), Mode 2 (exploratory, agile) — Gartner concept
    • Composable Enterprise — modular, packaged business capabilities, recomposable
    • Packaged Business Capabilities (PBC) — MACH architecture, microservices, API-first, cloud-native, headless
    • MACH Architecture — Microservices, API-first, Cloud-native, Headless
    • Total Experience (TX) — multi-experience + customer experience + employee experience
    • Mesh App and Service Architecture (MASA) — interconnected, distributed apps
    • Intelligent Composable Business — adaptable, augmented with AI, decision intelligence
    • Data Fabric — unified data management, active metadata, AI-powered integration
    • Data Mesh — domain ownership, product thinking — Gartner endorses as pattern

    27. Enterprise Architecture Governance

    • EA governance definition — system by which EA activities are directed and controlled
    • Governance objectives — alignment, value delivery, risk management, resource optimization
    • Governance vs management — governance = oversight/direction, management = execution
    • EA governance framework — principles, policies, standards, processes, roles, metrics
    • Architecture principles — high-level statements, Data is an asset, Reuse before buy before build
    • Architecture standards — specific technology, pattern, naming, format requirements
    • Architecture patterns catalog — approved reusable solutions, microservices, event-driven
    • Architecture decision log — ADRs, rationale, alternatives considered, consequences
    • Architecture Review Board (ARB) — governance body, review/approve architectures
    • ARB charter — mandate, authority, membership, cadence, decision rights
    • ARB process — submission → review → decision (approve/reject/conditional) → monitor
    • Architecture compliance — verify implementations conform to approved architecture
    • Compliance levels — Irrelevant, Consistent, Compliant, Conformant, Fully Conformant (TOGAF)
    • Dispensation / Exception — approved deviation from standard, time-bounded, rationale
    • Exception register — track all approved exceptions, expiry, owner
    • Architecture debt — technical debt at architecture level, accumulation over time
    • Architecture fitness functions — automated architecture tests, ArchUnit, Driftwood
    • Architecture drift — implementation diverges from intended architecture over time
    • Architecture erosion — cumulative drift, quality degradation
    • Governance artifacts — principles catalog, standards catalog, pattern library, ADR log, exception register
    • Technology lifecycle management — Emerging, Strategic, Current, Contained, Sunset, Retired
    • Application rationalization — reduce duplication, total cost of ownership, TIME model
    • TIME model — Tolerate, Invest, Migrate, Eliminate — application portfolio disposition
    • EA operating model — how EA function operates day-to-day, team structure
    • EA team structure — Enterprise Architect, Domain Architects (Business/Data/App/Tech), Solution Architects
    • Domain architect — specialize in one domain, drive standards, advise projects
    • Solution architect — project-level, bridge EA to delivery, solution design
    • Embedded architect — in delivery teams, architecture runway, guardrails
    • Architecture community of practice — cross-team, knowledge sharing, pattern validation
    • Architecture office hours — open forum, advise teams, early engagement
    • Early architectural engagement — engage before project design, shape not review
    • Architecture Runway — just-enough architecture ahead of delivery, SAFe concept
    • SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) EA — Agile Architecture, Intentional Architecture, Emergent Design
    • Intentional Architecture — planned, cross-cutting, long-term, enables agility
    • Emergent Design — evolve within sprint, team-level, tactical
    • Architecture Runway (SAFe) — existing implementation + infrastructure sufficient for near-term features
    • Fitness for Purpose review — is current architecture fit, what needs to change
    • Architecture Kanban — backlog of architecture work, WIP limits, flow
    • Architecture sprint — dedicated sprint for architectural work, spike, proof of concept
    • Proof of Concept (PoC) — validate architectural decision, time-boxed, throwaway
    • Spike — investigation, unknown resolution, produces knowledge not software
    • Architecture debt backlog — catalogue of architectural issues, prioritized
    • Technical debt register — item, impact, effort to fix, age, owner, priority
    • EA tooling — LeanIX, Ardoq, Sparx EA, Mega HOPEX, Alfabet, BiZZdesign, Avolution
    • LeanIX — cloud-native, Application Portfolio Management, Technology Risk, Fact Sheets
    • Ardoq — dynamic, Component, Process, Capability models, scenario analysis
    • Capability-based planning — invest in capabilities not projects, Business Capability Map
    • Portfolio management — align IT investments to strategy, PPM (Project Portfolio Management)
    • Investment lifecycle — Plan → Fund → Execute → Benefit Realization
    • Benefits realization — track actual vs expected benefits, post-implementation review
    • Roadmap governance — EA roadmap, milestone tracking, dependency management
    • Architecture risk management — identify, assess, mitigate architectural risks
    • Risk taxonomy — technical risk, integration risk, data risk, security risk, compliance risk
    • EA metrics / KPIs — application rationalization rate, standard compliance %, time-to-architecture-review, architecture debt age
    • EA value demonstration — business outcomes linked to EA decisions, case studies
    • Stakeholder engagement — CIO, CDO, business leaders, development teams, auditors
    • EA communication plan — audience, message, channel, frequency, feedback loop
    • Architecture storytelling — narrative, business context, why before how
    • Change management for EA — resistance, adoption, sponsorship, quick wins
    • EA and digital transformation — enabler, guardrail, agility vs governance balance
    • EA and cloud adoption — cloud strategy, migration patterns, landing zones, guardrails
    • EA and AI/ML — AI architecture patterns, responsible AI, MLOps governance
    • EA and API economy — API strategy, API governance, API Product thinking
    • EA and security — zero trust alignment, threat modelling, security architecture review
    • EA certification landscape — TOGAF, Zachman, FEAF, IASA CITA, BCS EA, AWS/Azure/GCP Solutions Architect
    • IASA CITA — IT Architect Association, CITA-A (Associate), CITA-P (Professional), CITA-F (Fellow)
    • Open CA — Open Group Certified Architect program, TOGAF-aligned

    28. Data Modelling & Architecture Tooling

    • Modelling tools — erwin Data Modeler, IBM InfoSphere Data Architect, SAP PowerDesigner, ER/Studio, Hackolade
    • erwin Data Modeler — logical/physical, forward/reverse engineering, erwin Mart
    • PowerDesigner — SAP, conceptual/logical/physical, impact analysis, metadata
    • ER/Studio — IDERA, Data Architect, Business Architect, team repository
    • Hackolade — NoSQL and polyglot, MongoDB, Cassandra, DynamoDB, Avro, JSON Schema
    • dbdiagram.io — lightweight, DBML language, quick conceptual diagrams
    • DataGrip — JetBrains, multi-DBMS, ER diagrams, query console
    • DBeaver — open-source, ER diagrams, ERD from schema
    • Lucidchart / draw.io — general diagramming, ER templates
    • EA tools — LeanIX, Ardoq, Sparx Enterprise Architect, Mega HOPEX, Alfabet, BiZZdesign
    • Sparx Enterprise Architect — UML, ArchiMate, TOGAF, Zachman, BPMN, SysML
    • ArchiMate — EA modelling language, Open Group standard
    • ArchiMate layers — Strategy, Business, Application, Technology, Physical, Implementation & Migration
    • ArchiMate aspects — Active Structure, Behavior, Passive Structure
    • ArchiMate relationships — Association, Composition, Aggregation, Realization, Serving, Access, Influence, Triggering, Flow, Specialization, Assignment
    • ArchiMate viewpoints — Organization, Business Process, Application Cooperation, Technology, Motivation
    • BPMN tools — Camunda Modeler, Signavio, Bizagi, Visio
    • Data catalog tools — Collibra, Alation, DataHub, Apache Atlas, AWS Glue, Purview
    • Collibra — enterprise governance, Workflow, Policy Manager, Data Intelligence Cloud
    • Alation — AI-powered catalog, Open Data Quality, Trust Flags
    • DataHub — LinkedIn open-source, GMS, Metadata Events, lineage graph
    • Apache Atlas — open-source, Hadoop ecosystem, classification, lineage
    • Data lineage tools — OpenLineage, Marquez, DataHub, Spline (Spark)
    • Data quality tools — Informatica DQ, Talend DQ, Great Expectations, Deequ, Soda, Monte Carlo
    • MDM tools — Informatica MDM, SAP MDG, IBM InfoSphere MDM, Reltio, Profisee, Semarchy
    • Integration tools — MuleSoft, Dell Boomi, Azure Data Factory, Talend, Informatica IICS
    • Metadata standards tools — Protege (ontology), VocBench (SKOS), TopBraid Composer
    • Version control for data models — Git with model exports, erwin Mart, PowerDesigner repository
    • Model-driven architecture (MDA) — OMG standard, PIM (Platform Independent Model), PSM (Platform Specific Model)
    • CIM (Computation Independent Model) — business view, no implementation detail

    29. Staff / Principal Engineering Competencies

    • Enterprise data strategy — vision, principles, capabilities, roadmap, funding model
    • Data strategy alignment — link data strategy to business strategy, measurable outcomes
    • Operating model design — how data management function operates, roles, processes
    • Build vs buy vs partner — data platform, tooling, capability decisions
    • Total cost of ownership (TCO) — license, implementation, support, training, migration
    • Data platform architecture decision — lakehouse vs DW vs data lake, trade-off analysis
    • Multi-cloud data architecture — vendor lock-in, portability, Iceberg as common format
    • Data mesh vs data fabric — federated ownership vs unified layer, decision criteria
    • EA framework selection — TOGAF vs Zachman vs custom, context-appropriate
    • ADR (Architecture Decision Records) — data architecture decisions, lineage, rationale
    • Architecture governance program — establish ARB, principles, standards, compliance process
    • Technology rationalization program — TIME model, application portfolio, license optimization
    • Data governance program launch — sponsorship, operating model, quick wins, roadmap
    • Master data program — domain prioritization, MDM tool selection, match/merge rules
    • Data quality program — dimensions, tooling, ownership, SLA, remediation workflow
    • Metadata management program — catalog, lineage, glossary, stewardship
    • Privacy program — GDPR compliance, DPO, DSAR workflow, consent management
    • Data classification program — policy, tooling, automation, enforcement
    • Data literacy program — training, certification, data champions, culture
    • Data product thinking — discoverable, trustworthy, addressable, interoperable, valuable
    • Data contract adoption — schema, SLO, ownership, tooling (soda-core, Great Expectations)
    • Stakeholder management — executive communication, board reporting, business case
    • Capability maturity assessment — DCAM, DAMA, internal maturity model, gap analysis
    • EA maturity improvement — roadmap, quick wins, capability targets, governance evolution
    • Hiring & calibration — data architect, EA, data modeller — interview rubrics
    • Mentorship — data modelling reviews, architecture office hours, learning paths
    • Industry standards contribution — DAMA, OMG, ISO working groups
    • Certification roadmap — CDMP, DCAM, TOGAF 10, CIMP, Zachman, IASA CITA-P

    30. System Design — Data & Architecture Scenarios

    • Designing an enterprise data architecture — subject areas, canonical model, integration patterns, governance
    • Designing a data mesh platform — domain data products, self-serve infra, federated governance, contracts
    • Designing an MDM solution — domain selection, match/merge algorithm, golden record, distribution
    • Designing a bi-temporal data model — valid time + transaction time, query patterns, SQL:2011
    • Designing a Data Vault 2.0 warehouse — hubs, links, satellites, business vault, PIT, bridge
    • Designing a governed data lake — zone architecture, metadata, classification, access control
    • Designing a metadata-driven ETL framework — config-driven, lineage, reusable patterns
    • Designing a data quality framework — profiling, rules engine, quarantine, remediation, SLA
    • Designing an enterprise glossary — term lifecycle, stewardship, tooling, integration with catalog
    • Designing a data lineage solution — OpenLineage, catalog integration, column-level lineage
    • Designing a privacy-by-design system — data minimization, consent, anonymization, erasure
    • Designing an EA governance model — ARB process, compliance levels, exception handling, metrics
    • Designing a TOGAF ADM for a digital transformation — phases, artifacts, roadmap, governance
    • Designing a DoDAF architecture — capability viewpoint, operational viewpoint, data viewpoint
    • Designing a dimensional model for retail — star schema, grain, SCDs, factless facts, bus matrix
    • Designing a NoSQL data model for DynamoDB — access patterns, single-table, GSI overloading
    • Designing a canonical data model — enterprise entities, integration points, schema registry
    • Designing a multi-tenant data architecture — isolation strategies, shared vs silo, row-level security
    • Designing a real-time data platform — Kafka → Flink → Delta → serving, data contracts, governance
    • Designing a data platform capability roadmap — current state, target state, DCAM assessment, priorities

    Enterprise Architecture

    1. Business Architecture

    Strategy & Planning

    • Business capability modeling — Defines what an organization does (not how), mapping capabilities to strategic goals; used to identify gaps and investment priorities.
    • Value stream mapping — Visualizes the end-to-end flow of value delivery to the customer, highlighting waste and bottlenecks.
    • Business motivation model (goals, objectives, strategies) — OMG standard linking mission/vision to concrete goals and the strategies/tactics that achieve them.
    • Operating model design — Defines how an organization delivers value — covering people, processes, technology, and governance across business units.
    • Business process architecture — Structures and categorizes all business processes into a hierarchy (process landscape) showing relationships and ownership.
    • Organization structure mapping — Documents reporting lines, roles, and accountabilities aligned to capabilities and processes.
    • Strategic alignment frameworks (e.g., Balanced Scorecard) — Tools like BSC translate strategy into measurable objectives across financial, customer, process, and learning dimensions.

    Business Processes

    • Process modeling techniques (BPMN, flowcharts) — BPMN is the standard notation for precise, executable process models; flowcharts are simpler for high-level communication.
    • Process maturity assessment — Evaluates process consistency and capability (e.g., CMMI levels 1–5) to identify improvement opportunities.
    • Process optimization and reengineering — Optimization fine-tunes existing processes; reengineering radically redesigns them from scratch for step-change improvements.
    • End-to-end process design — Designs processes that span multiple departments or systems, ensuring handoffs and touchpoints are seamless.
    • Process governance frameworks — Defines ownership, oversight, and control mechanisms to ensure processes are followed and continuously improved.
    • Business rules management — Captures and centralizes decision logic (eligibility, pricing, compliance) separately from application code for easy maintenance.

    Stakeholder Management

    • Stakeholder analysis and mapping — Identifies all parties affected by or influencing an initiative, assessed by interest and influence to tailor engagement.
    • Requirements gathering techniques — Interviews, workshops, surveys, and observation methods to elicit business and functional needs.
    • Business case development — Structured justification for an initiative covering problem statement, options, costs, benefits, risks, and recommendation.
    • Cost-benefit analysis — Quantifies expected costs against tangible and intangible benefits to support investment decisions.
    • Change management strategies — Plans for transitioning people through organizational change (e.g., Kotter's 8-step, ADKAR model).
    • Communication planning — Defines what information is shared with whom, through which channels, at what frequency.

    Performance & Metrics

    • KPI definition and measurement — Key Performance Indicators are quantifiable measures tied directly to strategic objectives with defined targets and data sources.
    • Business metrics frameworks — Structures for organizing metrics (input, output, outcome, leading, lagging) to give a balanced performance picture.
    • ROI calculation — Return on Investment = (Net Benefit / Cost) × 100; used to compare and justify technology or process investments.
    • Business value realization — Post-implementation tracking to confirm that expected benefits from a project were actually achieved.
    • Performance dashboards — Visual displays of real-time or near-real-time KPIs, enabling management to monitor and act on business performance.

    2. Data Architecture

    Data Strategy & Governance

    • Data governance frameworks — Policies, roles, and processes (e.g., DAMA-DMBOK) ensuring data is managed as a trusted, accountable enterprise asset.
    • Data quality management — Continuous processes to profile, measure, and remediate data against quality dimensions (accuracy, completeness, timeliness).
    • Master data management (MDM) — Creates a single, authoritative source of truth for critical business entities (customer, product, supplier) shared across systems.
    • Metadata management — Captures and maintains data about data — definitions, lineage, ownership, and usage — enabling discoverability and trust.
    • Data stewardship models — Assigns accountability for data domains to business stewards who define standards and resolve quality issues.
    • Data policies and standards — Enterprise rules governing data classification, retention, access, naming conventions, and acceptable use.

    Data Modeling

    • Conceptual data modeling — High-level model showing key business entities and their relationships, independent of technology; used for business alignment.
    • Logical data modeling — Detailed, technology-agnostic model defining entities, attributes, relationships, and constraints; basis for physical design.
    • Physical data modeling — Database-specific model translating the logical model into tables, indexes, partitions, and storage considerations.
    • Dimensional modeling (star schema, snowflake) — Data warehouse design pattern optimizing for analytical queries: fact tables surrounded by dimension tables.
    • Entity-relationship diagrams — Visual representation of entities and their associations, cardinality, and attributes; used for database design.
    • Data normalization techniques — Organizes data to reduce redundancy (1NF–3NF/BCNF), improving integrity at the cost of some query complexity.

    Data Management

    • Data lifecycle management — Manages data from creation through active use, archival, and deletion, balancing cost, compliance, and accessibility.
    • Data cataloging and classification — Inventories data assets with business context and sensitivity labels, making them findable and governable.
    • Data lineage and provenance — Tracks the origin, movement, and transformation of data, essential for impact analysis, audit, and debugging.
    • Reference data management — Controls shared lookup/code tables (country codes, status values) ensuring consistency across systems.
    • Data retention and archival — Defines how long data must be kept (regulatory or business needs) and strategies for moving it to cheaper storage tiers.
    • Data privacy and compliance (GDPR, CCPA) — Regulations requiring consent, data minimization, right-to-erasure, and breach notification; architecture must support PII identification and control.

    Data Integration & Quality

    • ETL/ELT processes — Extract-Transform-Load moves data between systems; ELT loads first and transforms in-place, leveraging modern cloud data warehouse power.
    • Data integration patterns — Replication, federation, virtualization, and event streaming — each with different latency, consistency, and coupling trade-offs.
    • Data migration strategies — Big-bang vs. phased migration approaches with cutover planning, data mapping, validation, and rollback strategies.
    • Data quality dimensions — Standard measures: accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, and validity, each requiring specific monitoring rules.
    • Data profiling techniques — Automated analysis of data sets to discover structure, distributions, patterns, and anomalies before integration or migration.
